Start Here · One-page Executive Summary v3.51

S.1Production Reference and Market Map

This document is a practical field guide for designing, choosing, operating, and governing agentic AI systems. Use it to move from product intent to architecture choices, from architecture choices to implementation stacks, and from implementation stacks to production controls.

Canonical thesisAgentic systems are controlled software systems—not merely LLM applications.

Production quality emerges from the interaction of models, context, loops, tools, state, retrieval, verification, permissions, and operational controls. Start with the simplest inspectable workflow that can satisfy the requirement; increase autonomy only when uncertainty creates measurable value and evaluation evidence justifies the added risk.

Default architectureWorkflow before agent

Prefer deterministic orchestration, typed tool contracts, bounded loops, and explicit recovery paths.

Default knowledgeGround every consequential claim

Use source-aware retrieval, provenance, freshness controls, and abstention when evidence is weak.

Default stateSeparate context, cache, state, and memory

Place each by lifetime, access pattern, durability, query mode, and privacy requirement.

Default controlRisk-gate actions

Keep least privilege, budgets, verification, human approval, audit, and rollback around side effects.

Who should read what
ExecutivesRead this summary, the brief version, production rules, and risk checklist.
Product leadsStart with product patterns, use-case decisions, and anti-patterns.
EngineersUse the stack map, revised architecture, default stacks, state, tools, and deployment sections.
ML / DS teamsFocus on retrieval, evals, data quality, model gateways, and the ML/DS bridge.
Platform / securityPrioritize observability, identity, policy gates, audit trails, rollback, and freshness controls.
Top 10 production rules
  1. Do not add agents where deterministic workflows are enough.
  2. Separate planning, execution, memory, retrieval, and evaluation.
  3. Treat tools as privileged interfaces with permissions and audit logs.
  4. Log decisions, tool calls, inputs, outputs, and failure paths.
  5. Version prompts, schemas, tools, embeddings, evals, and policies.
  6. Keep human approval on irreversible or high-risk actions.
  7. Make retrieval freshness and source provenance first-class controls.
  8. Design rollback, timeout, and graceful-degradation paths before launch.
  9. Measure task success and operational risk, not only model quality.
  10. Prefer small, inspectable loops over broad opaque autonomy.
Continue toChoose the shortest useful path →
Production takeaway — Use this reference as a decision system: begin with the architecture and production defaults, then consult the market map only after requirements are clear.
Continue to Start Here Overview →
Canonical decision journey

S.2Production Decision Spine

Every production design should move through the same sequence. Skipping a stage usually creates hidden operational debt that appears later as cost, security, reliability, or quality failure.

One-page canonical architecture
1Experience & identity

UI, API, tenant, user, session, intent, risk tier.

2Router & policy gate

Classify, authorize, select model/pattern, enforce budgets.

3Harness & control loop

Plan, act, observe, verify, replan, stop, recover.

4Context & knowledge

Prompt, retrieval, memory, state, caches, provenance.

5Tools & execution

Typed contracts, sandboxing, idempotency, side effects.

6Verification & operations

Evals, observability, audit, HITL, rollback, learning.

Escalation rule: single model call → deterministic workflow → bounded agent loop → multi-agent coordination. Move right only when the previous form cannot meet a measured requirement.
01Intent & risk

Define user outcome, reversibility, impact tier, owner, and success metric.

02Complexity test

Choose deterministic workflow first; add autonomy only where uncertainty requires it.

03Architecture & loop

Select orchestration, tools, knowledge, state, budgets, stops, and recovery semantics.

04Verification & controls

Define evals, policy gates, permissions, provenance, observability, and human review.

05Release & operate

Shadow, canary, monitor, respond, roll back, learn, and promote only with evidence.

Release contract: prompts, models, tools, schemas, retrieval indexes, policies, eval datasets, and rollback instructions are one versioned production artifact—not independent changes.
Continue toWhen not to add agentic complexity →
Production takeaway — Start with task determinism, risk, knowledge needs, and action authority; those four questions determine whether an agent is appropriate and how constrained it must be.
Continue to When Not to Use Agents →
// Anti-Patterns

S.3When Not to Use Agents

Use deterministic workflows when autonomy does not create measurable value.

Use this section as a brake. Most production failures come from adding autonomy before the system has observability, evaluation, access control, rollback, and clear user value.

Do not use agents when a workflow is deterministic.Prefer: rules, queues, cron jobs, SQL, or standard workflow orchestration when the task does not require reasoning or ambiguity handling.
Do not add memory before you know what should persist.Prefer: explicit user profiles, scoped preferences, and auditable state before implicit long-term memory.
Do not add multi-agent orchestration to fix unclear requirements.Prefer: one reliable agent with a narrow objective, clear tool contracts, and measurable success criteria.
Do not use RAG as a substitute for content governance.Prefer: curated corpora, ACL-aware indexing, deduplication, freshness metadata, and citation evaluation.
Do not give write tools to unmeasured systems.Prefer: read-only tools, sandbox writes, dry runs, approvals, and rollback plans until evals and monitoring pass.
Do not optimize model choice before measuring task quality.Prefer: a small golden dataset, task-specific evals, latency/cost traces, and side-by-side model regression tests.
Continue toChoose Your Reading Path →
Production takeaway — Prefer deterministic workflows whenever the task can be specified, tested, and operated without open-ended planning or tool selection.
Continue to Choose Your Reading Path →

Continue to For ML & Data Science Practitioners →
ML & Data Science role path

Evaluation, retrieval, memory, data quality, model gateways, and production operations for ML and data science practitioners.

If you know machine learning or data science, you already understand more of this stack than you think. Agentic AI is not a replacement for ML — it's inference-time compute applied to goals rather than tokens. This section maps concepts you know to concepts in this document, so you can navigate the rest of it with confidence.

// Concept mapping: what you know → what this document calls it
Memory
Context window ≈ Working memory / RAM
An LLM's context window is finite (~200k tokens) — everything the model can "see" right now. When it fills, you need external memory (Mem0) or retrieval (RAG). This is why vector stores exist: they extend context beyond what fits in the window.
Retrieval
RAG ≈ Retrieval-augmented inference
RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) is retrieval-augmented inference: instead of embedding all knowledge in model weights, you retrieve relevant documents at inference time and inject them into context. You already know embedding models and nearest-neighbor search — those are the DS-familiar pieces of the RAG stack.
Control loops
ReAct / MCTS / Beam Search ≈ Inference-time search
Control loops are search algorithms run at inference time over goals rather than tokens. ReAct is a greedy search loop. Tree-of-Thought branches and prunes. MCTS (Monte Carlo Tree Search) — which you may know from AlphaGo — is the same algorithm applied to reasoning traces rather than game boards.
Evaluation
Agent eval ≈ Online experiment design
Agent evaluation is closer to online A/B testing of decision policies than offline model benchmarking. Instead of comparing predictions vs. ground truth, you compare trajectories — did the agent take the right sequence of actions? Golden datasets here are (input, expected trajectory) pairs rather than (input, label) pairs.
Observability
MLflow / experiment tracking ≈ Langfuse / LangSmith traces
You know MLflow for experiment tracking. Langfuse and LangSmith are its agentic equivalent — they trace not just model calls but tool invocations, context assembly, retrieval results, and token costs across every step of a run. Same concept, more dimensions.
Features vs. context
Feature store ≈ Context + retrieval layer
In traditional ML, a feature store serves engineered features to a model at serving time. In agentic AI, the retrieval layer plus in-context injection serves the same function — assembling the right facts into context before inference. The "features" are now retrieved passages, structured records, and tool outputs rather than numerical vectors.
// Traditional ML vs. agentic AI — a quick comparison
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
DimensionTraditional ML / DSAgentic AI
Core loopTrain → deploy → predict (batch or online)Prompt → think → act → observe → repeat until done
InferenceSingle forward pass, fixed outputMulti-step, tool-augmented, adaptive
KnowledgeEncoded in model weights at training timeRetrieved from external stores at inference time (RAG)
EvaluationOffline metrics: accuracy, F1, AUC on held-out setTrajectory quality, tool-call accuracy, golden datasets, human review
DeploymentModel artifact + serving infra + feature pipelineModel + harness + tools + memory + retrieval + observability
DriftData drift, concept drift, model degradationPrompt drift, retrieval staleness, tool contract changes, model version changes
Your roleBuild models, tune hyperparameters, feature engineerDesign agents, curate retrieval corpora, build eval sets, tune context
✓ Skills that transfer directly
  • Embedding models (the RAG retrieval foundation)
  • Re-rankers and cross-encoders (retrieval quality)
  • Chunking strategies (context quality for RAG)
  • Precision/recall thinking (retrieval evaluation)
  • Experiment design and A/B testing (agent eval)
  • Feature store patterns (context assembly design)
  • Monitoring and drift detection (observability)
  • Fine-tuning domain models (task-specific performance)
→ New concepts to learn
  • Context window management (working memory limits)
  • Tool schemas and MCP (how agents call external APIs)
  • Control loops (ReAct, Plan–Execute–Reflect)
  • Prompt engineering and system prompts
  • Trajectory evaluation (did the agent choose the right steps?)
  • HITL patterns (human-in-the-loop approval gates)
  • Guardrails and policy enforcement
  • Agent harnesses (Claude Code, LangGraph runtimes)
The key insight: In traditional ML, your job is to build a model that encodes knowledge at training time and produces predictions at serving time. In agentic AI, your job is to build a system that assembles knowledge at inference time, decides what actions to take, and improves from feedback over time. Your embedding models, retrieval skills, and evaluation instincts are load-bearing in the agentic stack — they're not obsolete, they're promoted.
Continue toThe production agent stack: layers, inner loop, and cross-cutting planes →
Production takeaway — Translate familiar ML concepts—evaluation, feature quality, drift, and experimentation—into agent concerns such as context quality, tool behavior, memory, and loop control.
Part I

System Architecture

Understand the production stack, capability model, system map, primitives, and complexity spectrum before choosing patterns or products.
In this part
  • 1.1The Stack
  • 1.2Production Agent Stack
  • 1.3Agentic AI System Map
  • 1.4Agentic Primitives
  • 1.5Agent Complexity Spectrum
  • 1.6Production Engineering Map
TL;DR
  • The stack has 16 layers; every layer exists to feed, constrain, observe, or govern the model layer.
  • A chatbot is an agent with fewer layers active - same stack, lower autonomy.
  • Use the complexity spectrum to pick which layers you need before adding tooling.
  • Control loops (ReAct, Plan-Execute-Reflect, HITL) are execution policies, not architecture layers.
  • Data substrate quality and permissions matter more than model choice.

1.1The Stack

// The Stack
🧠MODEL LAYER
Core frontier: Claude 4 · GPT-5/5.5 · Gemini 2.5/3.xOpen/self-host: Llama 4 · Mistral 3 · DeepSeek · Qwen · Gemma · Granite · NemotronCoding/agentic: Claude · GPT-5 · Devstral/Codestral · Composer 2.5 · Kimi K2/K2.5Fast/cheap: Haiku · GPT mini/nano · Gemini Flash · Mistral Small · PhiRetrieval: Cohere · Voyage · Jina · BGE/E5 · Titan · OpenAI embeddingsAWS-native: Amazon Nova · Titan · Bedrock-hosted Claude/Llama/MistralRouter principle: task → lane → model → eval gateEvery other layer exists to feed, constrain, observe, or govern this layer

// Revised Agent Stack · Architecture Boundaries

1.2Production Agent Stack

Layers, inner loop, and cross-cutting control planes.

Use this as the canonical mental model for placing agent components. The vertical stack describes implementation boundaries; the inner loop describes the agent's behavior; the side planes describe controls that instrument, evaluate, secure, and govern more than one layer at a time.

Layer placement cheat sheet
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
QuestionAnswer / Layer
Who owns the user workflow, permissions, approvals, and audit posture?Product / Control Plane
What starts the run and manages the session or workspace?Agent Harness
What defines agents, prompts, model calls, tools, and structured outputs?Agent Framework
What manages state, retries, checkpoints, graph transitions, and interrupts?Agent Runtime
What centralizes model access, routing, fallback, spend, budgets, and provider abstraction?Model Gateway / AI Gateway — LiteLLM, Portkey, OpenRouter, Kong AI Gateway, Cloudflare AI Gateway
What exposes external capabilities to the agent?Context + Capability Interface — MCP, function tools, OpenAPI tools, resources, prompts
Where do side effects actually happen?Execution Substrate — sandbox, browser, database, filesystem, queue, SaaS API
What watches, scores, secures, and governs the whole path?Cross-cutting planes — observability, evals, security, governance, privacy, cost, policy
Vertical implementation stack
What owns the product surface, agent session, reusable abstractions, durable execution, tool boundary, and actual side effects.
Layer 0
CONTROL
Product / Control Plane
Business workflow, UX, auth, permissions, approvals, audit, quotas, launch gates, admin dashboards, workflow catalog.
Layer 1
HARNESS
Agent Harness
Claude Code, Cursor, Deep Agents, CLI scripts, Slack bots, scheduled jobs, service entrypoints, workspace/session management.
Layer 2
FRAMEWORK
Agent Framework
LangChain, OpenAI Agents SDK, Anthropic SDKs, Semantic Kernel: prompts, tools, agents, model calls, structured outputs.
Layer 3
RUNTIME
Agent Runtime
LangGraph, Temporal, Dagster, Inngest, durable state machines, checkpointing, fan-out/fan-in, retries, interrupts, HITL.
Layer 3.5
MODEL GATEWAY
Model Gateway / AI Gateway
LiteLLM, Portkey, OpenRouter, Kong AI Gateway, Cloudflare AI Gateway: provider abstraction, model routing, retries, fallbacks, virtual keys, budgets, spend tracking, caching, and policy hooks.
Layer 4
INTERFACE
Context + Capability Interface
MCP, function tools, OpenAPI tools, resources, prompts, retrieval connectors, capability discovery, schemas, auth boundary.
Layer 5
EXECUTION
Execution Substrate / Sandboxes / External Systems
Docker, Kubernetes, microVMs, browser sandboxes, Python kernels, databases, filesystems, SaaS APIs, queues, storage.
Cross-cutting planes Observability, evaluation, security, governance, privacy, cost, policy, and incident response do not live in one layer. They instrument or constrain the harness, framework, runtime, tools, sandbox, and product workflow.
Inner behavioral loop
Think → Act → Observe
ReAct, plan-and-execute, reflection, evaluator-optimizer, and tree search run inside the framework/runtime. They call outward through tool interfaces and receive observations from the execution substrate.
Boundary rule LiteLLM-style gateways standardize and govern model access; MCP standardizes tool/resource access. MCP exposes a capability; the sandbox, database, browser, filesystem, or SaaS service performs the side effect. LangSmith observes traces across layers; it is not itself the runtime.
Model path vs. tool path
Model path
Agent Framework / Runtime → LiteLLM or AI Gateway → OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, Bedrock, Azure OpenAI, Groq, Together, vLLM, Ollama, or another inference backend.
Tool path
Agent Framework / Runtime → MCP or tool interface → sandbox, database, browser, filesystem, SaaS API, queue, or storage system.
Read the stack downward, then read controls sideways: product/control plane decides what may run; the harness starts the agent session; the framework provides reusable agent abstractions; the runtime manages state over time; the capability interface exposes tools/resources/prompts; the execution substrate safely performs side effects. Observability, evals, security, and governance wrap the whole path.
Continue toProduction Engineering Map →

1.3Agentic AI System Map

🖥️UI / APP LAYERThe human-facing interface — everything below this is invisible to the end user
Streamlit
Snowflake · Python-first UI
Rapid Python-native UI for data and agent apps · no frontend experience needed · ideal for internal dashboards, agent control panels, quick prototypes · native streaming + chat support
Next.js + React
Vercel · production web stack
Standard for production agent UIs · server components · streaming via Vercel AI SDK · API routes as agent endpoints · used by most serious production chat and copilot interfaces
CopilotKit
React-first embedded copilot framework
Application-layer framework for embedding AI copilots directly into product UIs · sidebars, in-app chat, state-aware actions, approvals, and agentic workflows · ideal when the assistant should live inside an existing React app
UI patterns:Chat UI — conversational, streaming thread  ·  Dashboard UI — trigger runs, monitor progress, inspect traces  ·  Embedded Copilot — assistant inside an existing product surface (CopilotKit)  ·  IDE — dev environment with embedded harness (Cursor · Copilot · Codex)  ·  Headless / API — no UI, triggered by schedule or webhook (Airflow in Orchestrator layer)
Self-hostable UI sub-layer — pre-built interfaces you can run yourself
🌐 OpenWebUI & OpenTerminal  ready-made chat and terminal interfaces · no custom frontend required
OpenWebUI· self-hostable chat UI
Polished browser-based chat interface for LLMs and agents · supports multiple backends · useful when you want a team-facing UI without building one from scratch
OpenTerminal· terminal-native UI
CLI-style interface for developer-facing agent access · good when the natural workflow is terminal-first instead of browser-first
Treat these as UI products, not runtimes: they sit above the agent pipeline and expose a usable interface to humans.
IDE / Dev Environment sub-layer — UI and harness in one
💻 IDE Harnesses & Cloud Agents  AI-native dev environments · straddle UI and Harness layers
Cursor· most adopted
VS Code fork · multi-model · Composer agent mode · .cursorrules config · interactive AI coding · local
GitHub Copilot· enterprise
Microsoft / OpenAI · VS Code extension + JetBrains · Copilot Workspace · enterprise default on GitHub · local
Codex· cloud agent
OpenAI · fully managed cloud coding agent · sandboxed container · async · parallel tasks · opens PRs · no local interface
Claude Code (CLI · local · headless) · Cursor (GUI · local · interactive) · Copilot (extension · local · enterprise) · Codex (cloud · async · parallel — closest to Claude Managed Agents)
API / Gateway sub-layer — sits between UI and the agent pipeline
🔗 Flask & FastAPI  Python API gateway frameworks · HTTP boundary between agent pipeline and consumers
Flask· 2010 · sync-first
Battle-tested Python micro framework · HTTP routing + request handling · enormous installed base · maximum simplicity · best for straightforward integrations and existing codebases
FastAPI· 2018 · recommended default
Native async/await — fits LangGraph + Claude Agent SDK natively · auto OpenAPI docs at /docs · schema validation · first-class StreamingResponse for token-by-token output · use for all new agent API work
Use cases: POST /run agent endpoint · webhook receiver · SSE streaming to browser · subagent microservice · auth middleware
surfaced via ▼
📡GENERATIVE UI / UI PROTOCOLS — AGENT ↔ UI COMMUNICATIONThe last mile · AG-UI streams events · A2UI/MCP-UI generate surfaces · Open-JSON-UI schemas · compose with MCP and A2A
AG-UI Protocol
Anthropic / community · event streaming · agent↔UI
Streams tool calls · state updates · intermediate steps · final output to the frontend in real time · the MCP equivalent for agent↔UI · analogy: HTTP (the transport layer)
A2UI Protocol
Google · Apache 2.0 · v0.9.1 current (v0.8 legacy) · declarative UI generation
Agents generate declarative component trees the client renders natively · Angular · Flutter · React · Lit · secure by design (pre-approved catalog) · uses A2A as transport · MCP-native · analogy: HTML (the content layer)
MCP-UI
MCP ecosystem · UI surfaces through MCP contexts
Lets MCP tools/resources return trusted interactive surfaces · forms · previews · charts · task panes · bridges MCP Protocol and Generative UI
Open-JSON-UI
Open declarative UI pattern · JSON schema
Validated JSON contract for layouts · components · actions · bindings · safe portable rendering without arbitrary code execution
Protocol map:   MCP — agent ↔ tool  ·  A2A — agent ↔ agent  ·  AG-UI / A2UI / MCP-UI / Open-JSON-UI — agent ↔ UI (last mile)  ·  A2UI uses A2A transport · MCP-UI uses MCP contexts · Open-JSON-UI gives renderer-safe schemas
triggers / displays output of ▼
🎯MULTI-AGENT ORCHESTRATORCoordinates specialist subagents · fan-out · synthesis · above the harness layer
Outer Orchestration — DAG / Workflow
Airflow
Apache · enterprise DAG orchestration · your pipeline
Enterprise standard · complex DAG dependencies · battle-tested at scale · SLA tracking · rich ecosystem · triggers LangGraph / Agent SDK runs on cron or event
Agent Teams — Supervisor / Worker
Claude Agent Teams
Anthropic · Claude-native · supervisor / worker
Supervisor routes tasks to role-based specialist agents · built on Agent SDK · MCP tools + hooks + memory inherited · natural escalation from single-agent pipeline · Claude-only
LangGraph Multi-Agent Supervisor
LangChain · LangGraph · model-agnostic · supervisor / worker
LLM supervisor node decides which worker subgraph to invoke next · each worker is a full subgraph with own tools + system prompt · supervisor reads TypedDict state · signals FINISH when done · checkpointable · model-agnostic
Subagent Spawning — Dynamic Fan-out
Claude Subagent Spawning
Anthropic · Agent SDK · Task() · dynamic at runtime
Parent agent spawns N child agents at runtime via Task() · children run concurrently · parent awaits all results then synthesises · number of children decided at runtime not at definition time · Claude-only
LangGraph Send() API
LangChain · fan-out primitive · model-agnostic · dynamic
Dispatches N subgraph instances in parallel at runtime from Send() list · each writes to shared TypedDict state · fan-out count decided at runtime · your DuckDB + OpenSearch pipeline pattern · model-agnostic
Patterns:DAG — Airflow (outer)  ·  Supervisor → workers — Agent Teams · LangGraph Supervisor (pre-defined roles)  ·  Fan-out / fan-in — Task() · Send() (dynamic N at runtime)  ·  Peer-to-peer — A2A Protocol
delegates to ▼
↔️A2A — AGENT-TO-AGENT PROTOCOLHorizontal · cross-ecosystem agent communication · Linux Foundation · open standard
What it solves: cross-vendor agent delegation — Claude Code subagents talking to LangGraph agents, Google Vertex AI agents, or third-party specialist agents without bespoke integrations  ·  Analogy: TCP/IP — peer-to-peer, horizontal, ecosystem-agnostic  ·  Relevant when: enterprise multi-team interop · Vertex AI ↔ Claude handoffs · open agent marketplace
enforced within ▼
🛡️SECURITY, GOVERNANCE & TRUSTPolicy envelope around the harness · five concerns · fires in sequence
① Entitlements
Is this user allowed to ask this?
→
② RBAC
Can this role invoke this agent / tool?
→
③ Policies
Is this action within agent's allowed behaviour?
→ agent executes →
④ Guardrails
Is this output safe to return?
→
⑤ Filtering
Is this data the user can see?
Guardrails AI
Input/output content safety · validators-as-code
Schema enforcement · PII detection · off-topic blocking · hallucination detection · prompt injection prevention · runs at hook points (PreToolUse · PostToolUse · Stop)
Bedrock Guardrails
AWS · managed · zero infrastructure · your stack
Topic denial · content filters · PII detection + redaction · grounding checks (hallucination) · word blocklists · zero infra · attaches to any Claude on Bedrock call · IAM-native · complements Guardrails AI for custom validation
OPA — Open Policy Agent
CNCF · policy-as-code · Rego · RBAC / ABAC / entitlements
Harness asks OPA "can this role invoke this MCP tool on this resource?" → OPA returns allow/deny with reason · policies live in repo not application code · already standard in K8s · API gateways · now applied to agent stacks
☁️ AWS-Managed Alternative — AgentCore Identity + Policy
AgentCore Identity
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
Agent identity, access and authentication · compatible with Cognito · Okta · Azure Entra ID · Auth0 · any IdP · no user migration · replaces bespoke auth wiring for AWS-native stacks
AgentCore Policy
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
Natural language → Cedar policies automatically · intercepts every tool call via AgentCore Gateway before execution · deterministic control · no reasoning loop slowdown · AWS-native alternative to OPA
wraps ▼
🛠️AGENT HARNESS + RUNTIMEWhere sessions start, framework abstractions bind tools, and runtime state executes over time
Anthropic · Claude-only
Claude Code ★

Filesystem · bash · MCP tools · hooks · skills · CLAUDE.md · subagent delegation · headless mode

Programmatic runtime
Python library you compose · same primitives as Claude Code · Claude-only
LangChain · Orchestration-first
Deep Agents
Execution-first harness
Parallel to Claude Code · prompt + plan + filesystem + subagents · model-agnostic · HITL interrupts
LangGraph

☁️ AWS-Managed Tool Sandboxes — AgentCore Code Interpreter + Browser
AgentCore Code Interpreter
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
Isolated managed sandbox for agent code execution · Python · JavaScript · TypeScript · zero infrastructure · no container setup · AWS-native alternative to E2B / Modal
AgentCore Browser
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
Managed cloud browser runtime · agents navigate sites, fill forms, extract data · Playwright + BrowserUse compatible · zero infrastructure · AWS-native alternative to self-hosted browser automation
Same layer, different roles: Execution-first harnesses — Claude Code · Deep Agents  ·  Runtimes — Claude Agent SDK · LangGraph
🌿 Git Worktrees — harness-level parallelism primitive
Not infrastructure — a harness primitive. Spin up N worktrees → run N Claude Code instances in parallel, each isolated in its own working directory, all sharing .git history. Near-zero overhead vs Docker containers.  ·  git worktree add ../feature-a · git worktree add ../feature-b  ·  vs Docker: worktrees isolate filesystem only · Docker isolates full environment
Agentic primitives — the internal anatomy of Claude Code & Agent SDK
Internal Primitives  the vocabulary you use to program agent behavior
🤖 Subagents
Delegate tasks to fresh Claude instances with own context windows · fan-out primitive · reaches into Orchestrator layer
⚡ Hooks
Event listeners on agent lifecycle · pre-tool · post-tool · on-error · injection point for Langfuse tracing · reaches into Observability layer
📦 Skills
Reusable capability bundles · prompt + tools + instructions packaged · can bundle RAG retrieval · reaches into Knowledge layer
/ Slash Commands
Custom invocable actions in .claude/commands/ · CLI macros · invocation primitive · user-defined agent workflows
📄 CLAUDE.md
Harness system prompt · persistent context · rules · persona · tool guidance · acts as hand-authored long-term memory · reaches into Memory layer
🔌 MCP Tools
External tool connections via Model Context Protocol · DuckDB · Power BI · GitHub · Browserbase · reaches into Tool & Integration layer
calls tools via ▼
↕️CONTEXT + CAPABILITY INTERFACE — MCPVertical capability boundary · tools/resources/prompts · schemas · auth boundary
What it solves: standardized capability exposure — tools, resources, prompts, schemas, and auth boundary  ·  Analogy: USB-C — vertical, device-to-peripheral, plug-and-play  ·  Complements A2A: MCP is vertical (agent→tool) · A2A is horizontal (agent→agent)
☁️ AWS-Managed MCP Server Factory — AgentCore Gateway
AgentCore Gateway
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
Converts any API, Lambda function, or existing service into an MCP-compatible tool in a few lines of code · connects to pre-existing MCP servers · built-in integrations: Salesforce · Zoom · JIRA · Slack · intercepts tool calls for Policy enforcement · AWS-native alternative to writing your own MCP server wrappers
APIs & CLIs → MCP — the raw substrate MCP wraps
API / CLI → MCP wrapping pattern
REST API → MCP: OpenSearch · GitHub · Power BI · Slack · Notion
CLI → MCP:git · docker · kubectl · aws · gh · terraform
APIs and CLIs are the same category — raw interfaces. MCP is the typed, discoverable protocol layer on top.
Three ways to call an API or CLI from an agent
1. Direct — requests.get() / subprocess.run() — raw, untyped, hard to observe
2. Wrapped as MCP server — typed · schema-validated · observable · recommended
3. Claude Code bash/fetch tool — direct access within controlled harness
reads from / writes to ▼
🧠MEMORY LAYERWhat the agent remembers across sessions
Mem0
Mem0 AI · embeddable middleware
Lightweight · extracts + stores memories via API · drops into any existing harness · low overhead · self-hostable · best for adding memory to existing pipelines
// ML/DS insight · continual learning & catastrophic forgettingCross-session memory is continual learning without weight updates — and faces the same core problem: storing new information without overwriting or contradicting prior facts. This is the catastrophic forgetting problem from continual learning research. When a user's preference changes ("I used to prefer formal tone, now I want casual"), naive memory systems either ignore the update (forgetting failure) or overwrite the prior state (interference failure). Mem0 addresses this at the extracted-fact level — deduplicate, reconcile contradictions, merge summaries — rather than updating model weights. This sidesteps gradient-based forgetting but introduces a new problem: contradiction resolution is still hard. Design memory schemas with explicit TTLs for volatile preferences vs. stable facts, and build correction mechanisms so users can delete or amend stored memories.
☁️ AWS-Managed Alternative — AgentCore Memory
Short-term
AgentCore Memory · in-session
Turn-by-turn context within a session · agents maintain immediate context without users repeating themselves
Long-term
AgentCore Memory · cross-session
Auto-extracts user preferences, facts, and session summaries · persists across sessions · replaces Mem0 for AWS-native stacks
Episodic
AgentCore Memory · learning
Captures context + reasoning + actions + outcomes · reflection agent analyzes patterns · agents improve from past experiences
AgentCore Memory replaces custom memory infrastructure for AWS-native stacks · zero infrastructure · three managed tiers · works with LangGraph, LangChain, Strands
queries for context ▼
📄DOCUMENT PROCESSING & INGESTIONRaw unstructured content in → clean embeddable text out · runs before the agent, not during
When it runs: batch / scheduled (Airflow · S3 event) — not real-time. Documents are processed and indexed ahead of time, then retrieved at agent query time via RAG.
AWS Textract
AWS · OCR + document understanding
Beyond basic OCR · understands tables · forms · key-value pairs · checkboxes · multi-column layouts · handwriting · outputs structured JSON ready for chunking and embedding · ideal for financial reports and regulatory filings
AWS Bedrock Data Automation
AWS · managed multimodal pipeline
End-to-end multimodal ingestion: Textract + classification + extraction + enrichment in one managed service · handles PDFs · images · audio · video · outputs to S3 · less control than raw Textract · far less glue code
Pipeline:  Raw docs → Textract / Bedrock Data Automation (extract + structure) → Chunking + Embedding → OpenSearch / pgvector (index) → RAG at agent query time
indexes into ▼
🗄️KNOWLEDGE & RETRIEVALDomain knowledge fed into agent context at query time
Embeddings — the foundation. Text → vectors via embedding model (OpenAI text-embedding-3, Cohere, HuggingFace local). Everything in this layer depends on them.
Vector DBs — retrieval substrate
Vector + Search
OpenSearch ★ · pgvector
Hybrid lexical + semantic retrieval · BM25 + vector + metadata filters · best when keyword precision and vector recall both matter · OpenSearch is your primary stack; pgvector keeps retrieval inside PostgreSQL
Vector (Pure-Play)
Pinecone · Weaviate · Chroma
Dedicated embedding stores · Pinecone for managed scale · Weaviate for open-source object-aware retrieval · Chroma for local-first prototypes and lightweight RAG apps
Vector (S3-Native)
Amazon S3 Vectors
Store and query embedding vectors directly in S3 · lowest-cost AWS-native option for high-volume vector storage · fits document-heavy RAG pipelines
Graph Traversal — relationship traversal over structured data
Amazon Neptune
AWS · managed graph DB
Property Graph: Gremlin / openCypher · RDF: SPARQL · Neptune Analytics adds vector search on top of graph traversal · native GraphRAG on AWS
RAG: query → embed → vector search → retrieve chunks → generate

GraphRAG: build knowledge graph from docs → graph hops + vector search → multi-hop reasoning
iterates via ▼
🔁INNER LOOP · LOOP PATTERNS & ITERATION CONTROLBehavioral control patterns that run inside the framework/runtime
Primitive + foundational execution
Ralph Loop
while → run → stop
Mechanical keep-going substrate · terminal-first · every named loop specifies what happens inside the body before completion
ReAct
Reason → Act → Observe
Foundational inner tool-calling loop · calls tools or subagents · observes result · repeats until done
Plan–Execute–Reflect
Outer structured agent loop
Plan full approach → execute stepwise → reflect on quality/failures → revise · best for long multi-step tasks
HITL + Confidence Gates
Interrupt when uncertain or policy-gated
Agent pauses for human approval/correction or routes to verification/retry based on risk and confidence
Test-Driven Loop
Observe = test result
Write → test → observe failures → revise · strongest coding feedback loop because pass/fail is explicit
Reflexion
Attempt → lesson → retry
Stores reusable self-feedback as future experience · distinct from one-off critique
Search and quality control
Search / Tree-of-Thought
Branch → score → prune
Explores multiple reasoning/action paths instead of one linear trajectory · useful for hard reasoning and planning alternatives
Best-of-N / Evolutionary
Generate candidates → evaluate → select
Improves output through parallel diversity, ranking, mutation, and selection · useful for prompt/design optimization
Critique / Debate
Draft → challenge → revise / judge
Separates producer, critic, opponent, and judge roles · surfaces hidden assumptions and improves artifact quality
Consensus / Verifier
Compare outputs · enforce constraints
Multiple agents/models reconcile disagreements · hard checks validate schemas, tests, citations, permissions, and policies
Knowledge, capability, and coordination loops
Retrieval-Grounded Research
Query → retrieve → synthesize → gap-check
Loop continues until evidence is sufficient · core RAG/deep-research pattern
Memory + Tool Discovery
Act → remember · Need → discover tool
Updates future behavior through persistent memory · acquires missing capabilities through APIs/MCP/tool catalogs
Multi-Agent Coordination
Supervisor · Contract-Net · Blackboard · Swarm
Distributes work across specialists by routing, bidding, shared workspace, or decentralized local actions
Production Control
DAG · State Machine · Event-Driven · Monitor–Act
External orchestration, explicit transitions, event triggers, and continuous environment feedback for production agents
Default composition: DAG / Workflow → Plan–Execute–Reflect → ReAct → Verifier / HITL / Memory hooks. Add Search when the solution space is broad, Critique/Consensus when quality risk is high, Retrieval when evidence is missing, and Multi-Agent coordination when specialist parallelism is needed.
persists state via ▼
💾AGENT STATE & PERSISTENCELow-latency state, SQL checkpoints, time-series telemetry, and warehouse-backed context
Low-latency KV State Stores
Redis
Redis Ltd · in-memory · ephemeral
Fast in-memory store for agent working state · pub/sub for inter-agent messaging · shared scratchpad during fan-out · LLM response caching · rate limiting
Amazon DynamoDB
AWS · serverless KV · zero infrastructure · pay-per-request
Serverless durable KV — where Redis is ephemeral and in-memory, DynamoDB is durable and serverless · session state between runs · agent config store · rate limiting · atomic counters · pay-per-request cheaper than always-on Redis for bursty workloads · IAM-native
Relational + Analytical SQL Databases
PostgreSQL
Durable checkpoint backend
LangGraph's production checkpointer backend · stores graph state snapshots · run history · audit logs · pairs with pgvector for single-DB retrieval + persistence
Amazon Redshift
AWS · analytical SQL warehouse
Warehouse-backed database source for agents · governed enterprise facts · historical metrics · audit/report tables · OLAP queries through tools · separate from Redis/DynamoDB hot state stores
LangGraph Checkpointer
LangChain · built-in
Snapshots full graph state at each node · resume interrupted runs from last checkpoint · backends: in-memory (dev) · PostgreSQL (prod) · SQLite (local)
Time-Series DBs — telemetry, high-frequency events, and operational metrics over time
Temporal persistence: use time-series stores when the agent system needs to query behavior over time — latency curves, event bursts, cost trends, traces, simulations, market-style streams, and operational anomaly windows.
TimescaleDB
Timescale · PostgreSQL-native time-series
SQL-native time-series analytics · joins metrics with relational run metadata · continuous aggregates · retention policies · good when Postgres is already the system of record
kdb+ / KX
KX · high-performance time-series analytics
High-frequency timestamped facts · market data · streaming events · agent trace analytics · latency windows · anomaly detection · best when event volume and temporal query speed dominate
Document DB — variable-schema agent artifacts
MongoDB
MongoDB Inc. · flexible schema · Atlas vector search
Default document DB · stores variable-shape agent run outputs · generated reports · cached tool results · Atlas Vector Search adds retrieval in same store · supported as LangGraph checkpointer backend
Amazon DocumentDB
AWS · managed · MongoDB-compatible
AWS-managed with full MongoDB API compatibility · zero cluster management · integrates with IAM · Secrets Manager · CloudWatch · natural choice for AWS-native stacks
observed by ▼
📊OBSERVABILITY & EVALUATION PLANECross-cutting plane — traces model, runtime, tool, retrieval, sandbox, and product events
LLM-Native Tracing — what the model did
Langfuse
Langfuse GmbH · open source
Framework-agnostic tracing · evals · prompt versioning · cost tracking · self-hostable · best for multi-framework pipelines
LangSmith
LangChain · SaaS-only
LangGraph-native · full graph viz of node-by-node execution · evals · datasets · human annotation · LangGraph Platform default
Ops / Infrastructure — what the system did
Splunk
Enterprise log aggregation
Ingests everything: server logs · agent stdout · pipeline errors · security events · infrastructure metrics · the ops observability layer alongside LLM-native tracing
Grafana
Grafana Labs · metrics & dashboards
Metrics visualization + operational dashboards · latency · throughput · error rates · cost per run · connects to Prometheus · CloudWatch · Datadog · SLA monitoring
Experiment Tracking — what you tried and learned
MLflow
Databricks · open source MLOps
Tracks prompt experiments + eval runs · manages fine-tuned model versions · model registry · most relevant when doing model fine-tuning alongside agent development · integrates with Databricks · AWS SageMaker · Azure ML
☁️ AWS-Managed Alternative — AgentCore Observability
AgentCore Observability
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
Unified trace, debug, and monitor view · step-by-step execution visualization of each agent workflow step · inspect execution paths · audit intermediate outputs · debug bottlenecks and failures · OTEL-compatible · integrates with any monitoring stack · results in CloudWatch · covers LLM-native tracing + ops in one managed service
Langfuse / LangSmith — what the model did  ·  Splunk / Grafana — what the system did  ·  MLflow — what you tried and learned  ·  AgentCore Observability — all three concerns in one managed CloudWatch-backed service for AWS-native stacks
validated by ▼
🧪EVALUATION & TESTINGPre-production validation · golden datasets · regression suites · trajectory evals
PromptFoo
Open source · prompt & agent output testing
YAML-defined test cases · LLM-as-judge assertions · schema validation · CI integration via GitHub Actions · blocks merge if scores drop · the test-driven agent loop's testing framework
DeepEval
Confident AI · RAG & agent eval framework
Correctness · faithfulness · hallucination detection · contextual relevance · answer relevance · strong for RAG pipeline validation · are retrieved docs faithfully represented in output?
RAGAS
Open source · RAG and LLM application evaluation
Component-level metrics for retrieval and generation · context precision and recall · faithfulness · response relevancy · noise sensitivity · tool-call accuracy · agent-goal accuracy · best for dataset-based RAG benchmarking and experiment comparison
AgentEvals
Anthropic · trajectory-level evaluation
Evaluates decision quality not just output quality · did the agent choose the right tool in the right order? · golden trajectory datasets · trajectory comparison metrics · HITL annotation workflows · complements PromptFoo (output) with sequence-level eval
Eval loop:   PromptFoo defines test suites and CI gates → RAGAS benchmarks retrieval and answer quality → DeepEval runs application-level regression tests → AgentEvals evaluates tool use and trajectories → Langfuse or LangSmith tracks production results and failed examples  ·  Golden datasets — curated (input, expected output) pairs that catch regressions when prompts, models, or retrieval strategies change  · 
☁️ AWS-Managed Alternative — AgentCore Evaluations
AgentCore Evaluations
AWS · AgentCore · managed alternative for AWS-native stacks
13 built-in evaluators: correctness · faithfulness · safety · tool selection accuracy · edge case handling · output reliability · custom scoring · evaluates sessions, traces, and spans from LangGraph or Strands · instrumented via OpenTelemetry or OpenInference · all results in CloudWatch alongside AgentCore Observability · AWS-native alternative to PromptFoo + DeepEval
AgentCore Evaluations — if AWS-native, replaces PromptFoo + DeepEval with a managed eval service · use AgentEvals (Anthropic) alongside for trajectory-level evaluation
runs on ▼
⚙️EXECUTION SUBSTRATE / INFRASTRUCTURESandboxes · containers · orchestration · databases · SaaS systems where side effects occur
Sandbox boundary: MCP or function calling exposes the capability; the execution substrate performs the side effect under isolation, resource limits, network policy, secrets controls, and artifact cleanup.
Container Infrastructure — the execution substrate
Docker
Container runtime · foundational primitive
Packages agent pipelines into portable containers · isolates Claude Code headless runs and subagent environments · foundation everything else runs on
Kubernetes
CNCF · container orchestration
Runs thousands of containers · schedules LangGraph workers · FastAPI endpoints · Airflow schedulers · self-healing · auto-scaling · the production fleet manager
EKS
AWS · managed Kubernetes
AWS manages the K8s control plane · natural runtime for AWS-native stacks (Textract · Neptune · S3 Vectors · Bedrock · OpenSearch) · you write K8s · AWS runs it
Temporal
Temporal Technologies · durable execution
Durable execution under LangGraph · crash-safe long-running workflows · retries · timeouts · exactly-once · pipeline completes even if a node dies mid-run
Agent-Specific Runtime — managed agent infra
Amazon Bedrock AgentCore — 9 modular services
AWS · fully managed · any framework · any model · 2025
⚙️ Runtime
Serverless · session isolation · async · streaming · MCP + A2A → Infrastructure

Short-term · long-term · episodic → Memory layer

Cognito · Okta · Azure Entra · Auth0 → Security

Isolated sandbox · Python · JS · TS → Harness tools

AWS production path:DIY: Docker → EKS → LangGraph + FastAPI + Airflow containerised  ·  Managed: AgentCore — 9 services covering Runtime · Memory · Gateway · Identity · Code · Browser · Observability · Evals · Policy
CI / CD — the automated feedback loop
GitHub Actions
GitHub / Microsoft · CI/CD automation
Agent-triggered CI: agent writes code → opens PR → Actions runs tests → results feed back to agent → agent iterates · the feedback loop that makes autonomous coding agents useful in production
Agent-as-Action:claude --headless runs inside a workflow step triggered by PR comment or label — the agent is the CI · pairs with Codex to close the full autonomous development loop
powered by ▼
🧠MODEL LAYER — the layer every other layer servesProduction model-selection architecture — choose by role, not brand
Model selection principle: Treat the model layer as a routing architecture. Use different model families for frontier reasoning, open/self-hosted deployments, coding-agent workflows, fast/cheap traffic, retrieval infrastructure, and AWS-native production stacks.
Core frontier — highest-capability generalist and reasoning models
Claude 4 family
Opus · Sonnet · Haiku
Use Opus/Sonnet for complex reasoning, coding, orchestration, long-form synthesis, and high-value agent nodes; use Haiku for fast subagent traffic.
GPT-5 / GPT-5.5 family
OpenAI · frontier / mini / nano tiers
General-purpose reasoning, coding, multimodal workflows, tool use, and cost-tiered production routing when the OpenAI ecosystem is preferred.
Gemini 2.5 / 3.x
Google · Pro / Flash / Live tiers
Strong fit for long-context, multimodal, real-time conversational, Google-native, and A2A-aligned agent workflows.
Open / self-host — data sovereignty, private deployment, and cost control
Llama 4
Meta · open-weight
Self-hosted and air-gapped deployments, private inference, open model governance, and cost control at scale.
Mistral 3 family
Mistral · Large / Medium / Small
Enterprise, EU/sovereign AI, multilingual, and self-hostable or commercially hosted stacks.
DeepSeek
Reasoning / coding / cost-performance
Cost-sensitive reasoning and coding workloads; useful as an open or API-accessible alternative in multi-model routing.
Qwen
Open / multilingual / coder variants
Multilingual agents, coding subagents, open alternatives, and China/APAC-relevant model stacks.
Gemma · Granite · Nemotron
Google · IBM · NVIDIA
Smaller/open enterprise model options for local, governed, synthetic-data, or NVIDIA-stack deployments.
Kimi K2 / K2.5
Moonshot AI · MoE · long-context
Agentic coding, tool use, long-context reasoning, and cost-efficient autonomous workflows; belongs near DeepSeek, Qwen, Llama, and Mistral.
Coding / agentic — models specialized for building, debugging, and IDE workflows
Claude Sonnet / Opus
Coding + agentic reasoning
Strong default for code generation, refactoring, repository reasoning, subagent orchestration, and tool-use loops.
GPT-5
Coding · reasoning · tool use
General coding-agent and professional-work model option when OpenAI tooling, APIs, or Codex-style workflows are preferred.
Composer 2.5
Cursor · IDE-native
Cursor-native coding-agent model for sustained IDE workflows, multi-file implementation, and long-running engineering tasks.
Devstral / Codestral
Mistral · code-specialized
Software-engineering and code-generation workloads, especially in Mistral/EU/self-hosted coding stacks.
DeepSeek · Qwen Coder
Cost-efficient coding models
Useful for coding subagents, parallel implementation tasks, and cheaper code-generation lanes.
Gemini Pro / Flash
Google · coding + long context
Repository-scale context, multimodal debugging, Google-native workflows, and low-latency coding-agent paths.
Fast / cheap — high-volume routing, classification, and lightweight subagents
Claude Haiku
Fast Claude tier
Routing, filtering, extraction, classification, moderation pre-checks, and high-volume subagent work.
GPT mini / nano
OpenAI cost tiers
Cost-sensitive traffic, simple tool selection, structured extraction, and background evaluation tasks.
Gemini Flash / Flash-Lite
Google speed tiers
Low-latency responses, large-context price-performance, and real-time interactive agent experiences.
Mistral Small
Lightweight Mistral tier
Lower-cost multilingual, classification, extraction, and simple agent tasks.
Phi
Microsoft · small model family
Edge, local, narrow-task, and low-cost extraction/classification use cases.
Small open models
Gemma · Qwen small · distilled variants
Private lightweight agents, on-device assistants, and fallback lanes where frontier capability is unnecessary.
Retrieval models — embeddings, rerankers, and search quality infrastructure
Cohere Embed / Rerank
Enterprise RAG
Strong for enterprise search, reranking, multilingual retrieval, and managed RAG pipelines.
Voyage AI
Embedding + reranking
High-quality retrieval models for domain search, legal/finance-style RAG, and reranked context assembly.
Jina · BGE / E5
Open retrieval models
Self-hostable embeddings and rerankers for private, multilingual, or open-source retrieval stacks.
OpenAI embeddings
OpenAI retrieval lane
Default fit for OpenAI-native apps, semantic search, and general-purpose embedding pipelines.
Amazon Titan Embeddings
AWS-native retrieval
Bedrock-native embedding path for AWS governance, IAM, logging, and managed infrastructure alignment.
Google text embeddings
Google / Vertex AI
Retrieval option for Google-native agent and data stacks.
AWS-native — Bedrock-first model and retrieval path
Amazon Nova
AWS-native generation
Bedrock-first model path for teams standardizing on AWS governance, IAM, monitoring, and procurement.
Titan Embeddings
AWS-native retrieval
Embedding layer for AWS-native RAG with OpenSearch, S3, Bedrock, and AgentCore-aligned stacks.
Bedrock-hosted Claude / Llama / Mistral
Managed multi-model access
Enterprise model access through Bedrock controls, IAM, centralized billing, observability, and region-aware governance.
Production routing pattern: Core frontier models handle orchestration and high-value reasoning · fast/cheap models handle routing, extraction, classification, and background workers · open/self-hosted models handle sovereignty or cost constraints · coding-specialized models handle IDE/repo workflows · retrieval models power the RAG layer · AWS-native models simplify governed Bedrock deployments.

1.4Agentic Primitives

Stable
The internal anatomy of Claude Code & Agent SDK — not a separate layer, but the vocabulary that programs agent behavior from within Layer 2.
🤖
Subagents
Delegation primitive
reaches into
Orchestrator layer
Delegates a task to a fresh Claude instance with its own context window. The core fan-out primitive — each subagent is isolated, parallel, and can itself spawn further subagents. Configured via Task() in the Agent SDK or /agent invocation in Claude Code. Enables hierarchical orchestration: orchestrator → specialists → sub-specialists.
parallel executionisolated contexthierarchical
⚡
Hooks
Event primitive
reaches into
Observability layer
Event listeners that fire at agent lifecycle moments: PreToolUse, PostToolUse, Stop, Notification. Defined in .claude/hooks/. Ideal injection point for Langfuse tracing, custom logging, guardrails, and cost tracking. Can block, modify, or redirect tool calls before execution.
lifecycle eventsmiddlewareinterceptors
📦
Skills
Capability primitive
reaches into
Knowledge layer
Reusable capability bundles — prompt + tools + instructions packaged together and stored in .claude/skills/ or /mnt/skills/. Loaded on demand. Can wrap an entire RAG retrieval flow, a code generation pattern, or a document formatting pipeline. Composable: a skill can invoke other skills.
reusablecomposablebundled context
⌨️
Slash Commands
Invocation primitive
reaches into
Loop layer
Custom invocable actions defined as markdown files in .claude/commands/. Typed as /command-name at runtime. Act as user-defined macros — encapsulate multi-step workflows into a single invocation. Can accept arguments, reference project files, and chain into subagents.
macrosworkflow triggers.claude/commands/
📄
CLAUDE.md
Context primitive
reaches into
Memory layer
The harness system prompt — persistent context, rules, persona, tool guidance, and project conventions injected into every agent session. Hierarchical: project-level, directory-level, user-level. Acts as hand-authored long-term memory. The single most impactful file for shaping agent behavior.
system promptpersistent contexthierarchical
🔌
MCP Tools
Integration primitive
reaches into
Knowledge layer
External tool connections via Model Context Protocol — Anthropic's open standard for agent tooling. Configured in .claude/mcp.json. Each MCP server exposes typed tools the agent can call: DuckDB SQL, Power BI semantic queries, GitHub ops, browser control. The USB-C of agent integrations.
open standardtyped tools.claude/mcp.json
🧠
In-Context Memory
State primitive
reaches into
State & Persistence layer
The active context window — everything the agent can "see" right now. Finite and precious (~200k tokens). Managed by the harness: CLAUDE.md loaded first, then tool results, conversation history, retrieved chunks, and subagent outputs. When it fills, the harness must decide what to evict — this is where external memory and retrieval become necessary. Distinct from persistent memory (cross-session) and durable state (Redis/Postgres).
context windowephemeral~200k tokenssingle run
// Agentic primitives — which layer each one reaches into
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
PrimitiveConfig locationRoleReaches into layer
SubagentsTask() / /agentDelegate tasks to fresh Claude instancesOrchestrator
Hooks.claude/hooks/Lifecycle event listeners: pre/post tool, stopObservability
Skills.claude/skills/ · /mnt/skills/Reusable capability bundlesKnowledge
Slash Commands.claude/commands/Custom invocable macros and workflowsLoop Patterns & Iteration
CLAUDE.mdProject / dir / user levelPersistent system prompt and rulesMemory
MCP Tools.claude/mcp.jsonExternal tool connections via open standardKnowledge + Integration
In-Context MemoryActive context windowEverything the agent can see right now (~200k tokens)State & Persistence
// Concept explainer · For ML/DS readersWhy do control loops exist? A language model, on its own, produces one response per prompt — a single forward pass. A control loop wraps that model in a repeat until done structure: the model thinks, calls a tool, observes the result, and decides whether to continue. ReAct is the simplest version: Reason → Act → Observe, repeated. This is why agents can do multi-step work that a single inference call cannot. Think of it as replacing a single model call with an iterative search over actions — closer to dynamic programming than to standard batch inference.

1.5Agent Complexity Spectrum

Stable
A chatbot is a degenerate case of an agent — same stack, fewer layers active. Every layer you add moves it along this spectrum.
Chatbot
turn-based · reactive
Assistant
tools · memory · RAG
Agent
plans · loops · subagents
Multi-Agent
orchestration · fan-out
Autonomous
self-directed · persistent
Which stack layers activate at each level
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
Stack LayerChatbotAssistantAgentMulti-AgentAutonomous
UI / App Layer✓✓✓✓⚠️ headless
Protocols (MCP · A2A)⚠️ MCP✓ MCP✓✓✓
Security & Governance⚠️⚠️✓✓✓
Orchestrator——⚠️✓✓
Harness / Agent Runtime✓✓✓✓✓
Agentic PrimitivesCLAUDE.md onlypartial✓✓✓
Memory Layer—⚠️✓✓✓
Doc Processing & Ingestion—⚠️✓✓✓
Knowledge & Retrieval—✓✓✓✓
Loop Patterns & Iteration——✓✓✓
State & Persistence✓✓✓✓✓
Observability⚠️✓✓✓✓
CI / CD——⚠️✓✓
Infrastructure——⚠️✓✓
Eval & Testing⚠️✓✓✓✓
Model Layer✓✓✓✓✓
Scroll horizontally to compare →
✓  Active⚠️  Situational—  Not needed
Production engineering · navigational lens

1.6Production Engineering Map

Use this map to jump from an operating concern to the document's canonical guidance. It connects reliability, security, data quality, evaluation, deployment, and incident response without repeating those sections.

Reliability, SLOs & graceful degradation

Define availability, latency, task-success, dependency health, and fallback behavior.

Open SLO / SLA examples →
Loop safety & failure recovery

Bound autonomy with budgets, stopping conditions, checkpoints, replay, and escalation.

Open production loop engineering →
State durability & persistence

Separate ephemeral working state, durable checkpoints, memory, and system-of-record data.

Open state and persistence →
Security, approvals & authorization

Apply identity, least privilege, risk gates, sandboxing, and human approval to actions.

Open security threat model →
Evaluation & release confidence

Combine offline datasets, component metrics, trajectory review, regression gates, and live monitoring.

Open evaluation engineering →
Cost, capacity & performance

Control token, retrieval, embedding, tool, queue, and infrastructure spend against latency targets.

Open cost architecture →
Deployment, canaries & rollback

Version the complete agent release artifact and promote it through shadow, canary, and staged rollout.

Open deployment pipeline →
Knowledge & retrieval quality

Operate ingestion, provenance, freshness, deletion, retrieval, reranking, graph expansion, and groundedness.

Open knowledge engineering →
Incidents, ownership & governance

Clarify service ownership, escalation, evidence capture, containment, recovery, and postmortems.

Open incident response templates →
Continue toControl Loop Taxonomy →
Production takeaway — Treat production engineering as a connected control system: architecture, evaluation, security, cost, observability, and operations must be designed together.
Part II

Agent Behavior & Control

Understand how agents iterate, replan, coordinate, compose patterns, and stop safely.
In this part
  • 2.1Control Loop Taxonomy
  • 2.2Loop Engineering
  • 2.3Workflow Patterns
// Control Loop Taxonomy · v3.13

2.1Control Loop Taxonomy

Control loops determine how an agent moves from uncertainty to action. Treat them as execution policies: use simple linear loops by default, add replanning when the environment changes, add consensus when correctness matters, and reserve search-heavy loops for hard planning problems where extra latency and cost are justified.

Linear execution loopsThink–Act–Observe, ReAct, Replan, and Plan–Execute–Reflect. Best for tool use, workflow execution, debugging, and most production agents.
Search loopsBeam Search, Tree-of-Thought, MCTS, and LATS. Best when the agent must compare multiple candidate plans or reasoning paths before acting.
Consensus loopsSelf-consistency, majority voting, debate, and multi-agent review. Best when reliability is more important than speed or token cost.
Default production pathThink → Act → Observe → Replan if needed
→
Hard problem pathBranch → Score → Prune → Select
→
High-confidence pathSample → Vote / Debate → Verify
Control Loop Families │ ├── Linear execution loops │ ├── Think–Act–Observe / ReAct │ ├── Replan Loop │ └── Plan–Execute–Reflect │ ├── Search loops │ ├── Beam Search │ ├── Tree-of-Thought │ └── MCTS / LATS │ └── Consensus loops ├── Self-consistency ├── Majority voting └── Debate / multi-agent review
Production rule of thumb: start with Think–Act–Observe plus explicit stop conditions. Add a Replan Loop for changing environments, consensus for high-impact decisions, and search loops only when a single trajectory repeatedly gets stuck or misses better plans.
Continue toLoop Engineering →
Production takeaway — Choose the smallest loop that can reliably complete the task, and define explicit budgets, stop conditions, and escalation paths before adding autonomy.
Continue to Loop Engineering →
// Loop Engineering · v3.36

2.2Loop Engineering

Canonical treatmentControl behavior, convergence, budgets, retries, checkpoints, and stop conditions.

The Control Loop Taxonomy above answers which loop pattern fits a problem. Loop Engineering answers the operational question underneath all of them: once a loop is running, how do you bound it, observe it, and know when it should stop? An unbounded loop is not autonomy — it is an unmonitored cost center with a tool-use API key.

Stop-condition designEvery loop needs a falsifiable exit test, not a vibe. Define task-complete, budget-exhausted, and no-progress conditions before the first run, not after a runaway.
Budget & runaway controlCap iterations, tokens, wall-clock time, and tool calls per loop. Decide in advance what happens at each limit: hard stop, checkpoint-and-summarize, or escalate to a human.
Convergence detectionA loop that is not converging looks different from one that is working: shrinking diffs and narrowing plans versus repeated identical tool calls and oscillating decisions.
Stop conditionTriggerFailure mode if missing
Task completeVerifier, test suite, or schema validation confirms the goal state was reached.Loop keeps "polishing" a finished task, burning budget for no quality gain.
Budget exhaustedIteration count, token count, wall-clock time, or tool-call count hits a hard ceiling.Single runaway session consumes a tenant's entire daily cost budget.
No progressDiff size, plan content, or tool-call sequence is unchanged or repeating across N iterations.Agent loops on the same failed action indefinitely, mistaking motion for progress.
Confidence thresholdSelf-assessed or judge-scored confidence in the current answer crosses a set floor.Low-confidence output ships because the loop never paused to ask whether it was done.
Escalation triggerLoop detects ambiguity, conflicting instructions, or an action outside its risk tier.Agent guesses past a decision a human should have made, silently.
Do not ship a bare while(true).Prefer: an explicit budget (max iterations, tokens, time) checked every pass, with a defined action at the limit.
Do not treat iteration count as a quality proxy.Prefer: a verifier or eval signal as the stop condition; more loops is not the same as more correct.
Do not retry an identical failed action.Prefer: detect repetition (same tool, same args, same error) and force a replan or escalate instead of repeating it.
Do not skip checkpoints between iterations.Prefer: persist state every N iterations so a killed loop resumes instead of restarting from zero.
Production rule: log per-iteration cost, diff size, and tool calls to the same trace used for eval and cost engineering. A loop's iteration count distribution in production is itself a health metric — a sudden shift signals a regression upstream, not just downstream cost.
// Lifecycle, progress contracts & recovery
Created → Planning
Bind the goal, identity, permissions, risk tier, global budget, and success criteria. A loop should not begin until its completion and escalation contracts are explicit.
Executing ↔ Observing
Each pass emits an action, observation, cost delta, progress delta, and updated state. Tool permissions may narrow as the loop enters higher-risk phases.
Evaluating
Choose one transition: continue, replan, branch, roll back, compensate, escalate, or complete. Never let “continue” be the implicit default.
Progress contractFor each task define the goal metric, current value, expected direction, minimum meaningful delta, measurement window, and failure action. Progress is task-specific: declining test failures, increasing evidence coverage, or shrinking unresolved dependencies.
Adaptive budgetStart with a small allowance and grant more iterations only when measurable progress exists, the expected value of another step exceeds its cost, and risk remains within policy.
Nested-loop accountingChild loops inherit or reserve budget from the parent. Enforce global limits on fan-out × iterations, maximum loop depth, cancellation propagation, and verifier/retriever subloops.
Checkpoint & replayPersist prompt/model versions, tool inputs and outputs, evidence IDs, state transitions, approvals, and environment versions. Support trace replay with mocked tools and counterfactual replay against a new model or policy.
Recovery operationMeaningUse when
RetryRepeat the same operation.The failure is transient and the action is idempotent.
ReplanGenerate a different route to the same goal.The current approach is blocked or no longer promising.
RollbackRestore a prior valid checkpoint.A state mutation produced an invalid intermediate state.
BranchPreserve the current path while testing an alternative.Two plausible strategies need bounded comparison.
CompensateExecute a semantic reversal of an external side effect.The action cannot be transactionally rolled back.
EscalateTransfer control with state and evidence intact.Risk, ambiguity, permissions, or unresolved conflict exceed the loop's authority.
// Idempotency ruleNever blindly retry a state-changing tool. Require an idempotency key, a transactional rollback, or a tested compensation action before automatic retry.
Continue toAgentic Workflow Patterns & Control Loops →
Production takeaway — A production loop is not merely repeated prompting; it is a governed state machine with bounded iteration, verification, recovery, and observable termination.
Continue to Workflow Patterns →
// Agentic Workflow Patterns & Control Loops

2.3Workflow Patterns

Reusable control structures for routing, iteration, verification, and coordination.

Key distinction: Control loops define how an agent iterates. Workflow patterns define how work is organized. Architectures define how agents are structured.

Agentic Workflow Patterns ├── Routing & Decomposition │ ├── Classify-and-Act │ ├── Route-and-Execute │ ├── Query Decomposition │ └── Hierarchical Planning ├── Exploration │ ├── Fanout-and-Synthesize │ ├── Tree Search │ ├── Beam Search │ └── MCTS / LATS ├── Generation │ ├── Generate-and-Filter │ ├── Generate-and-Rank │ ├── Self-Consistency │ └── Draft-and-Refine ├── Evaluation & Verification │ ├── Tournament │ ├── Judge Model │ ├── Adversarial Verification │ ├── Debate │ └── Prosecutor–Defense–Judge ├── Iterative Control │ ├── ReAct │ ├── Plan–Execute–Reflect │ ├── Ralph Loop │ ├── Replan Loop │ └── Loop-Until-Done └── Multi-Agent Coordination ├── Planner–Executor ├── Orchestrator–Worker ├── Supervisor ├── Consensus Swarm └── Blackboard

Pattern Selection Guide

Need Routing? → Classify-and-Act Need Multiple Perspectives? → Fanout-and-Synthesize Need Candidate Generation? → Generate-and-Filter Need Best Of Many? → Tournament Need High Trust? → Adversarial Verification Need Specialists? → Orchestrator–Worker Need Autonomy? → Loop-Until-Done

How Patterns Nest in Production

Classify-and-Act
→
Planner–Executor
→
Fanout-and-Synthesize
→
Generate-and-Filter
→
Tournament
→
Adversarial Verification
→
Loop-Until-Done
GoalRecommended Pattern
Route requestsClassify-and-Act
Deep researchFanout-and-Synthesize + Verification
Best code candidateTournament
Reduce hallucinationsAdversarial Verification
Autonomous executionLoop-Until-Done
Multi-domain workOrchestrator–Worker
Continue toFrequently confused terms →
Production takeaway — Compose patterns hierarchically, but preserve one accountable controller for budgets, state transitions, and final acceptance.
Part III

Data, Knowledge, Context & Memory

Design the data substrate, retrieval systems, knowledge structures, context assembly, caches, durable state, and memory lifecycle that ground agent behavior.
In this part
  • 3.1Data Substrate for Agentic Performance
  • 3.2Data Engineering for Agentic Systems
  • 3.3Knowledge Base Engineering
  • 3.4Knowledge Graph Engineering
  • 3.5Knowledge-Grounded Agent Loops
  • 3.6Agent Systems Engineering
  • 3.7Context Engineering
  • 3.8State Placement Decision Tree
Terminology guide

Frequently Confused Terms

Use this quick guide to separate adjacent concepts. The full, authoritative definitions live in the canonical glossary near the end of the document.

Transformer KV cacheModel-runtime inference state; not application memory or a key-value database.
Prefix cacheReuse of stable prompt prefixes to reduce repeated prefill work.
RedisHot, reconstructable state, caches, leases, and coordination.
DynamoDBDurable operational state, checkpoints, idempotency, and session metadata.
StateMachine-readable workflow progress required to continue execution.
MemoryPrior experience retained because it may improve future behavior.
KnowledgeExternally governed information treated as a source of truth.
ContextThe model-visible working set assembled for one invocation.

Browse the complete canonical glossary (122 terms) →

Continue toKnowledge Base Engineering →

3.1Data Substrate for Agentic Performance

Agentic AI performance is not only a model problem. The data substrate controls how quickly an agent can assemble context, how accurately it can ground decisions, how safely it can respect permissions, and how well it can learn from traces. Treat relational, vector, graph, time-series, document, and in-memory databases as complementary performance infrastructure rather than interchangeable storage options.

🧬

3.1.1 Agent Data Loop

how data improves behavior
1 · Request
The user request enters the planner with identity, tenant, session, and policy context. This is where relational permissions and session state prevent unsafe or irrelevant work from starting.
2 · Context assembly
The agent gathers structured records, semantic passages, relationship paths, recent events, artifacts, and cache hits before the model reasons. Better context assembly usually beats larger prompts.
3 · Tool execution
The runtime calls tools with bounded schemas, transaction checks, and fresh data. Relational and in-memory systems keep actions correct and fast; graph and vector systems keep actions grounded.
4 · Response
The answer is produced with citations, provenance, policy filtering, and evidence. The database layer gives the model facts, not just memories.
5 · Feedback
Traces, eval outcomes, user corrections, events, and cost/latency metrics flow back into observability, memory, and future routing decisions.
🗃️

3.1.2 Database Type → Agent Capability

role map
Relational DBImproves correctness, permissions, joins, transactions, account state, workflow state, and auditability. Use it for users, tenants, ACLs, orders, approvals, tasks, and source-of-truth business records.
PostgreSQLAuroraMySQL
Vector DBImproves semantic recall, grounding, similarity search, deduplication, and retrieval-augmented generation. Use it for passages, chunks, embeddings, multimodal search, and nearest-neighbor memory.
pgvectorpgvectorscaleOpenSearchS3 Vectors
Graph DB Improves relationship reasoning, multi-hop retrieval, explainable paths, dependency analysis, entity resolution, permission-aware traversal, and GraphRAG. Use it when the agent must answer how entities are connected, what depends on what, which policies or obligations apply, or which evidence path supports a conclusion.
Amazon Neptune Neptune Analytics openCypher Gremlin / SPARQL
Graph Schema Design for Agents The highest-leverage decision in a graph system is what to model before you ingest anything. A schema designed for completeness rather than retrieval produces a graph that is expensive to traverse and hard to query.
Model entities as nodes People, organizations, documents, products, systems, concepts, risks, obligations, and events. Each node type gets a label. Node properties store attributes — name, ID, timestamp, confidence, permission tags.
Model facts as relationships WORKS_AT, DEPENDS_ON, OWNED_BY, CITED_IN, APPLIES_TO, CONNECTED_TO. Relationships should be typed, directed, and carry properties: confidence, provenance chunk ID, extraction timestamp. Avoid relationships named RELATED_TO — too vague for traversal.
Keep long text out of nodes Long document text belongs in the vector layer, not on graph nodes. Node properties should be short, structured, and queryable (name, ID, category, date, permission label). The graph and vector layers are complementary — don't duplicate content between them.
Schema design rule: Start from the questions the agent needs to answer, not from the documents you have. Work backwards from "How is A connected to B?" to the relationship type that path requires. Every node type and relationship type in your schema should be traceable to a real retrieval use case.
Time-Series DBImproves temporal awareness, trend detection, anomaly detection, monitoring, forecasting, and event memory. Use it for metrics, logs, prices, sensor streams, agent latency, and drift signals.
TimescaleDBkdb+ / KXPrometheus
Document DBImproves flexible state, semi-structured records, artifacts, conversation snapshots, evolving schemas, and tool outputs. Use it when agent state is nested, variable, or changing quickly.
MongoDBDocumentDBDynamoDB
In-Memory DBImproves latency, working memory, queues, locks, rate limits, deduplication, and session continuity. Use it for short-lived context and fast coordination, not as the only durable memory.
RedisValkeyMemcached
Hot KV & durable operational stateRedis accelerates sessions, caches, locks, rate limits, and coordination. DynamoDB preserves checkpoints, idempotency, approvals, retries, and session metadata. Keep cache, state, memory, knowledge, and audit as distinct responsibilities.
🐘

3.1.3 PostgreSQL Extension Stack for Agentic Systems

retrieval · telemetry · graph · scale · operations

Postgres is not only the relational layer. Through extensions it can become the semantic retrieval layer, lexical search layer, graph layer, time-series layer, scheduler, query-observability layer, distributed SQL layer, in-database ML runtime, and analytical execution surface. Every extension registers its capabilities via PostgreSQL's CREATE EXTENSION mechanism, which runs an install script that may register custom index access methods, background workers, executor hooks, and shared memory segments. Treat extensions as composable capabilities, not random plugins.

Diagram · PostgreSQL Extension Capability Map
🐘
PostgreSQL Core
MVCC · WAL · SQL planner · indexes · transactions · extensions
Retrievalpgvector pgvectorscale
pg_search pg_textsearch
Semantic nearest-neighbor search plus lexical/BM25 recall.
Time + TelemetryTimescaleDB pg_stat_statements
Agent traces, cost curves, latency history, and SQL hot spots.
Automation + MLpg_cron pgml
Scheduled refreshes, cleanup jobs, backfills, and selective in-database inference.
Scale + AnalyticsCitus pg_duckdb
Tenant sharding, distributed SQL, OLAP scans, and lakehouse-style analytics.
pgvector — semantic retrieval
IVFFlat · HNSW · cosine / L2 / dot operators
pgvector adds a native vector column type and distance operators (<->, <#>, <=>) to Postgres. It registers two custom index access methods via IndexAmRoutine: IVFFlat, which partitions vectors into inverted file lists for fast approximate search; and HNSW, which builds a multi-layer navigable small-world graph where each node connects to nearby vectors at multiple zoom levels — enabling fast greedy traversal at query time with high recall. HNSW is generally preferred for production RAG. Combine with standard SQL filters, joins, and transactions without any data sync overhead.
HNSW preferred for RAGSQL + vectors in one DBzero extra infra
pgvectorscale — large-scale ANN
DiskANN · streaming graph · RAM-efficient
pgvectorscale adds a DiskANN-style streaming index. Unlike HNSW, which loads the full graph into memory, DiskANN is designed for vector datasets that exceed available RAM — it streams graph neighborhoods from disk using sequential I/O patterns that keep page faults low. The result is dramatically better recall-per-second at scale for billion-scale embedding workloads. It sits on top of pgvector (not a replacement) and registers via the same index AM interface. Graduate from pgvector HNSW to pgvectorscale when your index no longer fits in memory or you need higher QPS at scale.
billion-scale vectorsdisk-resident indexpgvector complement
TimescaleDB — temporal telemetry
hypertables · chunks · LZ4/gorilla/delta-delta compression · continuous aggregates
TimescaleDB introduces hypertables — logical tables transparently partitioned into chunks by time (and optionally by space). Queries hit only relevant chunks via constraint exclusion. Older chunks are compressed using columnar codecs (LZ4 for general data, gorilla for floats, delta-delta for timestamps) — reducing storage 10–20×. Continuous aggregates are materialized views that refresh incrementally rather than recomputing from scratch. The planner hook enables chunk-level parallelism. Use for agent traces, token costs, latency curves, eval scores over time, and drift signals. Pairs with pgvector: store embedding vectors alongside their timestamps to query "most similar embeddings in the last 7 days."
