Postgres-native time-seriescontinuous aggregatesretention policies
pg_search — BM25 via Tantivy
BM25 · faceting · phrase slop · fuzzy · @@@ operator
pg_search (ParadeDB) embeds a Tantivy index (a Rust-based Lucene equivalent) as a custom Postgres index AM. Unlike native tsvector which uses simple term frequency, BM25 is a probabilistic ranking function that accounts for term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document-length normalization. It supports field boosting, phrase slop, and faceted search. Query via the @@@ operator. Stored as custom index AM structures inside Postgres buffer manager. Pair with pgvector for hybrid RAG — use BM25 for exact terms (product names, error codes, legal clauses) and vector search for semantic recall, then RRF-fuse the results. Best when you want Elasticsearch-style relevance without operating a separate cluster.
BM25 probabilistic rankinghybrid RAGno separate cluster
pg_textsearch — BM25 via Postgres FTS path
BM25 · Postgres text-search infrastructure · tickers · IDs · clauses
pg_textsearch (Timescale) leans into Postgres's native text-search plumbing (tsvector/tsquery infrastructure) while layering BM25 scoring on top. Where pg_search introduces a fully separate Tantivy index AM, pg_textsearch works closer to the standard FTS execution path, making it easier to combine with existing Postgres FTS indexes and GIN indexes. Best fit when exact tokens — tickers, IDs, legal clause identifiers, and structured error strings — must survive retrieval alongside semantic vector search. Pair with pgvector for hybrid retrieval pipelines in Postgres-centered stacks.
BM25 keyword relevancePostgres FTS pathstructured token survival
pg_cron — in-database scheduler
background worker · cron.job table · SQL or CALL invocations
pg_cron runs as a background worker (registered via Postgres's BGW API) that wakes every minute, reads the cron.job catalog table, and forks a new database connection to execute any matching jobs — SQL statements or stored procedure CALLs. This eliminates external schedulers for database-internal tasks. Use it for materialized-view refreshes, embedding backfills, retention cleanup, rollups, stale-document audits, and lightweight scheduled eval batches. Not a full workflow orchestrator — keep multi-step DAGs in Airflow, Dagster, or Temporal; use pg_cron for maintenance operations that live close to the data.
background workercron syntaxmaintenance automation
pgml / PostgresML — in-database ML
pgml.train() · pgml.predict() · pgml.embed() · Rust/PyO3 bindings
pgml embeds ML inference and training inside Postgres via Rust bindings (PyO3) to scikit-learn, XGBoost, LightGBM, and Hugging Face transformers. Functions like pgml.train(), pgml.predict(), and pgml.embed() run in-process, keeping data local and avoiding round-trips to an external Python service. You can train a gradient-boosted classifier or generate sentence embeddings in a SQL query. Best fit for controlled internal workloads and data-local inference — avoid turning the primary OLTP Postgres into a high-throughput model-serving tier unless the platform is explicitly designed for that load.
in-process inferenceRust/PyO3 bindingsdata-local ML
Citus — distributed Postgres
coordinator · workers · consistent hashing · 32 shards default · co-located joins
Citus introduces a coordinator node and multiple worker nodes. Tables are distributed by a shard key using consistent hashing — 32 shards by default, placed across workers. The coordinator receives queries, rewrites them into fragment queries targeting relevant shards, and fans them out in parallel. For co-located joins (joining on the same distribution key), Citus pushes the join down to workers entirely. It also supports reference tables (replicated to all workers) and columnar storage. The extension hooks into Postgres's executor using custom plan nodes and hooks. Use when tenant_id / account_id is a natural shard key and one node can no longer handle write throughput for documents, chunks, runs, and tool events.
tenant shardingco-located joinsscale-out Postgres
pg_stat_statements — query observability
ExecutorEnd hook · normalized query hash · buffer hits · WAL writes
pg_stat_statements intercepts every SQL query via the ExecutorEnd and ExecutorStart planner hooks, normalizes it (replacing literals with placeholders), and accumulates execution stats in a shared memory hash table keyed by query hash: total calls, total/min/max/mean execution time, rows returned, buffer hits/misses, and WAL writes. Planning time is captured separately. Essential for finding slow vector searches, missing ANN filters, expensive hybrid retrieval joins, and N+1 patterns generated by ORM or agent tool layers. The production default — enable it from day one.
query statsshared memory hashproduction default
pg_duckdb — OLAP inside Postgres
vectorized columnar execution · DuckDB scan nodes · Parquet/S3 · foreign tables
pg_duckdb embeds DuckDB's analytical engine inside Postgres as an execution engine. When a query touches a duckdb foreign table or uses DuckDB-specific syntax, the extension routes it to DuckDB's vectorized columnar executor (SIMD batch processing) rather than Postgres's row-based executor. DuckDB excels at aggregations, multi-column scans, and wide-table joins. Results are returned to Postgres as normal rows. You can also read Parquet files directly from S3 via DuckDB's scanner. Treat as an OLAP accelerator for heavy analytics next to operational data — not a replacement for OLTP query discipline.
OLAP accelerationParquet / S3 scanvectorized execution
How extensions hook into PostgresMost extensions use a combination of: custom index access methods (IndexAmRoutine), background worker processes (BackgroundWorkerHandle), executor/planner hooks (ExecutorRun_hook, planner_hook), custom scan providers, and shared memory segments. CREATE EXTENSION runs the install SQL script that registers these hooks in the catalog. The extension boundary is powerful, but it is still part of your database reliability and security model.
Architecture ruleStart simple: Postgres + pgvector (HNSW) + pg_stat_statements + pg_cron. Add pg_search or pg_textsearch when exact terms matter alongside semantic recall. Add TimescaleDB when traces and latency become operational data you query over time. Add AGE when relationships become first-class retrieval paths. Add Citus when one node is not enough and tenant_id is a natural shard key. Add pg_duckdb when OLAP scans over operational data get heavy. Add pgml only when in-database inference is genuinely cheaper or simpler than an external model service. Upgrade pgvector → pgvectorscale when the HNSW index no longer fits in RAM.
Diagram · PostgreSQL Extension Selection Decision Tree
Need semantic similarity?
Yes → pgvector
Index too large for RAM → pgvectorscale
Do exact terms / IDs matter?
Yes → pg_search or pg_textsearch
Use with vector search for hybrid recall.
Are traces/time ranges core?
Yes → TimescaleDB
Use for latency, cost, evaluation, drift, and event history.
Need scheduled DB-native jobs?
Yes → pg_cron
Refresh materialized views, summarize memory, clean traces, and run backfills.
Are SQL queries opaque or slow?
Always → pg_stat_statements
Baseline observability for retrieval, joins, filters, and index behavior.
Is one Postgres node not enough?
Yes → Citus
Best when tenant_id or account/workspace ID is a natural shard key.
Need OLAP / Parquet-style scans?
Yes → pg_duckdb
Use for analytical scans without moving operational data too far away.
Need in-database ML?
Maybe → pgml
Use only when local inference is simpler or cheaper than external model serving.
🔀

3.1.4 Hybrid Retrieval Flow

how it works

For high-value agents, retrieval should be hybrid: the system should extract intent and entities, call multiple data stores in parallel, then merge, rerank, filter, and cite evidence before the model responds.

QueryUser asks a question or requests an action.
ParseExtract intent, entities, tenant, permissions, time range, and freshness needs.
RetrieveSQL lookup + vector search + graph traversal + recent events + session cache.
AssembleRerank, dedupe, policy-filter, compress, and attach provenance.
Act / AnswerUse grounded context for response, tool call, approval request, or workflow step.
Diagram · Postgres-native hybrid retrieval path
User question → query planParse intent, tenant, permissions, entities, source constraints, time range, and freshness requirements before retrieval begins.
↓
1 · Semantic Search
pgvectorpgvectorscale
Find meaning-similar chunks with vector indexes while preserving tenant, source, and freshness filters.
2 · Keyword / BM25
pg_searchpg_textsearch
Recover exact terms, tickers, IDs, clause names, error codes, and rare entities that embeddings may smooth over.
3 · Graph Expansion
Amazon NeptuneNeptune Analytics
Expand from retrieved entities to connected accounts, policies, instruments, dependencies, owners, or evidence paths.
↓
Candidate mergeUnion vector, BM25, SQL, and graph results into a single candidate set before ranking.
RerankUse RRF, cross-encoder score, policy score, freshness, and source trust.
Context PackDedupe, compress, attach provenance, and fit the prompt budget.
Grounded PromptPass compact evidence, citations, constraints, and allowed actions to the model.
Answer / ActGenerate cited response, tool call, workflow step, or HITL approval request.
Why this mattersVector search gives recall by meaning; BM25 protects exactness; graph traversal adds relationship reasoning. Production RAG usually needs all three, especially for financial, compliance, engineering, and operational knowledge.
🕸️

3.1.5 Amazon Neptune GraphRAG Flow

graph-native retrieval

Amazon Neptune belongs in the architecture when the retrieval unit is an entity, relationship, path, neighborhood, community, permission boundary, or evidence chain. Vector search retrieves similar chunks; graph retrieval explains how facts connect.

1 · Extract entitiesPeople, organizations, accounts, documents, systems, products, risks, claims, events, owners, and obligations.
2 · Resolve identitiesMerge aliases, duplicate entities, ticker variants, account IDs, system names, and conflicting labels.
3 · Build graphStore nodes, relationships, properties, provenance, timestamps, confidence, and permission labels in Amazon Neptune Database.
4 · Retrieve hybrid contextCombine vector similarity in Neptune Analytics with openCypher, Gremlin, or SPARQL traversal, graph neighborhoods, shortest paths, relationship filters, and provenance filters.
5 · Answer with evidenceReturn answer, source chunks, graph path, confidence, lineage, and allowed actions.
Three hybrid retrieval patterns — choose by question type and cost tolerance
Entity-anchored retrieval
embed query → nearest entity nodes → traverse neighborhood
Embed the user query. Find the closest entity nodes using vector similarity. Then traverse the graph neighborhood around those entities via Cypher (e.g. 1–2 hops). Return the subgraph plus the source chunks that mention those entities.
lowest latencygood for lookup questions
Community summarization
pre-compute community summaries → retrieve summaries at query time
Detect graph communities (clusters of tightly connected entities) at index time. Use an LLM to write a summary of each community. At query time, retrieve the most relevant community summaries via vector search. This is Microsoft's original GraphRAG approach — high index cost, very low query-time cost.
global/thematic questionshigh index cost
Path-constrained retrieval
constrain traversal to typed paths · permission filters · relationship type filters
Traverse only along specific, typed relationship paths relevant to the question or the user's permission scope. Example: "Which obligations apply to this account?" traverses only APPLIES_TO and REGULATED_BY edges, filtered to the user's tenant. Prevents traversal from leaking into unrelated or unauthorized subgraphs.
compliance / permissionsaccess-controlled traversal
Cost curves differ: Entity-anchored has the lowest query latency and works well for most knowledge graph use cases. Community summarization has high index cost but very fast and broad query-time answers — use it when questions are global or thematic. Path-constrained is essential for regulated environments where retrieval scope must be enforced at the relationship level.
Retrieval insight · when graph beats vector searchUse Amazon Neptune when the relationship is the answer. If the user asks “how is A connected to B,” “what depends on X,” “which obligations apply to this account,” or “what changed upstream of this report,” the retrieval unit should be a path or neighborhood, not only a chunk.
Best agentic usesAgent memory graphs, permission graphs, data lineage graphs, compliance graphs, incident dependency graphs, financial entity graphs, and GraphRAG over document corpora.
Do not use it for everythingSkip graph infrastructure when the workload is simple document RAG, pure key-value lookup, normal relational reporting, or entirely similarity-based.
🔄

3.1.6 GraphRAG Incremental Updates vs Full Rebuild

graph freshness

The RAG embedding pipeline has a well-understood reindex discipline: pin the embedding model, upsert by document ID, trigger full reindex on model upgrade. The graph equivalent is more complex because new documents can introduce entities that should be merged with existing ones, and new relationships can alter the meaning of existing subgraphs.

ScenarioStrategyGraph operationRisk if skipped
New document addedExtract entities and relations from new doc only. MERGE into existing graph using canonical entity IDs.MERGE on entity ID; CREATE for new relationships; UPDATE provenance on existing nodes.Duplicate nodes accumulate; multi-hop paths break across the duplicate boundary.
Document updatedRe-extract from updated doc. Delete stale relations sourced from old version. Merge updated relations.Delete relations with old source chunk ID; re-run extraction; re-MERGE.Stale relationships persist; agent retrieves outdated connections.
Document deletedRemove all relationships with that document as provenance source. Orphan-check nodes — delete nodes whose only provenance was the deleted doc.DELETE relationships by provenance; DELETE orphaned nodes with no remaining relations.Agent traverses ghost relationships that no longer have valid source evidence.
New entity alias discoveredRun disambiguation across new entity and existing candidates. Merge duplicates into canonical node. Repoint all relationships from merged node.MERGE node IDs; redirect relationship endpoints; update entity index.Fragmented nodes break multi-hop reasoning — "MSFT" and "Microsoft" appear as unconnected entities.
Community structure changesIf using community summarization (Microsoft GraphRAG approach): recompute affected communities after significant graph changes. Re-generate community summaries with LLM.Re-run community detection (Louvain, Leiden); re-embed community summaries.Stale community summaries give wrong thematic answers for global queries.
Schema change (new relation type)Treat as a partial rebuild for affected document set. Back-fill extraction with new relation type. Do not full-rebuild unless the schema change is pervasive.Re-extract targeted document set; add new relation type to schema; verify existing traversals still work.Inconsistent schema — some subgraphs have new relation types, others do not.
Operations rule · treat graph updates like schema migrationsEvery incremental graph update is a structural change to shared state, not just a data write. MERGE semantics prevent blind duplicates, but entity resolution must run before the MERGE — otherwise two canonically identical entities arrive as separate nodes and silently split the graph. Build a pre-merge disambiguation step into the ingestion pipeline and run it before every MERGE operation. Test with cross-document queries that require traversal across the newly ingested entities before declaring an update complete.
⚠️

3.1.7 Data-Layer Anti-Patterns

avoid these
Vector-only memory
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Semantic search is not a substitute for permissions, transactions, temporal ordering, or source-of-truth records. Pair vector search with relational and policy layers.
Everything in the prompt
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Large prompts increase latency and cost while reducing controllability. Store durable facts, retrieve selectively, and compress context before model invocation.
Graph before relationships matter
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Graph databases are powerful when relationship traversal is central. Do not add them just because the system is called an agent.
No cache or TTL strategy
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Fast agent loops need explicit cache invalidation, session expiry, rate-limit state, and freshness rules. Stale cache can be worse than no cache.
No audit trail
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
If an agent can take action, persist who asked, what context was used, what policy checks ran, what tool was called, and what changed.
No retrieval evaluation
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Measure recall, precision, citation accuracy, freshness, latency, and permission leakage separately from final-answer quality.
Graph schema designed for completeness, not retrieval
Currentreviewed 2026-05-31
RiskMarket-map
Building a graph that models everything in the corpus before knowing what questions the agent needs to answer. The result is a large, expensive graph that is hard to traverse because the schema was not designed backwards from real retrieval use cases. Start with the questions, then define the schema.
Using GraphRAG for simple document Q&A
Currentreviewed 2026-05-31
RiskMarket-map
Adding a knowledge graph to a workload that is purely similarity-based lookup. If the user is asking "what does the policy say about expense limits," standard RAG is cheaper, faster, and equally accurate. GraphRAG earns its cost when the answer requires traversing entity connections. Add the graph only after standard RAG fails on relationship questions.
Duplicating document text into node properties
Currentreviewed 2026-05-31
RiskMarket-map
Storing full document text in graph node properties rather than keeping long text in the vector layer. This conflates the two layers, bloats the graph database, and duplicates data with no retrieval benefit. Node properties should be short, structured, and queryable. Long text belongs in the vector index; node properties carry IDs and references back to it.
No entity resolution before graph load
Auditreviewed 2026-05-31
RiskMarket-map
Loading extracted entities into the graph without running disambiguation first. "Microsoft," "MSFT," and "Microsoft Corp" arrive as three separate nodes. Multi-hop paths between them break. Entity resolution must run before the MERGE operation for every ingestion batch, not just the initial load.

3.2Data Engineering for Agentic Systems

Agent quality is upstream of model quality. The pipelines that move, clean, transform, and version the data your agents reason over are the unsexy infrastructure that determines whether RAG works in production.

3.2.1 Streaming Ingestion — Keeping RAG Fresh

A vector index is a snapshot. Without a streaming ingestion pipeline, it grows stale the moment source data changes. For most production systems, retrieval staleness is a silent failure — the agent confidently returns outdated facts with no error. The fix is a streaming pipeline that propagates source changes into the index within minutes, not days.

1
Capture source changes
CDC (Change Data Capture) on your operational database via Debezium → Kafka. Or webhook events from your CMS/CRM. Or S3 event notifications on document uploads. Every source system needs a change event, not a scheduled full dump.
debezium · kafka · kinesis · s3 events · webhooks
2
Quality gate before indexing
Every document passes a validation step before embedding: schema check (expected fields present), content check (non-empty, expected language, no injection patterns), deduplication check (content hash not already indexed). Reject and DLQ documents that fail — never silently drop them.
great expectations · soda · pandera · custom validators
3
Chunk, embed, upsert
Apply your chunking strategy, embed with the pinned embedding model, upsert into the vector store with metadata: source_id, version, ingested_at, content_hash, tenant_id. Upsert (not insert) so re-indexing an updated document replaces the old vectors rather than duplicating them.
langchain · llama-index · custom chunker → openai / cohere / voyage
4
Handle deletes explicitly
Source deletes are the hardest part of streaming ingestion. A document deleted from the source must be deleted from the vector store — or the agent will keep retrieving and citing it. Track source_id → vector_ids in a side table so a delete event can remove all associated vectors. This mapping is the most commonly forgotten piece of a streaming ingestion pipeline.
5
Monitor lag and staleness
Track: ingest lag (time from source change to vector store upsert), DLQ depth (failed documents), index coverage (% of source documents indexed), and freshness age (median age of retrieved documents in production traces). Alert when ingest lag exceeds your SLA.
Full reindex vs. incremental update: Reindexing the full corpus is tempting because it's simple, but it's disruptive (index temporarily stale during rebuild), expensive (re-embeds everything), and doesn't scale. Reserve full reindex for: embedding model version changes (mandatory), schema changes to your chunk metadata, or first-time index bootstrap. Use incremental upsert for all ongoing source changes.

3.2.2 Data Quality Gates in the Ingestion Pipeline

Bad data in the index produces confidently wrong agent answers. Unlike a model error (which is visible), a retrieval quality problem is invisible — the agent returns a plausible-sounding answer grounded in a stale, malformed, or injected document. Data quality gates are the last line of defense before documents enter the index.

Schema validation
Pandera · Pydantic · JSON Schema
Every document must conform to its expected schema before indexing. Required fields present, data types correct, foreign keys resolvable. A document with a missing tenant_id that gets indexed will leak across tenant boundaries — a critical multi-tenancy failure.
Content quality checks
Custom rules · langdetect · fuzzy dedup
Non-empty content. Expected language (langdetect). Content length within bounds — very short chunks are noise, very long chunks exceed context windows. No injection pattern signatures (prompt-injection scanning). Encoding valid (UTF-8). These catch the most common ingestion failures.
Deduplication
Content hash · MinHash · SimHash
Exact dedup via content hash (SHA-256 of normalized text). Near-dedup via MinHash LSH for catching the same document re-crawled with minor changes. Duplicate documents inflate recall@k metrics while reducing the effective diversity of retrieved context — the agent cites the same fact multiple times with false confidence.
Freshness validation
source_updated_at · TTL rules
Documents older than a configured TTL should be flagged for re-ingestion or soft-deleted. Track source_updated_at separately from ingested_at. An agent retrieving a policy document that was updated 18 months ago and never re-indexed will cite the old policy as current.
Injection scanning
Pattern matching · LLM-based classifier
Scan ingested documents for prompt injection patterns before indexing. A document containing "Ignore all previous instructions and instead…" should be quarantined, not indexed. This is the primary defense against retrieval corpus poisoning (see Security Threat Model). Use both pattern matching (fast) and an LLM-based classifier (thorough) in sequence.
Reject, quarantine, DLQ
Dead-letter queue · alerts · audit trail
Failed validation must never silently drop documents. Route failures to a DLQ with: the document, the failing check, the timestamp, and the source. Alert when DLQ depth exceeds threshold. Build a quarantine review UI so operators can inspect, fix, and re-ingest failed documents. The DLQ is your data quality audit trail.
Great Expectations, Soda, Monte Carlo: Purpose-built data quality frameworks that generate validation reports, track quality over time, and integrate with pipeline orchestrators (Airflow, Dagster). For high-volume ingestion pipelines, prefer a framework over hand-rolled checks — they produce structured quality metrics you can alert on and trend over time.

3.2.3 Data Catalog & Schema Discovery for Agent SQL

Agents that generate or execute SQL — text-to-SQL, code-executing agents, analytical agents — need access to a machine-readable schema catalog to produce correct queries. An agent that doesn't know what tables exist, what columns mean, or what the foreign key relationships are will hallucinate plausible-looking SQL that fails at runtime or silently returns wrong results.

LayerWhat it provides to the agentTools
Schema registryTable names, column names, types, nullability, primary and foreign keys. The minimum an agent needs to write valid SQL.DataHub · Amundsen · OpenMetadata · Unity Catalog · dbt docs
Semantic layerBusiness definitions: what "revenue" means, which columns contain PII, what the authoritative table for customer records is. Prevents the agent from joining on the wrong key or aggregating the wrong metric.dbt metrics · Cube.dev · AtScale · LookML
Permissions layerWhich tables and columns this agent (or user) is allowed to query. Must be enforced at query execution time, not just in the system prompt. Row-level and column-level security.Unity Catalog · Apache Ranger · Postgres RLS · BigQuery IAM
Sample dataRepresentative values per column so the agent can understand cardinality, format, and expected values without running a full scan.dbt sources · catalog samplers · profiling tools
Query historyPreviously successful queries for similar questions — analogous to few-shot examples but drawn from real query patterns rather than hand-written examples.Snowflake query history · BigQuery INFORMATION_SCHEMA · dbt exposures
Inject catalog context, not full schema dumps. Full database schemas can be enormous. Inject only the tables relevant to the question — use semantic search over the catalog (embed table descriptions, retrieve top-k relevant tables) the same way you use RAG for document retrieval. A well-designed schema retrieval pipeline is RAG for your database schema.
dbt as the semantic layer: If your team already uses dbt, you have a schema catalog for free. dbt docs generate produces a structured JSON artifact with all table descriptions, column definitions, and lineage. Parse this artifact and load it into your agent's context assembly pipeline as the authoritative schema source.

3.2.4 Lakehouse Architecture & Open Table Formats

As agents increasingly query analytical corpora — not just vector indexes — the underlying storage format determines schema evolution, time travel, and ACID guarantees on the data the agent reasons over. Open table formats are becoming the standard substrate for agent-queryable analytical data.

Apache Iceberg
Open table format · ACID · schema evolution · time travel
The most widely adopted open table format for cloud lakehouses. ACID transactions, schema evolution without full rewrites, partition evolution, and time travel queries (AS OF timestamp). Supported by Spark, Flink, Trino, Athena, BigQuery. For agents: time travel enables reproducible queries — run the same analytical query against a snapshot from a specific date.
Delta Lake
Databricks-origin · ACID · DML on Parquet
Originated at Databricks, now Linux Foundation open source. ACID transactions on Parquet files, Z-order clustering for query performance, Change Data Feed for CDC-style downstream consumption. If your stack is Databricks-native, Delta is the default. For agents querying Databricks-managed data, Delta tables with Unity Catalog are the production standard.
dbt (data build tool)
SQL transformation layer · lineage · docs · tests
dbt transforms raw data into agent-queryable models using version-controlled SQL. dbt test runs data quality assertions on every model. dbt docs generates the schema catalog agents need for text-to-SQL. dbt lineage DAG shows which upstream tables a model depends on — critical for understanding the provenance of data an agent cites.
// DE/ML insight · why this matters for agentsTime travel queries are reproducible agent behavior. If an agent answers an analytical question today and you need to reproduce that answer tomorrow, you need the data to be queryable as-of the same timestamp. Open table formats (Iceberg AS OF, Delta time travel) make agent answers reproducible in the same way that pinning model versions makes model outputs reproducible. Without time travel, analytical agent answers are unreproducible — a compliance and audit problem in regulated industries.
// MLOps insight · when agents call ML models as toolsIf your agent calls a traditional ML model as a tool — a recommender, a classifier, a propensity scorer — that model has a feature store dependency you must expose to the agent. The feature store serves engineered features to the ML model at serving time; the agent's tool call is the serving request. Design: (1) wrap the ML model call in a typed tool schema that hides the feature store internals; (2) version the tool schema alongside the model version — a model retrain that changes input features is a breaking tool contract change; (3) monitor feature freshness for the features the tool depends on — a stale feature store produces stale tool results the agent will reason over as if they were current. Tools: Feast, Tecton, Hopsworks, Redis-based feature serving.

3.2.5 Enterprise Data + AI Platforms for Agents

Databricks and Snowflake are not primarily agent frameworks. They sit under and beside the agent loop as governed enterprise data, retrieval, AI-serving, and evaluation platforms. Agent frameworks decide what to do next; these platforms decide what trusted data, tools, models, policies, and lineage the agent can safely use.

Databricks
Lakehouse · ML lifecycle · Unity Catalog · MLflow · Vector Search
Best when the agent is close to data engineering, ML engineering, feature pipelines, notebooks, model experimentation, or lakehouse-scale analytical data. Use Databricks as the governed data and ML lifecycle backbone: Delta Lake for reproducible tables, Unity Catalog for permissions and lineage, Vector Search for RAG over lakehouse-managed corpora, MLflow for experiment/eval/model lifecycle, and Mosaic AI / Model Serving for deployment.
lakehouseMLflowUnity CatalogVector Search
Snowflake
Warehouse · SQL analytics · Cortex · Streamlit · governed business data
Best when the agent is close to BI, finance, revenue analytics, operations reporting, governed SQL, or business-user-facing data apps. Use Snowflake as the governed analytics backbone: Cortex Analyst for text-to-SQL over structured warehouse data, Cortex Search for search/RAG over Snowflake-managed content, Cortex Agents for routing across structured data, unstructured data, stored procedures, and UDFs, and Streamlit for internal copilot and review UIs.
warehouseCortextext-to-SQLStreamlit
DecisionUse Databricks when…Use Snowflake when…
Center of gravityThe work starts in lakehouse, Spark, notebooks, ML pipelines, feature engineering, or model lifecycle.The work starts in warehouse analytics, BI, governed SQL, finance/revenue/ops reporting, or business metrics.
Agent roleAgent calls governed data, feature/model tools, retrieval indexes, ML experiments, and model serving endpoints.Agent answers business questions, generates governed SQL, searches enterprise content, and launches internal data workflows.
Governance primitiveUnity Catalog + Delta Lake + MLflow lineage across data, models, evals, and serving artifacts.Snowflake governance + Cortex + warehouse permissions around structured and unstructured analytical assets.
UI/app patternML/data engineering workbench, evaluation dashboards, model/agent deployment workflows.Streamlit internal apps, analyst copilots, metric drill-downs, approval/review interfaces.
Use both when…Databricks owns ML/feature/model workflows while Snowflake remains the business warehouse. The agent can call both: Snowflake for trusted business metrics; Databricks for ML-derived predictions, embeddings, feature-backed tools, and lakehouse-scale analytical processing.
// Placement ruleDo not classify Databricks or Snowflake as substitutes for LangGraph, OpenAI Agents, Claude Agent SDK, or CrewAI. Classify them as enterprise data + AI platforms that provide governed data access, retrieval, SQL, model serving, evaluation, lineage, and internal app surfaces that agent frameworks can call into.
// Knowledge Base Engineering · v3.36

3.3Knowledge Base Engineering

Canonical treatmentKnowledge lifecycle, source authority, retrieval-unit contracts, freshness, and deletion.

A production knowledge base is an operated information product, not a vector index. Its job is to preserve authoritative content, provenance, permissions, time, lifecycle state, and deletion semantics while publishing multiple derived serving structures for retrieval.

1 · Source systems
Files, databases, APIs, event streams, SaaS systems, and human-authored knowledge enter through owned connectors with source identity and capture timestamps.
2 · Canonical store
Normalize documents and records into a versioned source-of-truth representation. Vector, lexical, graph, and cache layers are derived serving indexes—not the only copy of the knowledge.
3 · Enrichment
Generate chunks, metadata, entities, summaries, ACLs, effective dates, quality signals, and provenance links. Every derived object points back to a stable source and version.
4 · Serving indexes
Publish lexical, vector, graph, relational, analytical, and cache representations optimized for different question types and latency targets.
5 · Retrieval planner
Route exact identifiers to lexical/SQL, concepts to hybrid retrieval, relationships to graph traversal, aggregations to analytical systems, and historical questions to temporal filters.
Knowledge lifecycleDiscovered → ingested → parsed → validated → published → superseded → archived → deleted. Define retrieval eligibility, citation eligibility, index propagation, and retention behavior for every state.
Bitemporal knowledgeTrack valid time—when a fact was true—and system time—when the platform learned it. This supports point-in-time answers, audit reconstruction, and safe handling of late-arriving updates.
Trust tiersRank authoritative primary sources above approved derived sources, reviewed secondary material, unreviewed content, and external/user-supplied content. Relevance alone must not determine ranking.
Ownership & SLOsAssign source owner, knowledge product owner, steward, retrieval owner, ontology owner, and security owner. Operate freshness, coverage, parse quality, citation resolvability, ACL correctness, deletion propagation, and retrieval-quality SLOs.
Required retrieval-unit contractPurpose
Stable source ID + document version + chunk IDReconstruct exactly which material grounded an answer.
Source location + content hash + parser versionResolve citations and detect extraction or source drift.
Valid-from / valid-to + ingestion timestampAnswer historical questions and reject temporally invalid evidence.
ACL / tenant scope + sensitivitySecurity-trim before retrieval and evidence packing.
Embedding/index versionSupport reproducibility, migrations, and rollback.
Authority, quality, and extraction confidenceRank evidence by trust as well as semantic relevance.
Knowledge scoring: final evidence score should combine relevance × authority × freshness × permission validity × extraction confidence. A highly similar but stale or unauthorized passage is not good evidence.
Do not make the vector index the system of record.Prefer: a canonical, versioned document store with regenerable serving indexes.
Do not publish before validation.Prefer: schema, parse, ACL, provenance, and freshness gates before content becomes retrievable.
Do not treat deletion as removing one row.Prefer: propagate tombstones or hard deletes through chunks, embeddings, graph assertions, summaries, caches, and citations.
Do not equate absence with falsehood.Prefer: distinguish unknown, unavailable, inaccessible, deleted, and explicitly false.
Continue toKnowledge Graph Engineering →
Production takeaway — A useful knowledge base depends more on ingestion quality, provenance, chunking, metadata, and maintenance than on the retrieval interface alone.
Continue to Knowledge Graph Engineering →
// Knowledge Graph Engineering · v3.36

3.4Knowledge Graph Engineering

Canonical treatmentGraph modeling, entity resolution, provenance, temporal integrity, graph security, and Neptune implementation.

GraphRAG is a retrieval pattern; Knowledge Graph Engineering is the broader discipline of modeling, validating, evolving, securing, and operating connected knowledge. The graph must represent evidence and uncertainty—not merely extracted nouns joined by model-generated edges.

// Neptune implementation matrix
NeedPreferred capabilityImplementation guidance
Operational property graphAmazon Neptune DatabaseUse openCypher when Cypher-style pattern matching best fits the team; use Gremlin for traversal-centric application logic. Persist provenance, confidence, timestamps, tenant labels, and source identifiers with graph facts.
RDF and ontology workloadsNeptune Database + SPARQLUse RDF when standards-based semantics, linked data, formal ontologies, or interoperable vocabularies are primary requirements.
Graph algorithms and investigationNeptune AnalyticsUse for centrality, community detection, path analysis, similarity, fraud or dependency investigation, and analytical subgraphs without overloading the operational database.
Vector similarity over graph entitiesNeptune AnalyticsStore or synchronize entity embeddings and combine vector candidates with graph neighborhoods, path constraints, permissions, and temporal filters.
High-volume initial ingestionNeptune bulk loaderStage validated node and edge files in S3, load in batches, record source manifests, and reconcile rejected records before publishing the graph.
Incremental graph maintenanceAWS SDK or query APIs + event-driven pipelinesApply idempotent upserts, version assertions instead of silently overwriting them, and preserve deletion, supersession, and replay metadata.
Cross-source graph constructionAirflow or Dagster + extraction and entity resolutionSeparate extraction, normalization, entity resolution, relationship validation, graph loading, and quality checks into independently observable stages.
GraphRAGVector retrieval + Neptune traversal + evidence assemblyRetrieve candidate chunks or entities, expand through governed paths, rank evidence chains, fetch source spans, and return both citations and graph-path provenance.
Assertion-level factsRepresent consequential facts as assertions containing subject, predicate, object, source, valid interval, extraction method, confidence, review status, and supersession links. This preserves disagreement and provenance.
Entity resolutionUse candidate generation, blocking, similarity scoring, contextual disambiguation, canonical assignment, and human review for uncertain cases. Handle aliases, renames, subsidiaries, ticker changes, and multilingual names.
Ontology evolutionVersion schemas, deprecate types safely, validate allowed node-edge combinations, and migrate data explicitly. Design from competency questions—the questions and decisions the graph must support.
Temporal graphEffective-date entities, attributes, and relationships. Support point-in-time traversal, event nodes, supersession, and both valid-time and system-time queries.
Graph quality dimensionExample metric
Entity qualityDuplicate-entity rate and canonical-link precision/recall.
Relationship qualityEdge precision/recall, invalid relationship rate, and unsupported-assertion rate.
ProvenancePercentage of assertions with a resolvable source and source span.
Temporal integrityOverlapping, impossible, or missing validity intervals.
Schema integrityInvalid node/edge combinations and deprecated-type usage.
CoveragePercentage of competency questions answerable with current graph data.
Retrieval utilityQuality lift over the non-graph baseline at acceptable latency and cost.
Query planning
Detect entities and intent → resolve entities → select entry points → apply path templates and temporal/ACL constraints → traverse with hop and fan-out budgets → rank paths → fetch source evidence → verify → pack evidence.
Confidence model
Separate extraction confidence, entity-resolution confidence, relationship confidence, source authority, path confidence, and answer confidence. A useful path score is bounded by its weakest edge, temporal validity, source authority, and entity-resolution quality.
Truth states
Represent true, false, unknown, and disputed states. “Not found in the graph” means absence of represented evidence—not proof that a relationship is false.
Security
Apply node-, edge-, tenant-, and path-level controls before traversal. Two visible entities can still have a sensitive relationship whose existence must not be inferred.
// Graph design ruleGraph size is not graph quality. Measure the graph by supported questions, provenance, entity resolution, temporal integrity, and retrieval lift—not node and edge counts.
Continue to GraphRAG Cost Model →
Continue toKnowledge-Grounded Agent Loops →
Production takeaway — Use graph databases when relationship traversal, path explanation, and entity connectivity are first-class requirements—not as a replacement for every retrieval system.
Continue to Knowledge-Grounded Loops →
// Knowledge-Grounded Agent Loops · v3.36

3.5Knowledge-Grounded Agent Loops

Canonical treatmentEvidence-driven planning, graph expansion, verification, knowledge-aware stopping, and gated write-back.

The agent loop, knowledge base, and knowledge graph should operate as one control system. Each iteration forms a knowledge need, selects the appropriate retrieval mode, evaluates evidence sufficiency, and either answers, verifies, expands, replans, or stops.

Goal → plan
Decompose the request into decisions, claims, subquestions, and required evidence. Attach temporal, authority, permission, and confidence requirements.
Form knowledge need
Choose lexical search for identifiers, hybrid retrieval for concepts, SQL for structured facts and aggregation, graph traversal for relationships, or a composed plan across several retrievers.
Retrieve & evaluate
Assess evidence coverage, authority, freshness, contradiction, temporal validity, source diversity, provenance, and novelty—not merely similarity score.
Control transition
Sufficient → answer or act. Conflicting → verify. Incomplete → reformulate. Relationship gap → graph-expand. Stale → seek a fresher source. Unauthorized → redact or escalate.
Persist & stop
Store the evidence pack, retrieval trace, claim-to-source map, and stop reason. Do not write unverified working hypotheses into durable knowledge as facts.
Knowledge-aware stop conditionMeaning
Claim coverage satisfiedEvery material claim has sufficient supporting evidence or is explicitly labeled as uncertain.
Authority threshold metRequired primary or approved sources have been found and validated.
Contradictions resolvedConflicting evidence is reconciled, scoped by time/context, or disclosed to the user.
Temporal coverage satisfiedEvidence is valid for the requested point or interval in time.
Graph path groundedMaterial relationships are backed by source assertions, not traversal alone.
No novel evidenceRepeated retrieval produces duplicate evidence and expected information gain falls below cost.
Permission boundary reachedThe remaining evidence cannot be accessed under the current identity or tenant scope.
Evidence packReturn claim-to-source mappings, source versions, relevant spans, trust/freshness signals, unresolved contradictions, temporal scope, and retrieval method with the model context.
Novelty-aware retrieval budgetCharge each expansion against a retrieval budget and stop when new calls return substantially overlapping sources, entities, paths, or claims.
Knowledge write gateBefore promoting output into durable memory or the knowledge base, validate provenance, schema, confidence, temporal scope, duplication, permissions, and human-review policy.
End-to-end evaluationMeasure task success, claim support, retrieval recall, path validity, iteration efficiency, repeated-action rate, escalation quality, cost, and answer calibration together.
Default architecture: canonical knowledge store → derived lexical/vector/graph/SQL serving layers → retrieval planner → evidence evaluator → bounded agent loop → verified answer/action → gated knowledge write-back.
Continue toFrom Prompt Engineering to Agent Systems Engineering →
Production takeaway — Ground every reasoning loop in retrievable evidence, preserve citations through synthesis, and stop or escalate when evidence quality is insufficient.
Continue to Context, Cache, State & Memory →
Context, Cache, State & Memory Engineering
Canonical treatment of model-side caches, application key-value state, durable memory, context assembly, harness controls, and multi-step loop behavior.
Canonical treatment Cache accelerates computation. State preserves execution. Memory preserves useful experience. Knowledge preserves external truth. Context selects what the model sees. The harness governs the runtime. The loop governs behavior over time.

3.6Agent Systems Engineering

The evolution from prompts to context, harness, loop, and operational engineering.

The ecosystem is expanding from optimizing one model call to engineering the full production trajectory. These disciplines are nested rather than mutually exclusive.

Prompt EngineeringDesign instructions, examples, constraints, roles, and output schemas for one model call.
Context EngineeringSelect, rank, compress, order, and govern everything the model can see for the current call.
Harness EngineeringBuild the runtime around the model: tools, policy, state, routing, sandboxing, observability, budgets, and fallbacks.
Loop EngineeringControl repeated planning, acting, observing, verifying, retrying, replanning, escalating, and stopping.
Agent Systems EngineeringOperate the whole system safely: reliability, cost, tenancy, evaluation, governance, deployment, and incident response.
Cognition planePrompts · examples · context assembly · planning · reasoning · response synthesis
Control planeHarness · policy · model routing · tool eligibility · loop budgets · HITL · verifier · release gates
State & memory planeRedis · DynamoDB · checkpoints · Mem0 · AgentCore Memory · summaries · user and agent memory
Knowledge & data planeOpenSearch · pgvector · S3 Vectors · Neptune · S3 · PostgreSQL · governed external truth

3.6.1Cache Layers

Transformer KV cache, prefix caching, and application key-value stores.

Model runtimeTokens are converted into attention keys and values. The transformer KV cache avoids recomputing prior token states during generation. Provider prefix or prompt caches reuse stable encoded prompt segments. These are computational optimizations, not durable agent memory.
→
Application runtimeRedis and DynamoDB store named, queryable business and execution state. A context builder retrieves that state, applies permissions and ranking, and serializes selected information back into model-visible tokens.
ConceptLives whereStoresLifetimePrimary purpose
Transformer KV cacheModel runtime / accelerator memoryAttention keys and valuesGeneration or serving sessionReduce token-generation recomputation
Prompt / prefix cacheModel provider or inference runtimeReusable encoded prompt prefixesProvider-defined minutes or hoursReduce repeated input latency and cost
Deterministic application cacheRedis or application cacheExact request → response or tool resultSeconds to hoursAvoid identical work
Semantic cacheRedis with vector similaritySimilar query, embedding, response, metadataSeconds to daysReuse semantically equivalent work
Durable KV / document stateDynamoDBSession, checkpoint, idempotency, approval, run metadataHours to yearsPreserve workflow continuity

3.6.2Cache, State & Memory Hierarchy

Layer
Recommended role
Durability
Latency
Model KV cache
Token-generation acceleration
Ephemeral
Lowest
Prompt / prefix cache
Stable system prompt, tool schemas, policy, examples
Short-lived
Very low
In-process working state
Current plan, scratch variables, intermediate calculations
Ephemeral
Very low
Redis hot plane
Sessions, locks, rate limits, recent state, semantic and tool-result caches
Reconstructable
Sub-millisecond to low milliseconds
DynamoDB durable plane
Run checkpoints, idempotency, approvals, retry state, compact profiles
Durable
Low milliseconds
Mem0 / AgentCore Memory
Durable user, agent, semantic, episodic, and preference memory
Durable and governed
Retrieval path
Knowledge stores
External truth, documents, vectors, relationships, graph evidence
Durable
Query-dependent
S3 / relational archive
Immutable traces, artifacts, audit, analytics, reprocessing
Highest
Highest

3.6.3Redis & DynamoDB

Hot coordination and durable execution state for agentic systems.

Redis — hot coordination and cache planeUse for active session state, recent context, semantic response cache, tool-result cache, distributed rate limits, locks, leases, deduplication, temporary approval state, and worker coordination. Treat critical records as reconstructable or backed by a durable system. Redis Pub/Sub is suitable for ephemeral notifications, not an authoritative replayable event log.
DynamoDB — durable operational state planeUse for conversation and session records, workflow checkpoints, run metadata, idempotency keys, approval state, retry counters, plan versions, actor/session indexes, compact memory metadata, and event envelopes. Use conditional writes for concurrency and TTL for eventual expiry—not exact-time scheduling.
TogetherRead hot state and caches from Redis; fall back to DynamoDB for authoritative workflow continuity. Archive large artifacts to S3, report from PostgreSQL or Redshift, retrieve knowledge from OpenSearch or pgvector, and expand governed relationships in Neptune.
RequirementPreferred defaultWhy
Hot session state, locks, rate limitsRedisFast, TTL-aware, coordination-friendly
Semantic response or tool-result cacheRedisSimilarity search plus cache eviction and metadata
Durable run checkpoint and retry stateDynamoDBServerless durability, conditional writes, partitioned access
Idempotency and approval recordDynamoDBAuthoritative, durable, easy keyed lookup
Large artifacts and immutable tracesS3Low-cost durable objects and lifecycle management
Semantic knowledgeOpenSearch / pgvector / S3 VectorsRetrieval and similarity search
Graph relationshipsAmazon NeptuneGoverned traversals and GraphRAG evidence

3.6.4Expanded Memory Taxonomy

Working memoryCurrent goal, plan, selected evidence, unresolved questions, and intermediate tool outputs.In-process · LangGraph state · Redis
Short-term / session memoryConversation summary, active constraints, recent failures, and task progress across turns.Redis · DynamoDB · checkpoints · AgentCore short-term memory
Long-term semantic memoryStable facts, preferences, recurring entities, conventions, and durable summaries.Mem0 · AgentCore Memory · vector + structured metadata
Episodic memorySituation → goal → actions → observations → outcome → evaluation → lesson.DynamoDB/PostgreSQL metadata · S3 traces · vector/Neptune index
Procedural memoryReusable runbooks, tool-use recipes, successful workflows, policies, and skills.Skills repo · prompt assets · code · workflow registry
Prospective memoryThings the agent must do later: scheduled checks, pending approvals, retries, obligations, and deadlines.Durable workflow engine · DynamoDB · scheduler/event bus

3.6.5Mem0 & the Memory Lifecycle

Mem0 should be treated as memory middleware: it extracts, scopes, reconciles, stores, retrieves, and updates durable memories rather than acting as a generic key-value database.

1 · ObserveConversation, event, tool result, or completed episode.
2 · ExtractIdentify candidate fact, preference, episode, instruction, or relationship.
3 · ValidateCheck usefulness, sensitivity, authorization, provenance, confidence, and scope.
4 · ReconcileAdd, update, merge, supersede, ignore, or request confirmation when memories conflict.
5 · Retrieve & evaluateRank by relevance, recency, authority, and permission; inject into context; measure usefulness.
Durable?If not useful beyond the active task, keep it in working or session state.
Allowed?Redact, require consent, restrict scope, or reject sensitive and unauthorized content.
Consistent?Resolve contradictions, preserve provenance, and avoid silently overwriting uncertain facts.
Governed?Attach user/agent/tenant scope, confidence, source, TTL, retention class, and deletion path.
Memory metricWhat it detectsExample gate
Write precisionHow often stored memories were worth retainingReject noisy extraction strategies
Retrieval precision / recallWhether the right memories appear without flooding contextPer-task relevance threshold
Contradiction and stale-memory rateConflicting or outdated personalizationReconciliation or decay workflow
Unauthorized recall rateCross-tenant or policy-violating memory exposureMust remain zero
Memory usefulnessDownstream task improvement attributable to memoryCompare against no-memory baseline
User correction frequencyHow often users must correct remembered factsTrigger review of write policy
AWS-first default: provider KV/prefix caching for model compute; ElastiCache for Redis for hot state and cache; DynamoDB for durable operational state; AgentCore Memory for managed short- and long-term memory; Mem0 when portable memory middleware or custom extraction/reconciliation behavior is preferred; OpenSearch/pgvector/S3 Vectors for semantic knowledge; Neptune for graph knowledge; S3 for immutable trace and artifact history.
Continue toContext Engineering · Production Discipline →
Production takeaway — Memory requires explicit write, retrieve, update, expire, and delete policies; persistence without lifecycle governance becomes a reliability and privacy risk.
Continue to Context Engineering →
Context Assembly Architecture
Treat context as a governed runtime product, not a single prompt string.
1 · CollectPolicy, user request, task state, memory, retrieved evidence, tool schemas, and environment state.
2 · FilterApply tenant, security, freshness, relevance, and provenance constraints.
3 · PrioritizeReserve budget for policy and active task state before optional history or memory.
4 · CompressSummarize with citations, preserve unresolved facts, and detect summary drift.
5 · TraceRecord what entered the model, why it was selected, and which version assembled it.
Context sourceDefault treatmentPrimary failure mode
System policyFixed, highest precedence, versionedInstruction dilution or conflicting policy
Task and workflow stateHigh priority, structured, checkpointedLoss of progress or duplicated work
Retrieved evidenceAdaptive budget with provenanceNoise, stale evidence, or unsupported claims
Conversation historySelective and compressedOld assumptions dominate the current task
Tool schemasExpose only eligible toolsTool confusion and excess token cost
MemoryPermissioned, confidence-scored, revocableContamination or inappropriate personalization
Measure context quality: relevance, provenance coverage, contradiction rate, token efficiency, stale-context rate, and downstream task success.

3.7Context Engineering

Production discipline for assembling, budgeting, validating, and observing context.

Context engineering governs the complete model-visible working set for each invocation. It decides what enters the context window, in what order, at what fidelity, under which permissions, and within which token and latency budgets.

Token-budget allocationReserve explicit budgets for policy, user intent, active state, retrieved evidence, memories, tool schemas, examples, and response space. Do not let conversation history consume the full window by default.
Evidence orderingPlace authoritative instructions first, task-critical evidence near the question, and lower-confidence context later. Repeat only the smallest critical constraints when long contexts risk attention dilution.
Compression and summary driftSummaries must retain decisions, unresolved constraints, provenance, and uncertainty. Evaluate whether repeated summarization mutates facts or silently removes obligations.
Permission-aware assemblyFilter documents, graph edges, memories, and tool descriptions before ranking or generation. A model should never receive context the principal is not authorized to view.
Conflict resolutionDetect contradictions across user statements, memory, retrieved sources, system policy, and current state. Prefer higher-authority and fresher evidence; surface unresolved conflicts explicitly.
Adaptive contextChange retrieval depth, examples, tool schemas, and compression by task risk, model capability, latency budget, and current confidence rather than using one static prompt envelope.
Context metricWhat it measuresFailure signalProduction response
Context utilizationShare of supplied context actually cited, used, or reflected in decisionsLarge unused context with rising costReduce low-value sources and tool schemas
Evidence coverageRequired facts present in the assembled contextCorrect source exists but was not includedImprove retrieval, ranking, or budget allocation
Instruction retentionCritical policies followed across long contextsLate-context evidence overrides policyReorder, restate, or enforce outside the prompt
Conflict rateContradictory facts or instructions entering one callUnstable answers across repeated runsAdd authority, recency, and reconciliation rules
Compression lossRequired facts lost during summarizationPlans forget constraints or open obligationsUse structured summaries and regression tests
Continue toState Placement Decision Tree →
Production takeaway — Treat context as a governed runtime resource: select, compress, prioritize, and validate it instead of simply increasing token volume.
Continue to State Placement Decision Tree →

3.8State Placement Decision Tree

Choose a store by access pattern, durability, query model, and governance—not by familiarity. A single request may use several stores because hot state, durable state, memory, knowledge, graphs, and audit records have different contracts.

Must it survive process failure?
Yes: DynamoDB, PostgreSQL, S3, memory service, vector store, or Neptune.
No: process memory or Redis.
Is access primarily by exact key?
Hot/transient: Redis.
Durable/serverless: DynamoDB.
Does it require transactions or joins?
Yes: PostgreSQL.
No: continue by retrieval pattern.
Is it a large immutable artifact?
Yes: S3, with metadata indexed elsewhere.
Is retrieval semantic?
Documents: OpenSearch, pgvector, or S3 Vectors.
Personal memory: AgentCore Memory or Mem0.
Are relationships and paths first-class?
Operational graph: Neptune Database.
Algorithms: Neptune Analytics.
Is it a future obligation?
Yes: durable workflow or scheduler plus DynamoDB/PostgreSQL state—not memory alone.
Is it an audit record?
Yes: append-only durable storage with immutable retention and searchable metadata.
Continue toFive recommended starting stacks →
Production takeaway — Place state according to durability, latency, consistency, and query needs; avoid using one store for every transient, operational, and analytical workload.

Context & Prompt Engineering

The highest-leverage layer. The model layer serves the context; the context layer determines everything else. Most production failures originate here.
System prompt anatomy
1 · Identity & role
Who the agent is, what it is responsible for, and what it must never do. Written once and version-controlled. Should be stable across releases.
2 · Task instructions
Exactly what the model should do with a user request. Includes output format (JSON schema, XML tags, markdown), step-by-step reasoning instructions, and negative examples.
3 · Tool manifest
Which tools are available, when to use each, and what valid invocation looks like. Describe edge cases and ambiguous overlap explicitly.
4 · Dynamic context slot
Retrieved documents, user profile, session state, and runtime data injected here. Always labeled. Never mixed with role text.
5 · Few-shot examples
Concrete input-to-output pairs showing desired behavior. Most effective for structured output, edge cases, and tone calibration. Curate from real production traces.
6 · Guardrail rules
Topics to avoid, escalation triggers, refusal language. Phrased as positive obligations ("always cite sources"). Audit when safety metrics worsen.
// ML/DS insight · in-context learningFew-shot examples exploit in-context learning (ICL): gradient-free meta-learning that temporarily biases the model's output distribution at inference time. Unlike fine-tuning (which updates weights), ICL requires no training — the model generalises from examples in its context window alone. Quality matters more than quantity: 3 carefully chosen, diverse, correctly-labelled examples consistently outperform 20 noisy ones. Curate examples from production traces where the model's behaviour was ideal, not from synthetic or hand-written cases — real query distributions produce better generalisation. ICL is also fragile to example order; if results vary, average over a few orderings or place the most representative example last.
Context window budgeting
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
SlotTypical allocationManagement rule
System prompt (static)2-8k tokensVersion-controlled in git. Changes require eval gate before deploy.
Retrieved context (RAG)20-60k tokensBudget per retrieval call. Drop low-scoring chunks if window is tight.
Conversation history10-30k tokensSummarize old turns beyond a threshold. Never truncate silently.
Tool call + result history5-20k tokensKeep last N turns of tool use. Compress intermediate reasoning if needed.
User message1-4k tokensEnforce max-length at the API gateway. Reject or chunk oversized inputs.
Output reservation4-8k tokensAlways reserve headroom for the response. Never fill the window to the limit.
Key rule: context window is not a bucket you fill — it is a budget you allocate. Measure token usage per slot in every production trace. When retrieval context routinely exceeds its budget, fix the retrieval strategy, not the window size.
// ML/DS insight · context as feature budgetThe context window is the agentic equivalent of a real-time feature budget in online ML. In ML serving, you can't compute expensive features in the critical path — you must prioritise the most predictive ones within a latency budget. The context window imposes the same constraint: you can't inject all available information, so you must select. The "dynamic context slot" for retrieved documents is your real-time feature store serving features at inference time. The retrieval strategy determines which features (chunks) are selected — exactly the feature selection problem in online ML. Techniques that transfer directly: entropy-based relevance scoring for chunk selection, compression of redundant features (duplicate retrieval results), and lazy loading (retrieve only when confidence is below threshold).
Prompt versioning & drift management
Store prompts in git
Treat system prompts as code. Every change goes through PR review with an eval run attached. Tag releases with semantic versions. Never edit prompts in a UI without a git-backed audit trail.
Pin prompts per environment
Dev, staging, and prod each reference a specific prompt version. Promotion is gated on passing evals. A model version bump may require a prompt rewrite even without changing the wording.
Track behavioral drift
Run the same golden-set eval weekly even if nothing changed. Models on the same version string can still drift subtly. Catch regressions before users do.
Structured output enforcement
Require JSON mode or constrained generation for any downstream-parsed output. Define the schema in the prompt with a concrete example. Validate every response against the schema at the harness layer.
Context engineering decision guide
SituationTechniqueWatch out for
Model ignores instructionsMove critical rules to top of system prompt. Add a negative example. Use XML tags to separate sections.Instruction conflict — two rules that contradict under edge cases.
Output format is inconsistentAdd a JSON schema with a filled-in example. Enable JSON mode. Add output validation and retry loop.Schema too rigid — model hallucinating to fit the schema.
Agent misuses toolsAdd a tool-selection rubric. Include a negative example of a wrong tool choice. Make tool descriptions shorter and more distinctive.Tool description overlap — model cannot distinguish when each applies.
Retrieval context is ignoredInject context immediately before the user question. Use explicit labels: "CONTEXT:" and "QUESTION:". Instruct the model to cite its source.Context-question distance — too many tokens between context and the instruction to use it.
Responses too verboseAdd explicit length constraints. Include a concise example. Measure response length in evals.Model truncating important information to hit length target.
Part IV

Product & Solution Patterns

Apply the architecture to chatbots, assistants, research systems, coding agents, workflow automation, and other product forms.
In this part
  • 4.1Chatbot Stack
  • 4.2Foundational AI Product Patterns

4.1Chatbot Stack

Most of this stack is overkill for a chatbot. Here's what you actually need, and when each layer earns its place.
// Minimal production chatbot stack
Claude Agent SDK / API
always
CLAUDE.md
always
Guardrails AI
always · production
MCP Tools
if it does things
RAG — OpenSearch or pgvector
if it knows things
Mem0
if it remembers users
PostgreSQL + Redis
always
FastAPI
production API gateway
Langfuse
production
// How chatbots evolve through phases
Phase 1 — Basic chatbot
Claude API + CLAUDE.md + PostgreSQL for history + Guardrails AI for I/O safety. That's it. No RAG, no memory, no tools. Answers from model knowledge only. Guardrails AI belongs here from day one — not phase 3.
Phase 2 — It needs to know things
Add RAG + OpenSearch or pgvector. Ground answers in your actual documents. Add an embeddings pipeline for complex PDFs. Add Langfuse for production tracing. This is where most production chatbots end up.
Phase 3 — It needs to do things
Add MCP tools (APIs, DB queries, write actions). Add hooks for validation and custom logging. Add OPA if different user roles need different tool access. Add HITL interrupt if the chatbot takes write actions requiring human approval.
Phase 4 — It needs to remember users
Add Mem0 for cross-session user memory and learned preferences. Add Bedrock Guardrails for managed content safety (topic denial, PII redaction, grounding checks) if on AWS. Add MongoDB / DocumentDB if conversation history is high-volume or variable-schema. Add Grafana for ops dashboards once at scale.
Phase 5 — Multiple specialised chatbots
Add LangGraph for routing between specialists (billing bot, support bot, sales bot). Add LangGraph Checkpointer for long-running multi-turn task continuity. Add LangSmith if running a pure LangGraph stack. At this point it's no longer a chatbot — it's a multi-agent system with a conversational interface.
// Chatbot — what to use vs. skip
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
Stack ComponentUse for chatbot?When / Why
Guardrails AIProductionInput/output safety — PII detection, off-topic blocking, prompt injection prevention
Bedrock GuardrailsProduction (AWS)Managed content safety — topic denial, PII redaction, grounding checks. Pairs with Guardrails AI for custom validation.
OPAEnterprise onlyRBAC/entitlements — when different user roles need different agent capabilities
Claude Agent SDK / APIAlwaysThe model call itself
Streamlit / Next.jsAlwaysThe interface your users interact with
OpenWebUIIf self-hostingPre-built chat UI — skip building one from scratch
FastAPIPreferredAsync API gateway — native streaming for chatbot responses
FlaskOftenSimpler API gateway if async isn't needed
AG-UI ProtocolProductionStructured streaming of agent events to frontend
A2UI ProtocolSituationalIf the chatbot needs to render rich interactive components (charts, forms, maps) not just text
CLAUDE.mdAlwaysPersona, tone, rules, guardrails
MCP ToolsIf it actsAPIs, DB queries, external actions via typed protocol
PostgreSQLAlwaysConversation history, user profiles, audit logs
RedisAlwaysSession state, rate limiting, response caching
RAG + OpenSearch / pgvectorAlmost alwaysOnce you need answers grounded in your docs
Mem0If it personalisesCross-session user memory, learned preferences
LangfuseProductionTraces, evals, cost tracking, regression detection
GrafanaSituationalLatency / throughput dashboards once at scale
SplunkEnterprise onlyIf IT/security teams require centralised log aggregation
HooksSituationalInput/output validation, guardrails, custom logging
LangGraphSituationalOnly if routing between multiple specialist bots
LangGraph CheckpointerSituationalOnly if conversations involve long-running tasks
Amazon NeptuneRarelyOnly if knowledge is highly relational (compliance, org charts)
S3 VectorsRarelyHigh-volume vector storage — overkill for most chatbots
MLflowNoModel experiment tracking — not relevant for chatbot deployment
Ralph LoopNoChatbots are turn-based — no keep-going loop needed
ReAct / Plan–Execute–ReflectNoAutonomous loop patterns — chatbots respond once per turn
HITLSituationalOnly if chatbot takes write actions requiring human approval
Git WorktreesNoParallel agent coding runs — irrelevant for chatbots
GitHub ActionsNoCI/CD for code — chatbots don't write code
Multi-agent orchestrationNoChatbots are single-agent by nature
Codex / Claude CodeNoCoding agents — wrong category for a chatbot
Cursor / CopilotNoIDE harnesses — for developers building the chatbot, not the chatbot itself
AirflowNoScheduled pipeline orchestration — chatbots are user-triggered
TemporalNoDurable execution runtime — long-running workflows, not conversational
PromptFooProductionRun eval suite on chatbot prompts in CI — catch regressions before deploy
DeepEvalIf using RAGValidate RAG faithfulness — are answers grounded in retrieved docs?
RAGASIf using RAGBenchmark retrieval quality and answer quality across datasets with context precision, recall, faithfulness, and relevancy
A2A ProtocolNoCross-ecosystem agent delegation — chatbots are single-agent

4.2Foundational AI Product Patterns

// Chatbots — types of chatbot patterns
💬

4.2.1 Types of Chatbot Patterns

UX pattern · agent boundary · escalation model
A chatbot is a conversational interface, not necessarily an agent. The chat surface captures intent, streams responses, shows citations, renders tool calls, accepts files, and asks for approval when actions are risky. The backend can be as simple as one model call or as complex as a multi-agent workflow with memory, retrieval, tools, policies, evaluation, and human review.
Core distinction
Chatbot
A user-facing conversation UI. It may only send messages to a model and return text. Its main job is interaction design: turn-taking, history, streaming, upload controls, and feedback.
Assistant
A chatbot with a defined persona, domain, instructions, memory rules, and safety boundaries. It usually supports retrieval, citations, and structured responses.
Agentic Chatbot
A conversational wrapper around an action system. It can plan, call tools, inspect results, ask for approval, update state, and continue work across turns.
Pattern catalog
1. Simple FAQ Chatbot
Model + prompt + static answers
What it does: Answers common questions from a defined domain using instructions and examples. It does not take action, retrieve deeply, or maintain meaningful state beyond the current chat.

Best for: basic support, policy summaries, onboarding, product FAQs, and internal help desks.

Stack shape: UI / App Layer → API gateway → Model Layer. Optional conversation history in PostgreSQL.

Watch out for: hallucinated answers if the prompt is not grounded; stale content if the source material changes.
2. RAG Chatbot
Chat + retrieval + citations
What it does: Retrieves relevant chunks from documents, databases, or knowledge bases, then generates an answer grounded in those sources. It should show citations, source snippets, and confidence boundaries.

Best for: enterprise knowledge search, policy lookup, support docs, technical documentation, sales enablement, and compliance Q&A.

Stack shape: UI → API gateway → retrieval layer → model. Common components: OpenSearch, pgvector, S3 Vectors, Textract, Bedrock Data Automation.

Watch out for: stale indexes, missing document permissions, poor chunking, weak citation UX, and answers that overstate evidence.
3. Tool-Using Chatbot
Chat + APIs + typed tools
What it does: Calls external systems to answer or act: search tickets, look up orders, query databases, check calendars, create drafts, open issues, or fetch account data. MCP often belongs here because it standardizes the agent ↔ tool boundary.

Best for: operational assistants, internal copilots, customer support agents, analytics assistants, and IT helpdesk bots.

Stack shape: UI → runtime/harness → MCP/tool gateway → business systems. Add auth, scopes, rate limits, tool allowlists, and audit logs.

Watch out for: overbroad permissions, unsafe write actions, tool-call loops, leaking private data, and unclear tool-call explanations.
4. Workflow Chatbot
Chat-triggered process automation
What it does: Converts user intent into a structured workflow: collect inputs, validate them, trigger a process, monitor status, and return updates. The workflow is usually deterministic even if the conversation is natural language.

Best for: onboarding, request intake, report generation, ticket creation, data refreshes, procurement, approvals, and employee-service workflows.

Stack shape: UI → API gateway → orchestrator or workflow engine → tools/systems. Airflow, Temporal, or LangGraph can own the workflow depending on durability needs.

Watch out for: ambiguous requests, partial inputs, duplicate submissions, long-running jobs, and status visibility.
5. Agentic Chatbot
Plan → act → observe → revise
What it does: Plans multi-step work, chooses tools, observes results, revises its approach, and may continue across turns. This is where a chatbot becomes the interface to an agent loop rather than merely a response generator.

Best for: research assistants, coding copilots, complex troubleshooting, multi-source analysis, report drafting, and investigative workflows.

Stack shape: UI → runtime/harness → loop control → tools/retrieval/state → model. Add LangGraph or another harness when branching, memory, interrupts, and checkpoints matter.

Watch out for: runaway loops, excessive tool use, unclear progress, brittle plans, and actions taken without user approval.
6. Human-in-the-Loop Chatbot
Chat + approval gates + escalation
What it does: Pauses at sensitive moments and asks a human to approve, edit, deny, or redirect the next step. The human may be the end user, a manager, support rep, compliance reviewer, or engineer.

Best for: refunds, account changes, production writes, customer messages, compliance decisions, legal/HR workflows, and high-impact recommendations.

Stack shape: Agent loop + interrupt mechanism + approval UI + audit log. Security and governance should define which actions require approval.

Watch out for: vague approval prompts. The user must see what will happen, why, what data is involved, and how to reject or undo it.
7. Personalized Memory Chatbot
Cross-session memory + preferences
What it does: Remembers stable user preferences, prior decisions, recurring context, and long-lived facts. Memory is curated, scoped, and retrievable context, not just raw chat history.

Best for: personal assistants, account managers, learning tutors, developer copilots, and recurring enterprise workflows.

Stack shape: UI → memory layer → model/runtime. Common components: Mem0, PostgreSQL, DocumentDB/MongoDB, and user profile stores.

Watch out for: privacy, incorrect memories, consent, deletion, tenant isolation, and the difference between “remember this” and “use this only now.”
8. Multimodal Chatbot
Text + files + images + structured outputs
What it does: Accepts and produces more than text: document uploads, screenshots, images, audio, charts, forms, tables, and generated UI components. It often pairs document processing with structured extraction and retrieval.

Best for: document review, claims processing, analytics assistants, visual troubleshooting, form completion, research synthesis, and image-aware support bots.

Stack shape: UI upload controls → ingestion/processing → multimodal model → storage/retrieval → response renderer.

Watch out for: file permissions, OCR/extraction errors, large context costs, unsupported file types, and presenting uncertain visual interpretations as facts.
9. Multi-Specialist Chatbot
Router + specialist agents
What it does: Routes each user request to a specialist: billing, support, legal, analytics, coding, research, or policy. The chat UI appears unified, but the backend may delegate to multiple expert prompts, tools, or agents.

Best for: enterprise copilots, broad internal assistants, customer-service platforms, and multi-domain knowledge systems.

Stack shape: UI → router/orchestrator → specialist agents/tools → shared memory/retrieval/governance. LangGraph supervisor-style patterns and A2A-style boundaries can become relevant.

Watch out for: routing mistakes, inconsistent tone, duplicated memory, unclear ownership of final answers, and handoff loops between specialists.
10. Embedded Copilot Chatbot
Chat inside an existing product
What it does: Lives inside a workflow the user already uses: CRM, analytics dashboard, IDE, admin console, support tool, or data app. It has contextual awareness of the page, selected object, current record, and user permissions.

Best for: “ask about this page,” “summarize this account,” “draft a reply,” “explain this chart,” “fix this config,” and “take action on this record.”

Stack shape: product UI → embedded chat panel → context provider → API/runtime → tools. UI protocols matter because the model may need to render cards, forms, actions, and progress updates.

Watch out for: context leakage between users, stale page state, confusing action ownership, and permission mismatches between the product and backend.
How the patterns evolve
Phase 1
