Simple assistant: prompt, model, chat history.
Phase 2
Grounded assistant: retrieval, citations, document lifecycle.
Phase 3
Action assistant: tools, MCP, auth scopes, audit logs.
Phase 4
Agentic assistant: planning, loops, checkpoints, approvals.
Phase 5
System interface: specialists, orchestration, governance, evals.
Design checklist
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
QuestionWhy it mattersLikely layer touched
Does the chatbot only answer, or can it act?Action requires tools, permissions, audit logs, and often approval gates.Runtime · MCP · Security
Does it need private or enterprise knowledge?Grounding requires retrieval, ingestion, citations, and document permissions.Knowledge & Retrieval · Ingestion
Does it need to remember users across sessions?Memory changes privacy, deletion, consent, and state architecture.Memory · State & Persistence
Does it perform long-running tasks?Long tasks need checkpoints, status updates, retries, and resumability.Loop Control · Orchestration · State
Can it change production systems or send messages externally?Write actions need human review, RBAC, policy checks, and rollback paths.Security · Governance · HITL
How will quality be measured?Chatbots degrade without regression tests, trace review, and user feedback loops.Evaluation · Observability
What happens when retrieval fails or confidence is low?The bot needs fallback behavior: ask clarifying questions, escalate, or refuse to answer.Runtime · UX · Guardrails
Who owns the final answer?Multi-agent or multi-specialist bots need clear routing and final-response authority.Orchestration · A2A · Runtime
🤖

4.2.2 AI Agents

plan · act · observe · complete tasks
What it is: An AI agent is a product pattern where the system does more than answer a question. It receives a goal, reasons about what needs to happen, chooses tools or subagents, executes steps, observes the results, and continues until the task is complete or needs escalation. In product terms, an agent is a task-completion interface rather than a response-generation interface.

Core distinction: a chatbot primarily manages conversation; an agent manages work. The user may still interact through chat, but the product promise is completion: “research this,” “fix this bug,” “prepare this report,” “triage these tickets,” “update this record,” or “monitor this process and notify me when something changes.”
Common agent product types
Task Agent
Completes bounded jobs such as generating a report, filing a ticket, creating a summary, or updating a CRM record. Best when the task has a clear beginning, end, and success condition.
Research Agent
Searches, reads, compares, cites, and synthesizes across sources. Best for market maps, competitive analysis, technical research, policy review, and due diligence.
Coding Agent
Reads a codebase, edits files, runs tests, observes failures, and opens a pull request. Requires sandboxing, version control, CI feedback, and review gates.
Data Agent
Queries warehouses, transforms datasets, generates charts, explains metrics, and investigates anomalies. Needs strong permissioning, semantic-layer awareness, and query validation.
Browser Agent
Navigates web or internal apps, fills forms, extracts information, and completes workflows. Needs browser sandboxing, careful auth handling, and safeguards against unintended actions.
Operations Agent
Monitors systems, triages incidents, drafts remediation steps, or executes approved runbooks. Needs audit logs, human approvals, rollback paths, and escalation policies.
Architecture mapping
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
Agent capabilityWhat the product needsMap layer
PlanningBreaks a goal into steps, dependencies, and checkpoints before acting.Loop Control · Orchestration
Tool useCalls APIs, databases, browsers, files, MCP servers, or internal services.Harness / Runtime · MCP Protocol
ObservationReads tool outputs, test results, traces, retrieved documents, or UI state.Runtime · Observability · Retrieval
StateMaintains progress across long-running tasks, retries, and interruptions.State & Persistence · Memory
DelegationRoutes subtasks to specialist agents or workflow nodes.Orchestration · A2A Protocol
ControlUses approval gates, policies, sandboxing, and audit trails before risky actions.Security, Governance & Trust
QualityEvaluates task success with regression suites, human review, traces, and golden datasets.Eval & Testing · Observability
Good fit when…
The user wants an outcome, not just information; the task requires multiple steps; tool calls are necessary; the system can observe success or failure; and the workflow benefits from autonomy, retries, or delegation.
Watch out for…
Over-agentifying simple Q&A, hidden tool failures, runaway loops, unclear stop conditions, permission mismatches, context drift, weak audit trails, and letting agents write to production without human approval.
📚

4.2.3 RAG Knowledge Assistants

grounded answers · citations · enterprise knowledge
What it is: A RAG Knowledge Assistant answers questions using a controlled knowledge base instead of relying only on the model’s internal training data. The product value is trust: it retrieves relevant passages, grounds the answer, cites sources, respects document permissions, and explains uncertainty when the knowledge base does not contain enough evidence.

Core distinction: a general chatbot answers from conversation and model knowledge; a RAG assistant answers from a governed corpus. The best RAG products feel conversational, but their architecture is closer to search + retrieval + synthesis + citation management.
Common RAG product types
Internal Knowledge Assistant
Answers employee questions over handbooks, policies, onboarding docs, architecture docs, meeting notes, and project plans.
Customer Support Knowledge Bot
Grounds support responses in help-center articles, troubleshooting guides, account data, known issues, and escalation policies.
Technical Docs Assistant
Helps engineers understand APIs, SDKs, architecture decisions, runbooks, and migration guides with source-linked answers.
Policy / Compliance Assistant
Answers questions from policies, control libraries, legal guidance, security standards, and audit evidence with strict citation behavior.
Research Library Assistant
Searches and summarizes papers, reports, transcripts, filings, and long-form research collections.
Account / Case Knowledge Assistant
Grounds answers in customer-specific histories, tickets, contracts, CRM notes, support logs, and entitlement data.
RAG pipeline anatomy
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
StageWhat happensCommon components
IngestDocuments are pulled from files, websites, Drive, SharePoint, S3, databases, tickets, or application records.Connectors · ETL · web crawlers · S3
Chunk & enrichContent is split, labeled, deduplicated, permissioned, and connected to source metadata.Chunkers · metadata extractors · ACL sync
Embed / indexChunks are indexed for semantic, keyword, hybrid, or graph retrieval.pgvector · OpenSearch · S3 Vectors · Neptune
RetrieveThe system selects the most relevant evidence for a user question.Vector search · BM25 · hybrid search · rerankers
SynthesizeThe model produces an answer using retrieved evidence and source citations.Model Layer · prompt templates · citation renderer
EvaluateAnswers are tested for relevance, faithfulness, citation quality, and refusal behavior.RAGAS · DeepEval · PromptFoo · golden datasets
Critical design decisions
Choose corpus boundaries, update frequency, chunking strategy, retrieval mode, citation style, permission enforcement, fallback behavior, and whether the assistant can answer without evidence.
Quality signals
Track answer faithfulness, retrieval precision, citation usefulness, unsupported claims, no-answer accuracy, stale-source usage, and user feedback on whether the answer resolved the task.
Common failure modes
Bad chunks, stale documents, missing permissions, irrelevant retrieval, citation mismatch, overconfident synthesis, duplicated sources, and treating the top retrieved document as automatically authoritative.
🔎

4.2.4 AI Search / Answer Engines

search · rank · synthesize · compare
What it is: An AI Search / Answer Engine is a product pattern where the primary interaction is finding, ranking, comparing, and synthesizing information. It may look like a search box rather than a chat thread. The system retrieves from public web, private corpora, databases, or mixed sources, then produces a concise answer with supporting evidence.

Core distinction: RAG assistants are usually built around a known knowledge base; answer engines are built around discovery. They are optimized for query understanding, source selection, ranking, evidence comparison, and synthesis across many candidate sources.
Common answer-engine product types
Perplexity-Style Answer Engine
Searches broadly, summarizes current information, and presents cited answers with follow-up exploration.
Enterprise Answer Engine
Searches across internal docs, chat logs, tickets, wikis, dashboards, and databases with permission-aware results.
Research Search Engine
Compares papers, filings, reports, expert sources, or news, then synthesizes points of agreement and disagreement.
Semantic Search Product
Finds conceptually similar records or passages even when exact keywords do not match.
Multimodal Search
Searches across images, documents, charts, screenshots, audio, video transcripts, and structured records.
Decision Search
Finds prior decisions, rationales, tradeoffs, owners, and outcomes so teams can understand why something happened.
Answer-engine pipeline anatomy
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
StageWhat happensProduct behavior
Query understandingClassify intent, expand terms, detect entities, infer filters, and decide whether freshness matters.Turns vague questions into searchable queries.
Source selectionChoose public web, internal docs, vector stores, databases, APIs, or specialized indexes.Prevents searching everything blindly.
Retrieval & rankingCollect candidate results, rank them, rerank for relevance, and diversify sources.Shows the most useful evidence first.
Evidence synthesisSummarize, compare, reconcile conflicts, and identify what the sources actually support.Produces an answer, not just a result list.
Citation & provenanceAttach sources, snippets, dates, owners, and confidence indicators.Makes the answer inspectable and auditable.
Follow-up explorationSuggest refinements, related searches, filters, and drill-down paths.Turns search into an interactive research workflow.
How it differs from a chatbot
The UX is retrieval-first: query, results, facets, sources, citations, summaries, and drill-downs. Conversation may exist, but the product is optimized around finding and verifying information rather than maintaining a long dialogue.
Watch out for…
Stale results, source over-ranking, citation laundering, summarizing weak evidence, ignoring conflicting sources, leaking private documents in blended search, and failing to distinguish “not found” from “not true.”
Architecture path complete. You now have enough context to design the system. Continue into the Market Map only when selecting products, comparing implementations, or auditing ecosystem coverage.
Part V

Selection & Market Landscape

Choose architectures and products using decision criteria first, then inspect the fast-changing market and managed-platform options.
In this part
  • 5.1Decision Guides
  • 5.2Default Stack Recipes
  • 5.3Default Stack Architectures
  • 5.4Build-vs-Buy Matrix
  • 5.5Agent Maturity Model
  • 5.6Projects Catalog
  • 5.6Projects Catalog
  • 5.7AWS AgentCore
  • 5.8Platform Bill of Materials
// Start Here · Default Stack Recipes

Five Recommended Starting Stacks

Use these as first-pass defaults before browsing the full catalog. Each recipe favors the smallest stack that can reach production safely; add autonomy, memory, multi-agent orchestration, or custom protocols only after the launch gates are measurable.

Prototype chatbotFast internal demoStreamlit or Next.js, one hosted frontier model, basic prompt templates, lightweight tracing, and a small golden set.
  • Skip memory unless users return across sessions.
  • Keep tools read-only until evals exist.
Enterprise RAGKnowledge assistantNext.js or Streamlit, ACL-aware retrieval, citation-first responses, document ingestion pipeline, eval CI, and trace capture.
  • Prioritize corpus quality and freshness.
  • Measure citation accuracy before adding actions.
Tool-using agentBounded internal operatorLangGraph or a durable workflow runtime, typed tool schemas, approval gates, state checkpointing, and rollback-safe actions.
  • Start with narrow tasks and explicit stop conditions.
  • Use HITL for irreversible operations.
Regulated systemHigh-assurance assistantPrivate retrieval, tenant-scoped data access, PII controls, policy-as-code, immutable audit logs, red-team evals, and formal launch gates.
  • Default to least privilege.
  • Log decisions, sources, and user approvals.
AWS-native pathManaged platform routeBedrock-hosted models, managed runtime/memory where appropriate, identity integration, observability, guardrails, and AWS-native deployment controls.
  • Choose this when platform governance matters more than portability.
  • Document vendor assumptions and exit constraints.
Continue toInference Cache Operations →
Selection Framework

5.1Decision Guides

Opinionated comparisons, build-vs-buy guidance, and maturity framing for choosing a stack.
TL;DR
  • Buy commodity layers (retrieval, observability, evals, guardrails); build only for durable advantage.
  • Maturity model has 5 levels - never add autonomy until the previous level is observable and reversible.
  • Use-case decision tree first, then tool selection - never pick tools before knowing the product shape.

Use-Case Decision Tree

🧭Choose by product risk, not hypev3.06 entry layer
Do users mainly ask questions?
Use: RAG assistant stack. Avoid: autonomous agents until citation accuracy, corpus quality, and permission filtering are reliable.
Does the system take actions?
Use: tool schemas, policy gates, dry-run mode, HITL approvals, idempotency, and rollback handlers. Avoid: model-managed durable state.
Are there multiple teams or tenants?
Use: model gateway, RBAC, tenant-scoped retrieval, observability, budget controls, and release artifacts. Avoid: shared memory or shared vector indexes without ACL enforcement.
Is the workflow long-running?
Use: LangGraph, Temporal, Airflow, or AgentCore with checkpoints. Avoid: single request/response loops for workflows that need retries, compensation, or approvals.
Is the domain regulated?
Use: private data boundaries, policy-as-code, audit retention, red-team suites, eval gates, and human escalation. Avoid: broad memory capture and unreviewed write tools.
Is speed more important than portability?
Use: managed platform track. Avoid: over-customized infrastructure when a managed runtime, gateway, observability, and eval layer can cover the need.

Status Taxonomy

🏷️How to label projects and claimstool · pattern · protocol · service
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
LabelUse forDecision meaningAudit expectation
matureWidely adopted products, databases, frameworks, and operating patterns.Acceptable default for production if it fits requirements.Verify version, security posture, and licensing.
emergingPromising protocols, managed services, or agent frameworks still evolving quickly.Use behind abstraction layers where portability matters.Re-check capabilities, APIs, and ecosystem support each release cycle.
experimentalResearch patterns, novel multi-agent loops, and rapidly changing UX protocols.Prototype first; do not make it a hard dependency for regulated flows.Require eval evidence, fallback plan, and owner sign-off.
deprecated-riskTools with weak maintenance signals, unclear roadmap, or high replacement risk.Avoid as a core platform dependency unless there is a migration path.Document alternatives and exit criteria.
current-market claimModel names, platform capabilities, service bundles, and pricing-sensitive claims.Treat as time-sensitive.Source-link and date-stamp during formal review.
stable patternArchitecture guidance such as audit logs, eval CI, HITL, RBAC, and rollback.Durable recommendation.Validate implementation details, not the concept.

5.2Default Stack Recipes

🧭Opinionated starting pointschoose a lane before choosing tools
The stack map is intentionally broad. Use these recipes as default decisions when the product shape is known. Deviation should be justified by scale, compliance, latency, budget, or ecosystem constraints.
stable patternwatch for drift
Product shapeDefault stackUse whenAvoid when
MVP RAG assistantNext.js or Streamlit · FastAPI · Claude Sonnet/GPT-4o · pgvector/OpenSearch · Langfuse · PromptFoo · basic HITLInternal knowledge assistant, support copilot, policy Q&A, document search.You need autonomous write actions, strict tenant isolation, or regulated audit trails on day one.
Enterprise agent platformModel gateway · LangGraph/AgentCore · OPA/Guardrails · OpenSearch/Neptune · Redis/Postgres · Splunk/Grafana/Langfuse · eval CI/CDMultiple teams, multiple tools, RBAC, auditability, release gates, and production ownership.Single-use prototype or team without platform ownership capacity.
Internal coding/research agentClaude Code/Cursor/Copilot · MCP servers · GitHub Actions · sandboxed shell/tools · golden task suite · approval gatesDeveloper productivity, repo automation, research workflows, documentation generation.Production customer-facing actions or unrestricted access to secrets and deploy credentials.
Workflow automation agentLangGraph/Temporal · tool registry · idempotency keys · approval workflow · event log · rollback handlersMulti-step tasks with external side effects: tickets, CRM, finance ops, internal approvals.Open-ended chat where task boundaries and success criteria are unclear.
Regulated-domain assistantModel gateway · private retrieval · tenant-scoped embeddings · PII redaction · policy engine · immutable audit log · HITLHealthcare, finance, legal, HR, government, enterprise data access.You cannot fund compliance review, adversarial evals, and audit retention.

Recommendation Strength

✅How strongly to prefer each optiondefault · alternative · experimental · risk
Use recommendation strength as a decision overlay on top of the catalog. It does not replace fit-for-purpose evaluation; it helps teams distinguish safe defaults from promising but immature choices.
recommended defaultgood alternativeemerging / experimentalhigh-governance / use carefully
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
Layer / DecisionRecommended defaultGood alternativeExperimental / use carefullyWhy it matters
Prototype UIStreamlit or Next.jsOpenWebUI, CopilotKitFully generated UI without guardrailsPrototype speed matters, but the UI must not hide model uncertainty or unsafe actions.
Production app shellNext.js + FastAPIFlask, embedded CopilotKitMonolithic notebook app in productionProduction systems need auth, routing, tracing, typed APIs, and deploy discipline.
RAG retrievalOpenSearch / pgvectorManaged vector DB, S3 VectorsGraphRAG before simple retrieval worksCorpus quality, ACL filtering, and recall testing matter more than novelty.
Agent runtimeLangGraph / AgentCoreClaude Agent SDK, Deep AgentsAd hoc while-loops with tool accessDurable state, interrupts, rollback, and traceability become mandatory once actions matter.
Tool protocolMCP for tool accessTyped REST toolsUnvalidated shell/API accessTool schemas and auditability are the security boundary, not the prompt.
Agent-to-agentA2A when ecosystem interoperability is neededInternal event busMulti-agent routing before single-agent reliabilityCross-agent coordination adds failure modes; use it only when ownership boundaries require it.
ObservabilityLangfuse / LangSmith + Grafana/SplunkOpenTelemetry-first custom tracesOnly app logsPrompt/model/tool traces are required for debugging, audit, and regression analysis.
EvaluationPromptFoo + DeepEval + golden setsAgentEvals for trajectoriesManual spot checks as release criteriaEvery prompt, model, retrieval, or tool change needs repeatable regression gates.
Security / policyOPA + Guardrails + HITLBedrock Guardrails for AWS-native stacksPrompt-only safety rulesPolicy enforcement must sit outside the model for sensitive operations.
MemoryMem0 / managed memory after policy designShort-term session memory onlyPermanent memory by defaultMemory creates privacy, consent, retention, deletion, and correction requirements.
Scroll horizontally to compare →

5.3Default Stack Architectures

🗺️Reference diagrams by deployment shape5 default stacks
These diagrams are intentionally opinionated. They show the default path for common builds, not every possible integration. Move right only when the previous layer has ownership, tests, traces, and rollback.
Prototype assistant
UIStreamlit or Next.js demo shell
ModelClaude Sonnet / GPT-4o with simple system prompt
KnowledgeManual files or lightweight pgvector
ObservabilityLangfuse traces + basic feedback
GateNo write tools; manual review before sharing
Production RAG assistant
AppNext.js + FastAPI + SSO/RBAC
IngestionTextract/Data Automation + chunking pipeline
RetrievalOpenSearch/pgvector + ACL prefilter + rerank
EvalGolden Q&A + citation/faithfulness tests
OpsLangfuse/Splunk + stale-source alerts
Workflow automation agent
TriggerTicket, webhook, schedule, or user request
RuntimeLangGraph/Temporal with durable state
ToolsMCP/typed REST tools with scoped permissions
Human gateApproval for external writes or high-risk changes
RecoveryIdempotency keys, rollback handlers, audit log
AWS-native enterprise agent
EntryApp/API Gateway + IAM/SSO + tenant context
PlatformBedrock + AgentCore runtime/memory/tools
KnowledgeOpenSearch/Neptune/S3 with governed ingestion
PolicyBedrock Guardrails + OPA-style entitlements
OpsCloudWatch/Splunk + eval CI/CD + incident runbooks
Regulated / high-governance stack
BoundaryPrivate network, SSO, tenant isolation, DLP
ContextPrivate retrieval with source permissions and freshness SLA
PolicyGuardrails, approval gates, action allowlists
EvidenceImmutable traces, eval reports, risk register
ReleaseCanary, rollback, incident drills, audit review

5.4Build-vs-Buy Matrix

🧱Default to buying commodity layersbuild only for durable advantage
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
LayerBuy firstBuild/customize whenCommon mistake
Model gatewayManaged provider gateway or lightweight internal router.You need multi-provider routing, tenant budgets, model pinning, or compliance logging.Hardcoding one model directly into product code.
Retrievalpgvector, OpenSearch, managed vector store.You need hybrid search, graph retrieval, strict ACL filtering, or extreme scale.Optimizing embedding choice before fixing corpus quality.
ObservabilityLangfuse, LangSmith, Splunk, Grafana.You need custom trace semantics or enterprise telemetry consolidation.Shipping without run-level traces and prompt/model version IDs.
EvaluationPromptFoo, DeepEval, AgentEvals.You need domain-specific grading, policy scoring, or adjudicated human labels.Treating manual prompt checks as release tests.
Guardrails/policyGuardrails AI, Bedrock Guardrails, OPA.You need regulated policy logic, explainable denials, or custom entitlement checks.Relying on prompt instructions as the security boundary.
Workflow orchestrationLangGraph, Temporal, AgentCore, Airflow for batch.You have proprietary scheduling, compensation, or approval semantics.Letting the model own durable workflow state.
MemoryMem0 or managed memory only after policies are clear.You need user-visible correction, retention controls, or compliance deletion workflows.Writing everything to memory without consent, scope, or expiry.

5.5Agent Maturity Model

📈From chatbot to governed autonomous system5 levels
LevelSystem behaviorMinimum architectureRequired controlsPromotion signal
1 · Prototype chatbotAnswers user questions with a prompt and single model.UI, model call, basic logging.Manual review, no write tools, no sensitive data.Useful answers in narrow demos.
2 · RAG assistantAnswers from approved knowledge sources with citations.Ingestion, embeddings, retrieval, citation display, trace capture.Groundedness eval, freshness checks, source permissions.Passes golden Q&A and citation-accuracy gates.
3 · Tool-using assistantCalls read-only or low-risk tools to complete bounded tasks.Tool registry, schemas, sandbox, routing, tool traces.Tool allowlists, auth scopes, injection tests, cost limits.Tool success rate and safe refusal rate meet thresholds.
4 · Supervised agentPlans, executes, and asks for human approval before material actions.State machine, HITL queue, idempotency, rollback, memory policy.Approval gates, runbooks, escalation ownership, autonomy ladder.Low reversal rate; human approvals become predictable.
5 · Governed multi-agent systemCoordinates multiple agents/tools across teams or ecosystems.A2A/MCP, model gateway, policy engine, observability, eval CI/CD, audit lake.RACI, release gates, risk register, incident drills, tenant isolation.Production SLOs stable across releases and tenants.
Scroll horizontally to compare →
Decision path complete. Product and architecture readers can stop here. Platform, security, SRE, data, and governance owners should continue through the Production Playbook.
TL;DR
  • Fast-moving section - check freshness badges before using any entry for a production decision.
  • The five default stack recipes cover 90% of use cases; browse the catalog only when they do not fit.
  • MCP = tool access; A2A = cross-agent delegation; AG-UI/A2UI = generative frontend.
  • Prefer mature tools behind abstraction layers so you can swap vendors without rewriting the agent.
Market-map rule: the cards below are useful for discovery, but procurement and production decisions should check current pricing, support, security posture, roadmap, and ecosystem maturity. The badge on each card is a starting recommendation, not a permanent ranking.

5.6 Projects

Monthly review
🧠

5.6.1 Model Layer — The Layer Every Other Layer Serves

model router · model registry · eval-gated lanes
Canonical treatment: owns production model selection, routing lanes, registries, compatibility testing, and eval-gated model promotion.
The layer every other layer serves. UI, orchestration, memory, retrieval, tools, evals, observability, and governance all exist to feed the right context into the right model, constrain its actions, measure its behavior, and route work away from it when a cheaper or safer option is enough. Treat this layer as a production model router, not a single vendor choice: task → lane → model → policy → eval gate → fallback.
Production model lanes
Core frontier
Claude 4 · GPT-5/GPT-5.5 · Gemini 2.5/3.x
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
WatchMarket-map
Use for high-value reasoning, orchestration, complex synthesis, multimodal work, and agent nodes where quality matters more than unit cost.
default for hard tasksfrontier reasoning
Open / self-host

Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
WatchMarket-map
Use for data sovereignty, private inference, air-gapped deployments, controllable cost at scale, and open-governance model strategies.
self-hostsovereignty
Coding / agentic

Watchreviewed 2026-05-24

Use for repository understanding, multi-file edits, IDE workflows, tool-use loops, implementation agents, and code-review/repair cycles.
coding agentstool use
Fast / cheap

low latencyhigh volume
Retrieval models

embeddingsrerankers

Bedrockmanaged governance
Model Gateway / AI Gateway
LiteLLM · Portkey · OpenRouter · Kong AI Gateway · Cloudflare AI Gateway
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
WatchMarket-map
Use between agent frameworks/runtimes and model providers to centralize provider abstraction, routing, fallback, virtual keys, budgets, spend tracking, caching, and policy hooks.
model accessroutingcost governance
Model routing architecture
1 · Classify taskReasoning, coding, retrieval, extraction, moderation, synthesis, planning, tool use, or multimodal understanding.
2 · Select laneFrontier, open/self-host, coding, fast/cheap, retrieval, AWS-native, or human-review path.
3 · Apply policyTenant, region, PII, tool permissions, autonomy level, data boundary, and allowed-provider constraints.
4 · Call modelUse the smallest model that clears quality, latency, safety, and cost thresholds for that task class.
5 · Verify outputRun schema checks, retrieval-grounding checks, policy checks, eval assertions, and human approval where needed.
6 · Fallback / escalateRetry with more context, rerank evidence, escalate to frontier model, or hand off to a human.
What to store in the model registry
Capability profile
what the model is allowed to do
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
WatchMarket-map
Reasoning depth, coding quality, context length, multimodal support, tool-calling reliability, JSON/schema reliability, multilingual quality, and retrieval-grounded answer quality.
Operating profile
how it behaves in production
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
WatchMarket-map
Latency bands, throughput, rate limits, region availability, batch support, streaming support, uptime expectations, cost per task, and fallback priority.
Governance profile
where it is safe to use
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
WatchMarket-map
Approved data classes, PII handling, tenant isolation rules, retention settings, provider risk, audit needs, procurement status, and regulatory constraints.
Evaluation profile
how you know it still works
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
WatchMarket-map
Golden-set scores, task-specific evals, regression history, hallucination rate, citation accuracy, tool-call success rate, refusal quality, and drift alerts.
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
LaneCandidate modelsBest forWatch-outs
Core frontierClaude 4 · GPT-5/GPT-5.5 · Gemini 2.5/3.xComplex reasoning · orchestration · multimodal · high-value agent nodesHigher cost · route selectively
Open / self-hostLlama 4 · Mistral 3 · DeepSeek · Qwen · Gemma · Granite · Nemotron · Kimi K2/K2.5Sovereignty · private inference · cost control · open governanceRequires infra, evals, and model-ops discipline
Coding / agenticClaude Sonnet/Opus · GPT-5 · Composer 2.5 · Kimi K2/K2.5 · Devstral/Codestral · Gemini Pro/Flash · DeepSeek · Qwen CoderRepo agents · IDE tasks · tool use · generate-test-repair loopsNeeds sandboxing, test gates, PR review, and write-action controls
Fast / cheapClaude Haiku · GPT mini/nano · Gemini Flash/Flash-Lite · Mistral Small · PhiClassification · routing · extraction · high-volume background workersDo not use for high-stakes reasoning without verification
Retrieval modelsCohere · Voyage · Jina · BGE/E5 · Titan Embeddings · OpenAI embeddings · Google embeddingsEmbeddings · reranking · hybrid search · RAG context qualityMust version embeddings, rerun evals, and manage reindexing
Model gatewayLiteLLM · Portkey · OpenRouter · Kong AI Gateway · Cloudflare AI GatewayProvider abstraction · routing · fallbacks · budgets · virtual keys · spend trackingMust avoid becoming a hidden policy bypass; connect to evals, observability, and access control
AWS-nativeAmazon Nova · Titan Embeddings · Bedrock-hosted Claude/Llama/MistralBedrock governance · IAM · centralized billing · AgentCore alignmentChoose by region, service quota, eval performance, and procurement constraints
Default selection rules
When the task is…Start with…Escalate when…
Ambiguous, high-value, multi-step, or customer-visibleCore frontier laneEvidence is weak, tools disagree, or answer affects money, safety, legal, or access
Repository-level implementation or debuggingCoding / agentic laneTests fail repeatedly, edits touch sensitive systems, or architectural judgment is needed
Classification, routing, extraction, summarization, or pre-checksFast / cheap laneConfidence is low, schema validation fails, or the item is high-risk
Private, regulated, air-gapped, or cost-at-scale workloadOpen / self-host or AWS-native laneOpen model quality misses the eval bar or compliance requires managed controls
RAG answer quality depends on evidence qualityRetrieval models + rerankers before generationTop-k evidence conflicts, citations are weak, or freshness is uncertain
Multi-model pattern: Don't use one model for everything. Use frontier models for orchestration and hard reasoning, fast/cheap models for high-volume subtasks, open/self-hosted models for sovereignty and cost constraints, coding-specialized models for IDE/repository workflows, retrieval models for RAG quality, and AWS-native models when Bedrock governance is the primary operating constraint.
🖥️

5.6.2 UI / App Layer

4 projects + API gateway + IDEs
Streamlit
Snowflake · Python-first UI
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Rapid Python-native UI for data and ML apps. Turn a Python script into a shareable web app in minutes. No frontend experience needed. Ideal for internal tools, agent dashboards, data exploration UIs, and quick prototypes on top of agent pipelines. Native support for streaming output and chat interfaces.
Python-nativerapid prototypingdashboardsstreaming
Next.js + React
Vercel · full production web stack
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
The standard for production agent UIs. Server components, streaming via Vercel AI SDK, API routes as agent endpoints. Pairs with the Vercel AI SDK for streaming LLM responses, tool call rendering, and multi-step agent UIs. Used by most serious production chat and copilot interfaces.
productionVercel AI SDKstreamingfull-stack
CopilotKit
CopilotKit · React-first AI copilot framework
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Framework for embedding AI copilots directly inside production React applications. Best placed in the UI / App Layer because it provides the application-side primitives for in-product assistants, sidebars, human approvals, state-aware actions, and agentic workflows. Cross-references the Generative UI layer because CopilotKit can act as the frontend adoption surface for agent↔UI protocols such as A2UI and AG-UI.
embedded copilotReacthuman-in-the-loop UIagent frontend
Five UI Patterns  ·  Chat UI — conversational, message thread, streaming (OpenWebUI, Claude.ai, custom React)  ·  Dashboard UI — trigger runs, monitor progress, inspect traces (Retool, Next.js)  ·  Embedded / Copilot — agent wired into existing product as sidebar or panel (CopilotKit)  ·  CLI / Terminal — developer-facing, terminal-native (OpenTerminal, Claude Code)  ·  Headless / API — no UI, triggered by schedule or webhook (Airflow in Orchestrator layer)
🌐Self-Hostable UI Sub-layerpre-built open-source interfaces · no frontend dev required
OpenWebUI
Open Source · self-hostable chat UI
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Open-source, self-hostable web interface for LLMs — think self-hosted Claude.ai or ChatGPT UI. Supports multiple backends: Ollama, OpenAI-compatible APIs, Anthropic. Rich feature set: conversation history, model switching, system prompt management, document upload, image generation. Use it to give your team a polished Chat UI on top of your agent stack without building one from scratch.
self-hostablechat UImulti-backendopen source
OpenTerminal
Community · terminal-native LLM UI
Stablereviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Terminal-native interface for LLMs — the CLI equivalent of OpenWebUI. Sits in the UI layer as the CLI/terminal UI pattern. Good for developer-facing agent access where a browser UI is overhead. Close to where Claude Code itself lives in the stack — the natural interface for engineers interacting with agent pipelines from the command line.
terminal-nativeCLI UIdeveloper-facingopen source
🔗API / Gateway Sub-layersits between UI and the agent pipeline · exposes HTTP endpoints · handles auth · rate limiting · streaming
Flask
Pallets · Python micro web framework · 2010
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
The battle-tested Python micro web framework. HTTP routing, request handling, response generation — nothing more. Exposes your agent pipeline as an HTTP endpoint any frontend or service can call. Sync-first, enormous installed base, maximum simplicity. The right choice for straightforward integrations and existing Python codebases. FastAPI is the recommended modern default for new agent API work.
Pythonsync-firstbattle-testedsimple integrations
FastAPI
Sebastián Ramírez · modern async framework · 2018
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
The modern default for Python agent APIs. Native async/await — LangGraph, Claude Agent SDK, and LLM clients are all async, so FastAPI fits without workarounds. Auto-generates OpenAPI docs at /docs. models validate every agent input before it hits the model. First-class StreamingResponse for token-by-token agent output. Recommended over Flask for all new agent pipeline work.
Pythonnative asyncstreamingauto OpenAPIrecommended
Flask vs FastAPI:   Same role — both are Python API gateways at the HTTP boundary of your agent stack  ·  Flask — sync, simple, battle-tested, large ecosystem  ·  FastAPI — async-native, auto OpenAPI, streaming-first  ·  Default to FastAPI for any new agent API work · migrate Flask if async or streaming becomes a bottleneck
💻IDE / Dev Environment Sub-layerAI-native editors that contain a harness · UI and harness in one · the dev's primary interface
These tools straddle the UI and Harness layers — the IDE is the interface the developer sees, but it contains harness-level primitives (rules files, agent modes, multi-file context, terminal access). Claude Code is a CLI harness. Cursor/Copilot are IDE harnesses. Codex is a cloud agent. Same category of tool, different UX paradigm.
Cursor
Anysphere · VS Code fork · multi-model
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
AI-native code editor built on VS Code. Multi-model (Claude, GPT-4, Gemini). Agent mode via Composer / Composer 2.5 — multi-step, multi-file autonomous coding. Config via .cursorrules (equivalent to CLAUDE.md). Tab autocomplete, codebase-aware chat, inline edits. The most widely adopted AI IDE. Best for interactive coding with AI assistance rather than long autonomous runs.
IDE harnessVS Code forkmulti-model.cursorrulesComposer agentComposer 2.5
GitHub Copilot
Microsoft / OpenAI · enterprise standard
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
The enterprise default AI coding assistant. VS Code extension (also JetBrains, Neovim). Copilot Chat, Copilot Workspace for multi-step agent tasks. GPT-4o / Claude backend. Massive enterprise adoption — if your team is already on GitHub Enterprise, Copilot is often already licensed. Less autonomous than Cursor but deeper GitHub integration.
enterpriseVS Code extensionGitHub-nativemulti-editor
Codex
OpenAI · cloud-native autonomous coding agent · 2025
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
A fully managed cloud coding agent — not an IDE, not a model. You give it a task, it spins up a sandboxed container in OpenAI's cloud, reads your codebase, writes code, runs tests, and opens a pull request. You never see a terminal. Supports parallel tasks simultaneously. The closest OpenAI equivalent to Claude Managed Agents — async, cloud-hosted, result-oriented rather than interactive. Best for batch coding tasks you want to run in parallel without watching.
cloud agentasyncparallel tasksmanaged sandboxGPT-4o / o3
Claude Code vs IDE harnesses vs Codex:Claude Code — CLI · Claude-only · local · headless · best for autonomous long-running tasks  ·  Cursor — GUI · multi-model · local · interactive-first  ·  Copilot — extension · enterprise · local · GitHub-native  ·  Codex — cloud agent · OpenAI · async · parallel · no local interface
🔁

5.6.3 Loop Patterns & Iteration Control

1 primitive · 34 loop families · search + consensus expanded
Loop control is the agent's execution policy. The primitive loop keeps an agent running; the loop family determines how it searches, acts, critiques, verifies, coordinates, remembers, escalates, and stops. These patterns are composable: a production system can use a DAG wrapper, a Plan–Execute–Reflect outer loop, ReAct inner tool calls, verifier gates, retrieval research, HITL interrupts, and multi-agent fan-out in the same run.
Primitive — the mechanical substrate
Ralph Loop
Geoffrey Huntley · bash primitive · foundational keep-going loop
Stablereviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Bash while loop that keeps Claude Code or another terminal-first agent running until a completion signal. It is not a reasoning strategy by itself; it is the mechanical substrate underneath every named pattern. All higher-level loops answer the question: what should happen inside the loop body before the agent stops?
while → run → stopsubstrateterminal-first
Foundational agent loops — single-agent execution control
ReAct
Reason + Act · foundational inner tool-calling loop
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Reason about the current state, act by calling a tool or subagent, observe the result, then reason again. ReAct is the default inner loop for tool-using agents because it connects model reasoning to external actions and observations.
Reason → Act → Observeinner looptool use
Think–Act–Observe
Plain-English ReAct framing · default tool-use loop
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Think through the next step, act through a tool, API, retrieval call, or subagent, observe the result, then continue or stop. This is the clearest operational framing for most production tool-using agents.
Think → Act → Observedefaulttool feedback
Replan Loop
Plan repair · changing-context execution
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Create a plan, execute a step, observe a failure, contradiction, permission block, missing dependency, or changed user goal, then revise the plan before continuing. Use it whenever the environment can invalidate the original plan.
Plan → Act → Observe → Replanadaptivefailure recovery
Plan–Execute–Reflect
Structured outer loop · deliberate multi-step work
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Plan the full approach, execute step by step, reflect on output quality and failures, then revise before the next cycle. Use it outside ReAct when premature action is costly or when the task needs checkpoints, dependencies, or explicit review.
Plan → Execute → Reflectouter loopmulti-step
Human-in-the-Loop (HITL)
Interrupt / approval gate · policy and uncertainty control
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
A control handoff inside any loop: the agent runs, reaches an uncertain or policy-gated decision, pauses, receives human approval or correction, then continues. HITL is best treated as an interrupt mechanism, not a standalone reasoning pattern.
Run → Interrupt → Human → Continueapprovalsafety valve
Test-Driven Agent Loop
Coding-specific ReAct specialization
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
The observe step is a test result. The agent writes code, runs tests, observes failures, reasons about the cause, revises, and repeats until tests pass. This is the tightest feedback loop because pass/fail evidence is explicit.
Write → Test → Observe → Revisecodingverifiable
Reflexion / Experience-Learning
Attempt → lesson → retry with memory
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
After success or failure, the agent writes a reusable lesson, stores it in working or long-term memory, then retries future attempts with that lesson. Unlike simple critique, the feedback becomes durable experience.
attempt → reflect → rememberlearningretry
Self-Evaluation / Confidence-Gated Loop
Uncertainty as the control variable
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
The agent estimates confidence, risk, or evidence sufficiency after each step, then decides whether to continue, retry, verify, escalate to HITL, or stop. This is the production-safe wrapper for regulated or high-impact actions.
answer → assess → routerisk gateescalation
Search loops — explore candidate solution spaces
Search / Tree-of-Thought Loop
Branch → score → prune → continue
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
The agent explores multiple reasoning or action branches, scores partial paths, prunes weak branches, and continues along promising ones. This is fundamentally different from ReAct because the agent searches a tree of possible solutions rather than following one linear trajectory.
candidate branchessearchpruning
Beam Search
Top-k candidate plan search · bounded exploration
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Generate multiple candidate next steps, keep the best few beams according to a scoring function, expand them, and prune the rest. Use when several plausible plans exist but exhaustive search is too expensive.
branch → keep top-kbounded searchadvanced
MCTS / LATS
Monte Carlo Tree Search · language-agent tree search
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Explore candidate action or reasoning trees, simulate outcomes, score branches, backpropagate value, and select stronger paths. LATS-style loops combine language-model generation, self-reflection, and tree search for difficult planning tasks.
select → expand → simulate → backpropplanningexperimental
Best-of-N / Sampling-Ranking Loop
Parallel diversity · selection loop
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Generate several candidate outputs, rank or score them, then select or merge the best. It improves quality through diversity and selection rather than stepwise correction.
generate Nrankselect / merge
Evolutionary / Genetic Loop
Population search · mutate · evaluate · select
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Generate a population of prompts, plans, tests, or designs; mutate or combine them; evaluate fitness; then select survivors for the next generation. Useful when the search space is broad and the scoring function is cheaper than manual design.
populationmutationoptimization
Critique, consensus, and verification loops — improve correctness and trust
Critique–Revise Loop
Producer / critic separation
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Draft an answer or artifact, critique it against criteria, revise it, then critique again until the result is acceptable. The key structural distinction is that generation and critique are separate roles, even if performed by the same model.
draft → critique → revisequality
Debate / Multi-Agent Review
Proponent · opponent · judge
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
One agent proposes, another challenges assumptions or argues the opposite position, then a judge selects or synthesizes the final answer. Use for high-impact reasoning, tradeoff analysis, red-team review, and hidden-assumption discovery.
argue → rebut → judgemulti-agentadvanced reliability
Consensus / Ensemble Loop
Multiple independent answers · reconcile
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Several agents or models answer independently, compare outputs, identify disagreements, then vote, merge, or escalate. The final answer emerges from agreement or aggregation rather than one model's confidence.
multi-sampleagreementmerge
Self-Consistency
Sample multiple reasoning paths · choose stable answer
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Ask the model to solve the same problem through multiple independent reasoning paths, then select the answer that appears most consistently. Use for math, classification, extraction, and structured reasoning where one greedy answer is brittle.
sample → compare → selectreasoning reliabilityconsensus
Majority Voting
Simple ensemble decision · production-friendly consensus
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Run multiple independent attempts, normalize the outputs, and choose the majority result or escalate ties. This is the simpler, more operational version of self-consistency for labels, extraction fields, graders, and policy decisions.
N attempts → voteclassificationtie escalation
Verifier / Formal-Check Loop
Hard constraints · schema / policy / evidence checks
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Produce an output, verify it against rules, schemas, tests, citations, permissions, or policies, then repair if verification fails. This loop is essential for structured LLM output, tool-call contracts, and safety gates.
produce → verify → repairguardrails
Knowledge and capability loops — acquire missing context or tools
Retrieval-Grounded Research Loop
Query → retrieve → read → synthesize → gap-check
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
The loop is driven by information sufficiency. The agent searches, retrieves, reads, synthesizes, detects gaps, and re-queries until the evidence is adequate for the task.
RAGgap-drivenevidence
Memory-Consolidation Loop
Act → extract memory → update state → reuse
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
After interaction, the agent extracts durable preferences, facts, summaries, or lessons, writes them to memory, and uses them in future runs. This changes future behavior rather than only solving the current task.
persistent memorypersonalization
Tool-Discovery / Capability-Acquisition Loop
Need → discover → test → bind → use
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
When the agent lacks a capability, it searches available tools, APIs, MCP servers, or plugins, tests the interface, binds the usable capability, then applies it. This is common in open enterprise tool ecosystems.
tool discoveryMCPcapability
Multi-agent coordination loops — distribute work across agents
Supervisor–Worker Loop
Manager agent routes to specialist workers
Stablereviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
A supervisor decomposes work, assigns subtasks to specialist agents, reviews results, and either merges, retries, or reassigns. This is the most common production multi-agent coordination pattern.
assign → execute → reviewspecialists
Market / Contract-Net Loop
Bid-based task allocation
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
A task is announced, available agents bid based on capability, cost, or availability, the coordinator awards the task, and results are verified. Use when assignments are dynamic and agent capabilities differ.
announce → bid → assignrouting
Blackboard / Shared-State Loop
Indirect coordination through shared workspace
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Agents read a shared workspace, add partial findings, monitor changes, and converge on a solution. Coordination happens through the evolving state rather than direct command chains.
shared workspaceconvergence
Swarm / Stigmergic Loop
Decentralized local actions · emergent coordination
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Many agents make local updates to an environment; those updates influence other agents' next actions. Useful for large search, red-team sweeps, simulations, and broad exploration where central planning is too expensive.
many agentslocal rulesemergent
Production orchestration and environment loops — operate safely over time
DAG / Workflow Orchestration
Airflow · outer scheduler and dependency wrapper
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
A Directed Acyclic Graph of sequential steps, conditional branches, parallel fan-out, and joins. Each node can contain an agent loop. The DAG decides when work runs; the agent loop decides how work is performed inside a node.
DAGscheduledouter wrapper
Workflow-State-Machine Loop
Explicit states · transition guards
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
The agent or workflow moves through predefined states with allowed transitions and guards. Use when production correctness depends on not skipping approvals, retries, or required intermediate states.
state → guard → actiondeterministic
Event-Driven Reactive Loop
Wait → classify → route → act → wait
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
The agent is triggered by external events such as tickets, emails, alerts, webhooks, or schedules. It classifies the event, routes work, acts, monitors the result, then returns to waiting.
event triggerreactiveops
Monitor–Act / Continuous Control Loop
Observe environment → act → observe delta
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
The agent repeatedly observes a changing environment, chooses an action, applies it, and monitors the delta. This is the loop for live operations, remediation, robotics-like systems, and infrastructure control.
observe → act → monitorcontinuous
Safety, autonomy & production-control loops — v3.06 additions
These loops make agent behavior more production-grade: they bound cost, classify risk before action, verify tool contracts, recover from failures, and route uncertain work to humans or safer fallbacks. Use them as wrappers around ReAct, Plan–Execute–Reflect, retrieval research, and multi-agent loops.
Sense–Model–Plan–Act
Environment-oriented control loop · changing-world agents
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Sense the environment, update an internal world model, plan the next action, act, then observe the delta. Use when the agent is operating against live systems rather than a static prompt or document set.
Sense → Model → Plan → Actenvironment-awareops / robotics-like
Goal–Decompose–Delegate–Integrate
Multi-agent lifecycle loop · task decomposition and synthesis
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Start with a goal, break it into subtasks, delegate to workers or tools, integrate outputs, and verify the final product. This is the full lifecycle behind supervisor-worker systems.
Goal → Decompose → Delegate → Integratemulti-agentsynthesis
Hypothesis–Experiment–Update
Scientific-method loop · research, debugging, analysis
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Form a hypothesis, run an experiment or query, inspect evidence, update belief, and continue or stop. Best for data analysis, root-cause investigation, scientific discovery, and ambiguous research tasks.
Hypothesize → Test → Updatediagnosticresearch
Budget-Gated Autonomy
Cost / latency / tool-call control · bounded agents
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Plan, estimate cost/risk/time, act within a budget, reassess, then stop, compress, switch strategy, or escalate when the budget is exceeded. Prevents runaway loops and uncontrolled tool use.
Plan → Budget → Act → Reassesscost controlrunaway guard
Risk-Gated Action
Permission-tier loop · safe autonomy
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Classify the intent and action risk, select the permission tier, then execute, ask for approval, or refuse. Use before write actions, production changes, financial operations, customer messages, or sensitive data access.
Intent → Risk → Permission → Actapproval tiergovernance
Retrieve–Read–Reason–Cite
Evidence-first RAG / research loop
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Retrieve candidate evidence, read and filter it, reason from supportable claims, cite the evidence, then check that the answer is actually grounded. Prevents retrieval from becoming decorative context.
Retrieve → Read → Reason → Citegrounded answersRAG
Observe–Diagnose–Remediate–Verify
Incident / operations loop · recovery-oriented control
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Observe a failure, diagnose likely cause, apply a fix or mitigation, verify recovery, and document the outcome. This is the natural loop for DevOps agents, support agents, and production remediation.
Observe → Diagnose → Remediate → Verifyincident responseops
Generate–Test–Repair
Builder loop · code, reports, data products
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Generate an artifact, run tests or evals, inspect failures, repair, and retest until a release condition is met. This generalizes test-driven coding into a broader artifact-building loop.
Generate → Test → RepairCI / evalsbuilder agent
Draft–Review–Approve–Publish
Business-output loop · safe externalization
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Draft the content or decision, run automated review, route to a human or domain owner when needed, revise, then publish. Best for customer-facing communications, reports, policy docs, and executive artifacts.
Draft → Review → Approve → PublishHITLcontent governance
Learn–Distill–Persist
Operational learning loop · memory with judgment
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
After a task, extract reusable lessons, distill them into compact memory or policy updates, persist them, and reuse them in future runs. Separates durable learning from raw transcript storage.
Learn → Distill → Persistmemory qualitycontinuous improvement
Uncertainty Calibration
Confidence and deferral loop · decision support
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Produce a candidate answer, estimate confidence, compare confidence against evidence quality, then answer, defer, retrieve more, or escalate. Use when overconfidence is more dangerous than delay.
Answer → Calibrate → Defer / Actconfidenceescalation
Plan–Simulate–Select
Pre-action simulation loop · expensive or risky choices
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Generate candidate plans, simulate or reason through consequences, score tradeoffs, choose a plan, and only then execute. Useful when real-world actions are expensive, irreversible, or risky.
Plan → Simulate → Selectrisk reductionstrategy
Contract-First Tool Loop
Tool schema / API validation loop
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Define the expected tool contract, call the tool, validate response shape and semantics, then retry, repair, or fall back if the response violates the contract. Critical for MCP, APIs, SQL, and workflow agents.
Contract → Call → Validate → Repairschema-safetool reliability
Fallback / Graceful Degradation
Resilience loop · partial success under failure
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Try the preferred path, detect failure, switch to a fallback, reduce scope if needed, and return the best safe partial result. Use for model outages, API failures, rate limits, missing permissions, and retrieval gaps.
Try → Detect → Fallback → Degraderesiliencepartial answer
Pick your loop — decision guide
Does your task require multiple steps?
Yes → use a control loop
No → single model call, no loop needed
Is the plan knowable upfront?
Yes → Plan–Execute–Reflect
No → ReAct (decide step by step)
Does the environment change during the run?
Yes → Replan Loop or Sense–Model–Plan–Act
No → standard Plan–Execute–Reflect
Is correctness more important than speed?
Yes → Consensus, Debate, or Verifier loop
No → standard ReAct with stop condition
Are there multiple candidate solutions to explore?
Yes → Tree-of-Thought, MCTS, or Best-of-N
No → linear ReAct or Plan–Execute–Reflect
Can the agent take irreversible actions?
Yes → Risk-Gated Action + HITL approval gates
No → standard loop with stop conditions
Taxonomy diagram — loop families by control driver
AI Agent Control Loops │ ├── Mechanical Substrate │ └── Ralph Loop │ ├── Single-Agent Iteration │ ├── Think–Act–Observe / ReAct │ ├── Replan Loop │ ├── Plan–Execute–Reflect │ ├── Sense–Model–Plan–Act │ ├── Reflexion / Experience-Learning │ └── Self-Evaluation / Confidence-Gated │ ├── Search & Optimization Loops │ ├── Beam Search │ ├── Search / Tree-of-Thought │ ├── MCTS / LATS │ ├── Best-of-N / Sampling-Ranking │ ├── Evolutionary / Genetic Search │ └── Plan–Simulate–Select │ ├── Evidence & Knowledge Loops │ ├── Retrieval-Grounded Research │ ├── Retrieve–Read–Reason–Cite │ ├── Memory-Consolidation │ ├── Learn–Distill–Persist │ └── Tool-Discovery / Capability-Acquisition │ ├── Critique, Verification & Quality Loops │ ├── Critique–Revise │ ├── Debate / Multi-Agent Review │ ├── Adversarial Red-Team │ ├── Consensus / Ensemble │ ├── Self-Consistency │ ├── Majority Voting │ ├── Verifier / Formal-Check │ ├── Generate–Test–Repair │ └── Contract-First Tool Loop │ ├── Safety & Autonomy Control Loops │ ├── Risk-Gated Action │ ├── Budget-Gated Autonomy │ ├── Uncertainty Calibration │ ├── Human-in-the-Loop / Approval Gate │ └── Draft–Review–Approve–Publish │ ├── Multi-Agent Coordination Loops │ ├── Goal–Decompose–Delegate–Integrate │ ├── Supervisor–Worker │ ├── Market / Contract-Net │ ├── Blackboard / Shared-State │ └── Swarm / Stigmergic │ └── Production Reliability & Environment Loops ├── DAG / Workflow ├── Workflow-State-Machine ├── Event-Driven Reactive ├── Monitor–Act / Continuous Control ├── Observe–Diagnose–Remediate–Verify └── Fallback / Graceful Degradation
Loop pattern decision tree — choosing the right control loop
Use this tree after choosing the product pattern. It selects the execution policy for the agent: simple linear loops by default, replan when the world can change, search when multiple solution paths matter, consensus when correctness matters more than latency, and safety gates whenever actions have consequences.
Start: what kind of control does the agent need? │ ├── Does the agent use tools, APIs, retrieval, code, or subagents? │ ├── Yes → Think–Act–Observe / ReAct │ └── No → Use a simple answer / transform flow; do not add an agent loop yet │ ├── Can tools fail, evidence conflict, permissions block action, or the user goal change? │ └── Add Replan Loop around the tool-use loop │ ├── Is the task multi-step, dependent, or risky to start without a plan? │ └── Wrap with Plan–Execute–Reflect │ ├── Is the agent triggered by schedules, tickets, alerts, webhooks, or external events? │ ├── Event-Driven Reactive for event handlers │ └── DAG / Workflow when dependencies and retries must be explicit │ ├── Are there multiple plausible solution paths? │ ├── Cheap parallel candidates → Best-of-N / Sampling-Ranking │ ├── Need bounded exploration → Beam Search │ ├── Need branching reasoning → Search / Tree-of-Thought │ └── Need deep planning with value estimates → MCTS / LATS │ ├── Is correctness more important than latency or token cost? │ ├── Reasoning answer → Self-Consistency │ ├── Classification / extraction / grading → Majority Voting │ ├── Draft or artifact quality → Critique–Revise │ └── Hard constraints → Verifier / Formal-Check │ ├── Is the decision subjective, high-impact, adversarial, or policy-sensitive? │ ├── Debate / Multi-Agent Review for opposing perspectives │ ├── Adversarial Red-Team for failure discovery │ └── HITL / Approval Gate for final authority │ ├── Does the task require fresh, external, or cited knowledge? │ ├── Retrieval-Grounded Research for exploration │ ├── Retrieve–Read–Reason–Cite for grounded deliverables │ └── Learn–Distill–Persist when lessons should improve future runs │ ├── Does the agent operate in a live or changing environment? │ ├── Sense–Model–Plan–Act for environment-aware planning │ ├── Monitor–Act for continuous control │ └── Observe–Diagnose–Remediate–Verify for incidents and remediation │ ├── Could the action affect users, money, infrastructure, sensitive data, or policy? │ ├── Risk-Gated Action to classify permission tier before execution │ ├── Budget-Gated Autonomy to bound time, cost, and tool calls │ ├── Contract-First Tool Loop for APIs, MCP tools, SQL, and schemas │ └── Draft–Review–Approve–Publish for external-facing outputs │ ├── Are latency or cost tight? │ ├── Prefer Think–Act–Observe + Replan + explicit stop rules │ ├── Avoid Beam / ToT / MCTS unless quality gains are measured │ └── Use caching, smaller models, and fallback paths before consensus loops │ ├── Can dependencies fail or answers be incomplete? │ └── Fallback / Graceful Degradation with partial safe output │ └── Recommended default production composition └── DAG / Workflow → Plan–Execute–Reflect → Think–Act–Observe / ReAct → Replan → Risk / Budget / Verifier / HITL → Fallback
Default loopsThink–Act–Observe, ReAct, Replan, Plan–Execute–Reflect. Use these before adding search or consensus.
Reliability loopsSelf-Consistency, Majority Voting, Critique–Revise, Verifier, HITL. Add when correctness beats speed.
Advanced loopsBeam Search, Tree-of-Thought, MCTS / LATS, Debate. Use only when the added latency and cost are justified by measured quality gains.
Pattern comparison
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
Loop familyControl cycleDistinctive structureBest for
ReActReason → Act → ObserveSingle linear tool-use trajectoryGeneral tool-calling agents
Search / ToTGenerate branches → score → pruneExplores many reasoning pathsHard reasoning, planning alternatives
Critique–ReviseDraft → critique → reviseSeparates producer and critic rolesWriting, reports, policy drafts
ConsensusAnswer independently → compare → mergeQuality from agreement / aggregationHigh-stakes QA, evals, factual checks
VerifierProduce → verify → repairHard external checksSchemas, tests, guardrails, citations
Retrieval ResearchQuery → retrieve → synthesize → gap-checkDriven by evidence sufficiencyRAG, deep research, due diligence
Multi-AgentAssign / bid / share → execute → mergeCoordination across specialistsLarge tasks, parallel work, specialist teams
Risk-Gated ActionIntent → risk → permission → actClassifies action before executionWrite actions, sensitive data, infrastructure, finance
Budget-Gated AutonomyEstimate → act → reassess → stop/escalateBounds cost, latency, and tool callsLong-running agents and production workloads
Generate–Test–RepairGenerate → test → repair → retestExternal pass/fail feedbackCode, reports, data pipelines, eval-backed artifacts
Contract-First ToolContract → call → validate → repair/fallbackSchema-first tool reliabilityMCP, APIs, SQL agents, workflow tools
Production ControlEvent/state/monitor → route → act → fallbackExternal triggers, explicit state, resilienceOps agents, workflows, live remediation
Legacy
Retained from v2.16 because it provides a compact production nesting model and comparison table for the original loop-pattern set. Use this as the canonical composition reference for how Ralph Loop, ReAct, Plan–Execute–Reflect, HITL, Test-Driven, and DAG / Workflow fit together inside a production agent system.
How patterns nest in a production system
DAG / Workflow(Airflow — outer scheduler)
    └── Plan–Execute–Reflect(outer agent loop)
            └── ReAct(inner tool-calling loop)
                    ├── HITL(interrupt when uncertain or policy-gated)
                    └── Test-driven(coding sub-loop where observe = test result)
These patterns are not mutually exclusive — they compose. A production agent system typically has all five active simultaneously at different levels of the call stack.
Pattern comparison
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
PatternCycleHuman involvedBest forImplements in
Ralph Loopwhile → run → stopNoSingle-agent keep-going substratebash · all harnesses
ReActReason → Act → ObserveNoGeneral tool-calling · inner loopClaude Code · LangGraph · all harnesses
Plan–Execute–ReflectPlan → Execute → Reflect → ReviseOptionalComplex multi-step tasks · report generationLangGraph · Deep Agents
HITLRun → Interrupt → Human → ContinueYes — requiredWrite-access agents · policy-gated decisionsLangGraph interrupt() · Deep Agents
Test-DrivenWrite → Test → Observe failures → ReviseNoAutonomous coding · codebase agentsCodex · Claude Code · GitHub Actions
DAG / WorkflowSequential → Conditional → Parallel → JoinTrigger onlyScheduled pipelines · outer orchestrationAirflow · LangGraph Send() API
Scroll horizontally to compare →
🏙️

5.6.4 Multi-Agent Orchestration

5 projects · 2 patterns
📅Outer Orchestration — DAG / Workflow Layerabove the agent layer · schedules and triggers pipelines · the "user" is a clock or event
Airflow
Apache Software Foundation · enterprise DAG orchestration
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
The enterprise standard for pipeline orchestration. Define workflows as Python code — tasks, dependencies, retries, and schedules expressed as Directed Acyclic Graphs. Battle-tested in production data engineering at scale, with a rich ecosystem of operators, sensors, and integrations. More operationally involved than lightweight Python-native orchestrators, but offers the deepest control for complex dependency graphs, SLA tracking, and enterprise monitoring. Your report generation pipeline's outer scheduler — triggers LangGraph / Agent SDK runs on a cron or event.
DAG orchestrationenterprise standardyour pipelinecomplex dependencies
Choose:Airflow when you have complex cross-system dependencies, enterprise SLA requirements, or a data engineering team already running it  ·  when you want to move fast, keep everything in Python, and avoid Airflow's operational overhead
🤝Agent Teams — Supervisor / Worker Coordinationa supervisor agent routes tasks to specialist worker agents · sequential or parallel · role-based
Claude Agent Teams
Anthropic · Claude-native · supervisor / worker · Claude Agent SDK
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
DefaultMarket-map
Anthropic's native multi-agent coordination system. You define a team of Claude agents — each with a distinct role, toolset, and system prompt — and a supervisor agent coordinates task routing between them. The supervisor decides which specialist agent handles which part of a task, manages handoffs, and synthesises results. Built on the Claude Agent SDK so it inherits all SDK primitives: MCP tools, hooks, memory, and subagent delegation. The natural escalation path from a single Claude Agent SDK pipeline to a coordinated multi-agent system — no new runtime required. Claude-only.
Anthropic-nativesupervisor / workerrole-based agentsClaude-onlyAgent SDK
LangGraph Multi-Agent Supervisor
LangChain · LangGraph · model-agnostic · supervisor / worker pattern
Stablereviewed 2026-05-24
DefaultMarket-map
The LangGraph equivalent of Claude Agent Teams. A supervisor node — itself an LLM — sits at the top of a StateGraph and decides which worker subgraph to invoke next based on the current state. Each worker is a full LangGraph subgraph with its own tools, system prompt, and logic. The supervisor routes: "this task needs the DuckDB agent", "now the synthesis agent". Workers return results to shared TypedDict state; the supervisor reads state and decides next step or signals FINISH. Model-agnostic — supervisor and workers can use different models. Checkpointing via LangGraph Checkpointer makes the full supervisor loop resumable.
LangGraph-nativesupervisor / workermodel-agnosticStateGraphcheckpointable
Claude Agent Teams vs LangGraph Multi-Agent Supervisor
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
DimensionClaude Agent TeamsLangGraph Multi-Agent Supervisor
PatternSupervisor routes tasks to specialist agentsLLM supervisor node decides next worker subgraph
Model lock-inClaude-onlyModel-agnostic — supervisor and workers can differ
RuntimeClaude Agent SDKLangGraph StateGraph
ExecutionSequential or parallel depending on supervisorSequential by default · parallel via Send() fan-out
StateManaged via Agent SDK primitivesExplicit TypedDict — all agents read/write same schema
CheckpointingVia Agent SDKLangGraph Checkpointer — resumable from any node
Best forClaude-native · fast to build · no new runtimeModel-agnostic · complex routing logic · full state control
🌱Subagent Spawning — Dynamic Fan-outan agent spawns N subagents at runtime · parallel execution · results collected into shared state
Claude Subagent Spawning
Anthropic · Claude Agent SDK · Task() · dynamic subagent delegation
Stablereviewed 2026-05-24
DefaultMarket-map
The Claude Agent SDK's native mechanism for spawning subagents at runtime. A parent agent creates child agents dynamically using Task() — each child gets its own system prompt, toolset, and context. Children run concurrently and return results back to the parent, which synthesises them. Unlike Agent Teams (where a supervisor routes to pre-defined workers), subagent spawning is dynamic — the parent decides at runtime how many subagents to create and what each one does, based on the task at hand. The natural pattern for: decompose a complex task → spawn N specialist subagents → wait for results → synthesise. Claude Code uses this pattern natively via subagent delegation.
Claude-nativedynamic spawningTask() primitiveconcurrent childrenfan-out / fan-in
LangGraph Send() API
LangChain · LangGraph · fan-out primitive · model-agnostic
Stablereviewed 2026-05-24
DefaultMarket-map
LangGraph's equivalent of Claude's subagent spawning — the fan-out primitive that dispatches multiple parallel subgraph invocations from a single node at runtime. An orchestrator node calls Send() with a list of items, spawning one subgraph instance per item concurrently. Each spawned subgraph runs in parallel and writes results into the shared TypedDict state. Unlike the Multi-Agent Supervisor (which routes sequentially to pre-defined workers), Send() is dynamic fan-out — the number of subagents is determined at runtime from the data. Your report pipeline pattern: one orchestrator node calls Send() to fan out over DuckDB and OpenSearch subagents simultaneously, both write results to shared state, synthesis node reads both.
LangGraph-nativedynamic fan-outSend() primitiveparallel subgraphsyour pipeline
Claude Subagent Spawning vs LangGraph Send() — dynamic fan-out models
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
DimensionClaude Subagent SpawningLangGraph Send() API
PatternParent spawns N child agents at runtime via Task()Orchestrator node fans out N subgraph instances via Send()
Spawning mechanismTask() — Claude Agent SDK primitiveSend(node, state) — LangGraph primitive
Number of subagentsDecided at runtime by parent agentDecided at runtime from list length
ExecutionConcurrent — children run in parallelConcurrent — subgraphs run in parallel
Result collectionParent awaits all children, receives resultsAll subgraphs write into shared TypedDict state
Model lock-inClaude-onlyModel-agnostic
vs Agent TeamsDynamic — parent decides N at runtime; teams use pre-defined workersDynamic — Send() list length varies; supervisor routes to fixed workers
Your pipelineSpawn query + search + synthesis agents dynamically per report requestSend() fans out DuckDB + OpenSearch subgraphs simultaneously
Multi-agent orchestration patterns:DAG pipeline — Airflow schedules and triggers the full pipeline from outside  ·  Supervisor → workers — Claude Agent Teams · LangGraph Multi-Agent Supervisor route tasks to specialist agents  ·  Fan-out / fan-in — Claude subagent spawning (Task()) · LangGraph Send() dispatch N parallel agents at runtime  ·  Peer-to-peer — A2A Protocol for cross-ecosystem agent communication

LangGraph
LangChain · runtime · Deep Agents is built on this · model-agnostic
