The stateful orchestration runtime underneath Deep Agents. Provides graph primitives, nodes, edges, state, checkpointing, retries, and fan-out for reliable long-running agent workflows.
runtimeDeep Agents runs on thisstatefulmodel-agnostic
🌿Git Worktrees — Harness-Level Parallelism Primitivenative Git feature · N branches simultaneously · N Claude Code instances in parallel · near-zero overhead vs Docker · each worktree = isolated filesystem · all share .git history
What git worktrees are
A native Git feature that lets you check out multiple branches simultaneously into separate working directories from a single repository. Each worktree is an isolated filesystem — different branch, different working files — but all share the same .git history. No container overhead, no duplication of the repo.
In an agent context
Spin up N worktrees → run N Claude Code instances simultaneously, each in its own isolated working directory. Parallel autonomous agent runs without branch collision. The lightweight alternative to Docker containers per agent run — near-zero overhead, just filesystem isolation via Git. Useful for parallel autonomous agent coding tasks.
git worktree add ../feature-a  # agent 1 works here — isolated filesystem
git worktree add ../feature-b  # agent 2 works here — isolated filesystem
git worktree add ../bugfix-c   # agent 3 works here — all share .git history
vs Docker containers: worktrees isolate the filesystem only (near-zero overhead) · Docker isolates the full environment (medium overhead) · use worktrees for parallel coding tasks on the same machine · use Docker when you need full env isolation
🧠

5.6.6 Memory & Cognitive Layer

1 project + AWS-managed
Mem0
Mem0 AI
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
WatchMarket-map
Lightweight, embeddable memory middleware. Extracts and stores memories from conversations, makes them retrievable via API. Drops into any existing harness — LangGraph, Claude Agent SDK, Claude Code — without restructuring. Low overhead, practical fit.
embeddablelow overheadAPI
Memory architecture
working · session · semantic · episodic · procedural · prospective
Design memory by purpose and lifetime. Working state supports the current trajectory; session memory preserves continuity; semantic and episodic memory support durable recall; procedural memory stores how to act; prospective memory stores future obligations.
memory taxonomygovernance
Redis + DynamoDB state plane
hot state + durable workflow continuity
Use Redis for fast, reconstructable session state and caches. Use DynamoDB for checkpoints, idempotency, approvals, retry counters, and authoritative operational metadata.
RedisDynamoDB
☁️AWS-Managed Alternativeif you are AWS-native, AgentCore Memory can replace custom memory infrastructure
Short-term memory
Turn-by-turn context within a single session · agents maintain immediate context without users repeating themselves · in-session continuity
Long-term memory
Automatically extracts and stores user preferences, important facts, and session summaries across sessions · persistent knowledge retention · replaces Mem0 for AWS-native stacks
Episodic memory
Captures structured episodes: context + reasoning + actions + outcomes · a reflection agent analyzes patterns · agents learn from past experiences and apply insights to future similar tasks
AgentCore Memory vs Mem0:AgentCore Memory — fully managed · zero infrastructure · AWS-native · three memory tiers including episodic · replaces custom memory infrastructure for AWS stacks  ·  Mem0 — model-agnostic · self-hostable · lightweight · best if not AWS-native
📄

5.6.7 Document Processing & Ingestion

2 projects
AWS Textract
AWS · OCR + document understanding
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Goes beyond basic OCR — understands document structure: tables, forms, key-value pairs, checkboxes, multi-column layouts, and handwriting. Outputs structured JSON ready for chunking, embedding, and indexing into OpenSearch or pgvector. Ideal for financial reports, regulatory filings, and structured data documents. Fully managed, scales to millions of pages.
OCRtables + formsstructured JSONAWS-native
AWS Bedrock Data Automation
AWS · end-to-end multimodal ingestion
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
End-to-end multimodal document ingestion pipeline on AWS — Textract + classification + extraction + enrichment in one managed service. Handles PDFs, images, audio, and video. Outputs structured data directly into S3 for downstream embedding and indexing. The highest-level abstraction for document ingestion on AWS — less control than raw Textract, far less glue code.
multimodalmanaged pipelineaudio + videoAWS-native
Ingestion pipeline  ·  Raw docs (PDF · scan · DOCX · image) → Textract / Bedrock Data Automation (extract + structure) → Chunking + Embedding → OpenSearch / pgvector (index) → RAG (retrieve at agent query time)  ·  Typically a batch process run on a schedule via Airflow, not real-time
🗄️

5.6.8 Knowledge & Retrieval

12 projects + patterns
Evaluation feedback loop · RAGASMeasure retrieval and generation separately. Send queries, retrieved contexts, responses, references, and metadata into RAGAS to score context precision, context recall, faithfulness, response relevancy, and noise sensitivity. Use the results to tune chunking, embeddings, query rewriting, top-k, filters, reranking, and generation prompts. RAGAS evaluates the retrieval system; it is not itself a retrieval component.
🧭Vector DBs — split by retrieval shapevector + search · pure-play vector · S3-native vector storage
Vector + Search — hybrid lexical + semantic retrieval
OpenSearch
AWS / OpenSearch Project · your current stack
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Distributed search and analytics engine with first-class vector search support. k-NN with HNSW indexing for semantic similarity, hybrid search combining BM25 keyword + RRF score fusion, and dense/sparse retrieval in one index. Your existing RAG retrieval layer for financial documents. Scales from laptop to cluster.
vector searchhybrid BM25+RRFk-NN HNSWyour stack
pgvector
PostgreSQL extension
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
DefaultMarket-map
Vector similarity search as a Postgres extension. Store embeddings as columns, query with cosine/L2/dot-product operators, combine with standard SQL — joins, filters, transactions — all in one database. The simplest path if you're already on Postgres. No separate infra, no separate query language, no data sync problem.
postgres-nativeSQL + vectorszero extra infra
pgvectorscale
Timescale / Tiger Data · PostgreSQL vector scaling extension
Watchreviewed 2026-05-29
Strong optionMarket-map
Performance layer for larger pgvector workloads. Use it when you want to keep embeddings, metadata, filters, and transactional state inside Postgres but need better vector search economics at larger scale. Think of it as a scale complement to pgvector, not a replacement.
pgvector scale layerlarge embedding tablesPostgres-native RAG
Postgres-native BM25 / Hybrid Search — exact terms + semantic recall
pg_search
ParadeDB · PostgreSQL search extension
Watchreviewed 2026-05-29
Strong optionMarket-map
BM25-ranked search, faceting, and search-engine-style retrieval inside Postgres. Best fit when you want Elasticsearch-like lexical relevance without operating a separate OpenSearch/Elasticsearch cluster. Pair with pgvector for hybrid RAG.
BM25faceted searchhybrid retrieval
pg_textsearch
Timescale / Tiger Data · PostgreSQL text-search extension
Watchreviewed 2026-05-29
Strong optionMarket-map
BM25 keyword search that leans into Postgres text-search infrastructure. Best fit when exact terms, IDs, tickers, legal clauses, product names, and error messages need to survive retrieval alongside semantic vector search.
BM25keyword relevancePostgres FTS path
Vector (Pure-Play) — dedicated embedding stores
Pinecone
Pinecone · managed vector database
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Purpose-built managed vector database for high-scale similarity search. Strong fit when the vector layer should be a dedicated service with namespaces, metadata filtering, managed indexes, and low operational overhead rather than part of a broader search engine.
managed vector DBnamespacesmetadata filters
Weaviate
Weaviate · open-source vector database
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24

Open-source vector database with schema-aware objects, hybrid search, metadata filtering, and modular deployment options. Useful when you want a dedicated vector store that can run self-hosted or managed while keeping rich object metadata close to embeddings.
open sourcehybrid searchobject metadata
Chroma
Chroma · local-first vector database
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
Lightweight vector database popular for local development, prototypes, and small-to-medium RAG apps. Simple Python-native workflow, fast setup, and easy persistence make it a good early-stage option before promoting to managed vector infrastructure.
local-firstprototype-friendlyPython-native
Vector (S3-Native) — object-storage-backed embeddings
Amazon S3 Vectors
AWS · native vector storage
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
NicheMarket-map
AWS's native vector storage service — store and query embedding vectors directly in S3 without a separate vector DB. The lowest-cost option for vector storage at scale on AWS. Pairs naturally with the ingestion pipeline: process docs with Textract / Bedrock Data Automation, embed them, store vectors in S3 Vectors, then query via OpenSearch or directly. Purpose-built for high-volume, cost-sensitive RAG workloads.
AWS-nativelowest costS3-backedhigh volume
🕸️Graph Traversal — relationship-aware retrieval
Amazon Neptune
AWS
Stablereviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Managed graph database supporting both Property Graph (Gremlin, openCypher) and RDF (SPARQL). Purpose-built for traversing highly connected data — entity relationships, knowledge graphs, compliance webs. Neptune Analytics adds vector search on top of graph traversal, enabling GraphRAG patterns natively on AWS.
Neptune Database vs Neptune Analytics — two separate services: Neptune Database is the managed graph database (property graph via Gremlin/openCypher, RDF via SPARQL) for transactional workloads and real-time traversal. Neptune Analytics is a separate analytics service that adds in-memory graph algorithms, vector similarity search, and GraphRAG query patterns on top of a Neptune Database or a graph snapshot. For GraphRAG workloads on AWS, you typically need both: Neptune Database for the live graph and Neptune Analytics for vector-augmented traversal at query time. Check current pricing and regional availability — Neptune Analytics is billed separately from Neptune Database.
graph DBmanaged AWSGraphRAG-readyknowledge graph
✂️Chunking & Reranking — quality levers between ingestion and retrieval
Chunking strategies
How you split documents before embedding dramatically affects RAG quality — a chunk boundary in the middle of a key fact means neither chunk is useful on its own.

Fixed-size — split every N tokens, simple, fast, ignores structure
Recursive — split by paragraph → sentence → word, respects natural boundaries
Semantic — split where meaning shifts, requires an embedding model
Document-aware — extract structure first, then chunk by heading / table / section

Recommendation: document-aware for complex PDFs (section-level chunks), recursive for plain text.
Reranking
Vector search retrieves top-K candidates quickly but imprecisely. A reranker (cross-encoder) then re-scores them against the query with much higher accuracy — expensively but only over K candidates, not the full index.

Cohere Rerank — managed API, best out-of-box quality
BGE Reranker — open-source, self-hostable, strong multilingual
Cross-encoder reranking — the general pattern: retrieve 20, rerank to top 5, generate

The standard pattern in production RAG: vector search (recall) → rerank (precision) → generate.
GraphRAG chunking — different rules than standard RAG
Preserve entity boundaries
Standard fixed-size chunking splits on token count regardless of semantic boundaries. For GraphRAG, a chunk that splits mid-sentence loses the relation triple connecting two entities — the extraction step will miss or corrupt the relationship. Use sentence-boundary chunking for any corpus that will feed a graph extraction pipeline.
Keep co-referring entities together
Entity resolution works within a chunk. If "the company" appears several sentences after "Acme Corp," and a chunk boundary falls between them, the extractor loses the coreference. Use longer overlapping chunks (e.g. 512 tokens with 128 overlap) for extraction, even if you use smaller chunks for the parallel vector retrieval path.
Run two parallel chunking strategies
Optimal chunking for graph extraction and optimal chunking for vector retrieval are different. Use larger, boundary-aware chunks for extraction → load into graph. Use smaller semantic or sentence chunks for embedding → load into vector index. The same source document feeds both pipelines from a single ingestion run.
Attach source provenance to every chunk
Every graph node and relationship should carry a reference back to the source chunk it was extracted from. This enables GraphRAG to return not just the graph path but the exact text evidence behind each relationship — essential for explainability and for debugging extraction errors.
RAG & GraphRAG — patterns, not tools. RAG wires the retrieval layer into the agent: query → embed → search vector DB → retrieve chunks → generate. GraphRAG (Microsoft) extends this with graph traversal: builds a knowledge graph from documents, then combines graph hops + vector search for multi-hop reasoning. More expensive to build, dramatically better on relational questions. Embeddings are the foundation both depend on — text converted to vectors via a model (OpenAI text-embedding-3, Cohere, or local HuggingFace models).
📊

5.6.9 Observability & Tracing

6 projects · 4 concerns · Postgres query observability
Canonical treatment: owns traces, metrics, logs, feedback signals, and the operational joins between quality, latency, cost, and failures.
LLM-Native Tracing — what the model did
Langfuse
Langfuse GmbH · open source
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
DefaultMarket-map
Open-source, framework-agnostic observability. Traces, evals, prompt versioning, cost tracking. Works cleanly with LangGraph and Claude Agent SDK. The practical choice for existing pipelines. Self-hostable.
open sourcetracesevalsself-hostable
LangSmith
LangChain · SaaS-only
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
LangChain's native observability platform. Full graph visualization of node-by-node LangGraph execution — not just flat spans. Evals, datasets, prompt versioning, and human annotation built in. LangGraph Platform's default observability layer.
LangGraph-nativegraph vizevalsSaaS
Ops / Infrastructure — what the system did
Splunk
Splunk Inc. · enterprise log aggregation
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Enterprise log aggregation, search, and alerting. Where Langfuse/LangSmith understand model traces, Splunk ingests everything: server logs, agent stdout, pipeline errors, security events, infrastructure metrics. In an agent stack, Splunk is the ops observability layer — Langfuse tells you what the model did, Splunk tells you what the system did. Standard in enterprise environments where IT/security teams control observability.
log aggregationenterprisesecurity eventsalerting
Grafana
Grafana Labs · metrics & dashboards
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
The standard for metrics visualization and operational dashboards. Where Splunk is log-centric, Grafana is metrics-centric — latency, throughput, error rates, cost per run, agent success rates over time. Connects to Prometheus, CloudWatch, Datadog, kdb+, and others as data sources. Gives your agent pipeline the operational dashboard that Langfuse/LangSmith don't — real-time pipeline health, SLA monitoring, cost trend charts.
metricsdashboardsPrometheusSLA monitoring
Experiment Tracking — what you tried and learned
MLflow
Databricks · open source MLOps platform
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Platform for tracking ML experiments, managing model versions, and packaging/deploying models. In an agent stack: tracks prompt experiments and eval runs (overlaps with LangSmith), manages fine-tuned model versions used by agents, logs agent run metrics as "experiments." Most relevant if you're doing model fine-tuning alongside agent development or need a model registry. Self-hostable, integrates with Databricks, AWS SageMaker, and Azure ML.
experiment trackingmodel registryfine-tuningopen source
Three distinct concerns:LLM-native tracing (Langfuse · LangSmith) — what the model did, per token, per trace  ·  Ops / infrastructure (Splunk · Grafana) — what the system did, pipeline health, SLA  ·  Experiment tracking (MLflow) — what you tried, what worked, model versioning  ·  Evaluation scoring (RAGAS) — whether retrieval and responses were good; publish those scores into Langfuse, LangSmith, MLflow, or CloudWatch for segmentation and monitoring
🧪

5.6.10 Evaluation & Testing

5 tools · pre-production and continuous validation
The gap between "I built it" and "I know it works." Every other layer in this document is about building agents. Eval is about validating they produce correct, consistent, safe outputs — before production and continuously after. Observability (Langfuse/LangSmith) tells you what happened. Eval tells you whether it should have.
Model-Agnostic Evaluation Tools
Use these when you want portable prompt, RAG, and trajectory evals across models, frameworks, and CI pipelines.
PromptFoo
PromptFoo · open source · prompt testing framework
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
The most practical open-source tool for testing prompts and agent outputs. Define test cases in YAML — input, expected output, assertion — and run them against your prompts or agent pipelines. Supports LLM-as-judge assertions, regex, JSON schema validation, and custom evaluators. Integrates with CI (GitHub Actions) so prompt regressions are caught before merge. The test-driven agent loop's testing framework. Self-hostable, model-agnostic.
prompt testingCI integrationLLM-as-judgeopen sourceYAML test cases
DeepEval
Confident AI · open source · agent eval framework
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Comprehensive LLM evaluation framework — covers correctness, faithfulness, hallucination, contextual relevance, answer relevance, and custom metrics. Designed specifically for RAG pipelines and agent outputs. Includes a test runner, CI integration, and a dashboard for tracking eval scores over time. Particularly strong for validating RAG quality: does the retrieved context support the answer? Is the answer faithful to the retrieved docs? Directly relevant for your OpenSearch/DuckDB pipeline.
RAG evaluationfaithfulnesshallucination detectionopen sourceCI integration
RAGAS
Open source · RAG and LLM application evaluation framework
Currentreviewed 2026-06-27
Strong optionMarket-map
Metric-oriented evaluation framework for retrieval and generation pipelines. Measures context precision, context recall, faithfulness, response relevancy, noise sensitivity, factual correctness, tool-call accuracy, and agent-goal accuracy. Best suited for component-level RAG benchmarking, experiment comparison, dataset-based regression evaluation, and diagnosing whether failures originate in retrieval or generation.
RAG evaluationretrieval metricsfaithfulnessagent metricsdataset evaluation
AgentEvals
Anthropic · agent-specific evaluation toolkit
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Anthropic's toolkit for evaluating agentic behaviours — not just output quality but decision quality: did the agent choose the right tool, in the right order, with appropriate confidence? Includes golden trajectory datasets (correct sequences of tool calls for a given task), trajectory comparison metrics, and HITL annotation workflows. Complements PromptFoo (which tests outputs) with trajectory-level evaluation of the agent's reasoning and action sequence.
trajectory evaluationgolden datasetstool call qualityAnthropic
AWS AgentCore Managed Evaluations
Dedicated subsection for AgentCore-native evaluation capabilities, especially when the agent runtime, traces, and observability are already on Bedrock / CloudWatch.
Amazon Bedrock AgentCore Evaluations
AWS · AgentCore · managed evaluation service
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
AWS-managed evaluation for agent stacks, especially AWS-native Bedrock / AgentCore deployments. Covers correctness, faithfulness, safety, tool selection accuracy, edge case handling, output reliability, custom scoring, and trace/session/span-level evaluation. Best fit when you want managed evals in CloudWatch alongside AgentCore Observability rather than wiring PromptFoo + DeepEval yourself. Complements AgentEvals for trajectory-level golden-path evaluation.
managed evalsAWS-nativetrace evaluationsafetytool accuracy
Golden Dataset Strategy
What a golden dataset is
A curated set of (input, expected output) pairs representing correct agent behaviour. For your pipeline: sample report requests + the correct queries, retrieved chunks, and final report structure. Used to catch regressions when you change prompts, models, or retrieval strategies.
Eval in the CI loop
PromptFoo runs eval suites on PR → GitHub Actions blocks merge if scores drop below threshold → RAGAS benchmarks context precision, recall, faithfulness, and answer quality across datasets → DeepEval runs application-level regression tests → AgentEvals checks tool-use trajectories → Langfuse or LangSmith tracks production scores and failed examples over time. AgentCore Evaluations can replace or complement this loop for AWS-native stacks with managed trace/session/span evaluation in CloudWatch.
🐘Postgres Query ObservabilitySQL performance · retrieval latency · index behavior
pg_stat_statements
PostgreSQL contrib module · query statistics
Stablereviewed 2026-05-29
DefaultMarket-map
Tracks normalized SQL planning and execution statistics across the database. For agentic systems, use it to find slow vector search, bad metadata filters, expensive hybrid retrieval joins, missing indexes, high-call-count tool queries, and regressions after prompt/tool changes.
query statsretrieval tuningindex diagnosisproduction default
💾

5.6.11 Agent State & Persistence

10 projects + taxonomy
Canonical treatment: owns run state, checkpoints, resumability, persistence boundaries, and the separation of working state from governed memory.
⚡Low-latency State StoresRedis + DynamoDB · session state · cache · coordination · config
Redis
Redis Ltd · in-memory data store
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
DefaultMarket-map
Fast in-memory store for agent working state during a run. Pub/sub for inter-agent messaging and coordination. Shared scratchpad across subagents during fan-out execution. Also useful for rate limiting and caching LLM responses.
in-memorypub/subephemeral statecaching
Amazon DynamoDB
AWS · fully managed · serverless KV + document · zero infrastructure
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
AWS's fully managed, serverless key-value and document store. Zero infrastructure — no cluster to provision, no connection pool to manage, no instance to right-size. Create a table and start writing. Scales automatically to any throughput and pays per request, making it significantly cheaper than an always-on Redis cluster for bursty agent workloads that run on a schedule and sit idle in between. Millisecond latency at any scale. Integrates natively with IAM, Lambda, and the rest of the AWS ecosystem. Best for: serverless agent session state, agent configuration store (model settings, prompt versions, per-user preferences), rate limiting and deduplication with atomic counters, and high-throughput tool call result caching where simple KV lookups outperform PostgreSQL. The natural complement to DocumentDB in an AWS-native agent stack — DocumentDB for variable-schema artifacts, DynamoDB for high-throughput KV.
serverlesszero infraAWS-nativepay-per-requestKV + document
🧱Relational + Analytical DB Sub-layerPostgreSQL + Redshift · durable SQL state · checkpoints · warehouse-backed agent queries
PostgreSQL
PostgreSQL Global Dev Group
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
DefaultMarket-map
Durable checkpoint storage for long-running agent runs. LangGraph's default checkpointer backend — stores graph state snapshots so interrupted runs can resume exactly. Also the home for structured agent output, audit logs, and run history. Pairs with pgvector for a single-DB retrieval + persistence stack.
durable storagecheckpointerrun historyaudit logs
Amazon Redshift
AWS · cloud data warehouse · analytical SQL
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Columnar analytical database for warehouse-backed agent workflows. Use Redshift as a durable SQL source for enterprise facts, metrics, audit/report tables, and large historical datasets that agents query through tools. It belongs with PostgreSQL in the SQL database family, but serves analytical / OLAP workloads rather than low-latency session state. Not a replacement for Redis or DynamoDB; it is the database source an agent reaches for when the question needs governed warehouse data.
data warehouseanalytical SQLOLAPAWS-native
LangGraph Checkpointer
LangChain · built into LangGraph
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
DefaultMarket-map
Built-in state snapshotting for LangGraph runs. Captures full graph state at each node — interrupted or failed runs resume from the last checkpoint rather than restarting. Backends: in-memory (dev), PostgreSQL (production), SQLite (local). The difference between a fragile prototype and a resilient pipeline.
LangGraph-nativeresumable runssnapshot
State stores and SQL databases are separated because they solve different persistence concerns:Redis — microsecond latency · in-memory · ephemeral by default · best for working state during a run  ·  DynamoDB — millisecond latency · durable · serverless · best for session state between runs and agent config that needs to persist  ·  PostgreSQL / Redshift — SQL databases for durable checkpoints, structured output, audit logs, and warehouse-backed analytical context  ·  TimescaleDB / kdb+ — time-series stores for telemetry, latency, usage, cost trends, and high-frequency event analytics
📈Time-Series DB Sub-layeragent telemetry · latency curves · cost/run trends · high-frequency event analytics
Time-Series DBs slot in as the temporal analytics character in the persistence layer — where Redis handles hot working state, PostgreSQL/Redshift handle relational and analytical SQL, and Document DBs handle variable-schema artifacts, time-series stores handle timestamped operational signals: latency curves, usage and cost-per-run trends, high-frequency events, telemetry streams, anomaly windows, and historical metrics that need efficient time-based queries.
TimescaleDB
Timescale · PostgreSQL extension
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Time-series database built on PostgreSQL. Good fit when you want SQL-native time-series analytics, joins with relational run metadata, retention policies, and continuous aggregates without adding a completely separate query model.
Postgres-nativetime-series SQLcontinuous aggregatesretention policies
kdb+ / KX
KX · high-performance time-series / vector analytics database
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Columnar, in-memory/time-series database optimized for high-frequency data, streaming analytics, historical event analysis, and market-data-style workloads. In an agent stack, use kdb as the analytical substrate for timestamped facts: prices, ticks, traces, run telemetry, cost curves, latency windows, anomaly detection, and simulation outputs. Pairs with OpenSearch/pgvector for semantic retrieval and PostgreSQL/Redis for durable app state.
time-seriesmarket datastreaming analyticsagent telemetryq queries
📄Document DB Sub-layerschema-flexible persistent storage · variable-shape agent artifacts · not relational, not ephemeral
Document DBs slot in as a third character in the persistence layer — where Redis is ephemeral and PostgreSQL is relational, document DBs handle variable-schema agent artifacts: run outputs with different shapes per run, cached tool results, richer agent memory documents, conversation history at scale, prompt templates and agent configs. Natural fit for things agents produce that don't fit cleanly into relational tables.
MongoDB
MongoDB Inc. · document database · Atlas cloud
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Default document database for variable-schema agent artifacts. Use it for generated reports, tool-call results, cached external API responses, richer agent memory documents, prompt templates, and conversation history that changes shape from run to run. Atlas Vector Search can support lightweight retrieval inside the same store, while MongoDB can also serve as a LangGraph checkpointer backend when you want flexible document-shaped state rather than strictly relational checkpoints.
document DBflexible schemaAtlas vector searchLangGraph backend
Amazon DocumentDB
AWS · managed · MongoDB-compatible
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
AWS-managed document database with full MongoDB API compatibility — same query language, same driver, zero cluster management. The natural choice for AWS-native stacks (Textract · Neptune · S3 Vectors · Bedrock · OpenSearch · EKS) that need document storage without operational overhead. Use it where you'd use MongoDB but want it managed inside your existing AWS account, IAM policies, and VPC. Integrates with AWS Secrets Manager and CloudWatch natively.
AWS-nativeMongoDB-compatiblemanagedyour stack
🗄 Full DB Taxonomy — which storage type for which agent concern
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
DB TypeExamplesLayerAgent use case
In-memory KVRedisState & PersistenceSession state · caching · pub/sub · rate limiting
Relational / analytical SQLPostgreSQL · Amazon RedshiftState & PersistenceCheckpoints · audit logs · run history · structured output · governed warehouse-backed context
DocumentMongoDB · DocumentDBState & PersistenceVariable-schema run artifacts · generated reports · agent memory · conversation history at scale
Key-Value (durable)DynamoDBState & PersistenceServerless session state · agent config · rate limiting · deduplication · pay-per-request · zero infra
Time-seriesTimescaleDB · kdb+State & PersistenceAgent telemetry · cost-per-run · latency over time · high-frequency event analytics · Grafana data source
Vector + searchOpenSearch · pgvectorKnowledge & RetrievalHybrid BM25 + vector · your primary stack
Vector (pure-play)Pinecone · Weaviate · ChromaKnowledge & RetrievalDedicated embedding store · no BM25 overhead · prototyping
Vector (S3-native)S3 VectorsKnowledge & RetrievalHigh-volume embeddings · lowest cost on AWS
GraphAmazon NeptuneKnowledge & RetrievalEntity relationships · multi-hop reasoning · GraphRAG
⚙️

5.6.12 Infrastructure / Stack

12 projects + CI/CD + Postgres ops
🐘Postgres Infrastructure Extensionsscheduler · scale-out · OLAP · ML-in-DB
pg_cron
Citus Data / PostgreSQL extension
Stablereviewed 2026-05-29
DefaultMarket-map
Cron-style scheduler inside Postgres. Use it for refresh jobs, retention cleanup, embedding backfills, materialized-view refreshes, stale-document audits, rollups, and lightweight eval batches. It is not a full workflow orchestrator; keep complex DAGs in Airflow, Dagster, or Temporal.
schedulerSQL jobsmaintenance
Citus
Microsoft / Citus Data · distributed Postgres
Currentreviewed 2026-05-29
Strong optionMarket-map
Horizontal scale-out for Postgres through distributed tables and sharding. Best when tenant_id, customer_id, account_id, or workspace_id is a natural distribution key and you want documents, chunks, runs, and tool events colocated per tenant.
distributed SQLtenant shardingscale-out Postgres
pg_duckdb
DuckDB / Hydra / MotherDuck · PostgreSQL analytics extension
Watchreviewed 2026-05-29
NicheMarket-map
Embeds DuckDB-style analytical execution into Postgres. Useful for columnar scans, Parquet/lakehouse analysis, feature exploration, and heavy aggregates near operational data. Treat it as an OLAP accelerator, not a replacement for OLTP query discipline.
OLAPDuckDB inside PostgresParquet analytics
pgml / PostgresML
PostgresML · in-database ML
Watchreviewed 2026-05-29
NicheMarket-map
Runs selected ML, inference, and embedding workflows close to Postgres data. Useful for controlled internal workloads and data-local inference, but avoid turning the primary OLTP database into a high-throughput model-serving tier unless the platform is explicitly designed for it.
in-database MLembeddingsdata-local inference
Container Infrastructure — the execution substrate
Docker
Docker Inc. · container runtime
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
The foundational container primitive. Packages your agent pipeline — harness, dependencies, environment — into a reproducible, portable artifact. Every other tool in this group runs on top of it. In an agent context, Docker also isolates Claude Code headless runs and subagent execution environments. The starting point before you need orchestration.
containersfoundationisolationportable
Kubernetes
CNCF · container orchestration
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Container orchestration runtime. Where Docker runs one container, Kubernetes runs thousands — scheduling, scaling, self-healing, load balancing. In an agent stack, K8s manages the fleet of containers running your LangGraph workers, FastAPI endpoints, Airflow schedulers, and agent processes. The production substrate everything runs on at scale.
orchestrationscalingself-healingproduction fleet
EKS
AWS · managed Kubernetes
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
AWS's managed Kubernetes service. Same as K8s but AWS handles the control plane — upgrades, availability, node management. The natural production runtime for AWS-native agent stacks (Textract, Neptune, S3 Vectors, Bedrock, OpenSearch). You write K8s, AWS runs it. Removes the hardest operational overhead of self-managed Kubernetes.
managed K8sAWS-nativeyour stackmanaged control plane
Temporal
Temporal Technologies · durable execution
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Durable execution runtime — what you put under LangGraph for crash-safe, long-running agent workflows. Handles retries, timeouts, and exactly-once execution automatically. If your report generation pipeline runs for minutes or hours, Temporal ensures it completes even if a node crashes mid-run.
durable executioncrash-saferetriesexactly-once
Agent-Specific Runtime — Amazon Bedrock AgentCore9 modular services · any framework · any model
Amazon Bedrock AgentCore
AWS · fully managed agentic platform · 2025 · model-agnostic · any framework
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
AWS's fully managed platform for building, deploying, and operating production agents. Works with any framework (LangGraph, CrewAI, Strands, Google ADK, OpenAI Agents SDK) and any model (Claude, GPT, Gemini, Nova, Llama, Mistral). The modular services below can be used together or independently, and each maps to a specific layer of the agent stack.
⚙️ Runtime
Serverless agent hosting · fast cold starts · true session isolation · async agents · bidirectional streaming · MCP + A2A support
→ Infrastructure layer
🧠 Memory
Short-term (in-session) · Long-term (cross-session: preferences, facts, summaries) · Episodic (reflection agent learns from past episodes)
→ Memory layer
🔌 Gateway
Converts APIs, Lambda, and existing services into MCP-compatible tools · connects to pre-existing MCP servers · Salesforce, Zoom, JIRA, Slack
→ MCP Protocol layer
🔐 Identity
Agent identity, access, and authentication · compatible with Cognito, Okta, Azure Entra ID, Auth0 · no user migration needed
→ Security & Governance layer
💻 Code Interpreter
Isolated sandbox for agent code execution · Python, JavaScript, TypeScript · enhances accuracy on complex computational tasks
→ Harness / Tools layer
🌐 Browser
Managed cloud browser runtime · agents navigate sites, fill forms, extract information · Playwright and BrowserUse compatible
→ Harness / Tools layer
📊 Observability
Unified trace, debug, and monitor view · step-by-step execution visualization · OTEL-compatible · integrates with any observability stack
→ Observability layer
🧪 Evaluations
13 built-in evaluators · correctness, safety, tool selection accuracy · custom scoring · CloudWatch dashboard · pre and post-deployment
→ Eval & Testing layer
🛡️ Policy
Natural language → Cedar policies · intercepts every tool call via Gateway · deterministic control · no agent reasoning loop slowdown
→ Security & Governance layer
9 modular servicesany frameworkany modelAWS-nativezero infra
Production path on AWS:Option A — DIY but controlled: Docker → EKS → LangGraph workers + FastAPI + Airflow containerised, K8s handles scaling  ·  Option B — Managed and fast: AgentCore → handles memory · tools · sessions · guardrails as a service → you focus on agent logic, not infrastructure
CI / CD — the automated feedback loop
GitHub Actions
GitHub / Microsoft · CI/CD · DevOps automation
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
The automated pipeline that runs when code is pushed, PRs are opened, or schedules fire. In an agent stack it plays two distinct roles. Agent-triggered CI: the agent writes code and opens a PR → Actions runs tests automatically → results feed back to the agent → agent iterates. The feedback loop that makes autonomous coding agents useful in production. Agent-as-Action: Claude Code or a LangGraph pipeline runs inside a GitHub Actions workflow as a CI step — claude --headless triggered by a PR comment or a label. The agent is the CI. Pairs with Codex (which opens PRs automatically) to close the full autonomous development loop.
CI/CDDevOpsagent feedback loopagent-as-actionGitHub-native
Two roles in an agent stack:Agent-triggered CI — agent writes code · opens PR · Actions runs tests · results feed back to agent · agent iterates  ·  Agent-as-Action — Claude Code / LangGraph runs inside a workflow step · claude --headless triggered by PR comment or label
🛡️

5.6.13 Security, Governance & Trust

3 projects · 5 concerns
Not one thing — five distinct concerns that fire in sequence. Conflating them is the most common mistake in production agent systems.
① Entitlements
What a given user or role is allowed to ask the agent to do. The claim layer — "User A can run reports but not modify schema." Fires first, before any agent logic runs.
② RBAC
Who can invoke which agents, skills, slash commands, or MCP tools. Maps roles to capabilities — analyst role can call query agent but not write agent. Implemented at harness level via tool permission gating.
③ Policies
Behavioural rules governing what the agent is allowed to do — distinct from guardrails (content) and RBAC (access). "May only read, not write." "Must request human approval before deleting." Capability restrictions on the agent itself.
④ Guardrails
Content safety at the input/output boundary. Validates what goes in and what comes out — blocks prompt injection, PII leakage, harmful outputs, off-topic responses. Fires at hook points (PreToolUse · PostToolUse · Stop).
⑤ Filtering
Data-level restriction on what the agent can see and return — distinct from all above: this is about data scope, not behavioural scope. Row-level security on DuckDB, index-level access control on OpenSearch, response redaction of fields the user isn't entitled to see.
User request → ① Entitlements → ② RBAC → ③ Policies → Agent executes → ④ Guardrails → ⑤ Filtering → Response
Tools — enforcement implementations
Guardrails AI
Guardrails AI Inc. · open source + managed · 2023
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Open-source and managed validation framework for LLM applications. Use it for validators-as-code around agent inputs and outputs: schema enforcement, structured-output validation, PII detection, off-topic blocking, unsafe-content checks, hallucination/grounding checks, and prompt-injection prevention. In this stack it belongs at the guardrail hook points — before model/tool execution for input checks and after model/tool execution for output validation — and complements Bedrock Guardrails rather than replacing it.
input guardrailsoutput guardrailsvalidators-as-codeschema validationPII detectionprompt injection
Amazon Bedrock Guardrails
AWS · managed · zero infrastructure · AWS-native content safety
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
AWS's fully managed content safety and policy enforcement service for LLM applications. Sits in front of and behind model calls — filtering inputs and outputs against configurable policies: topic denial, content filters (hate, violence, sexual, insults), PII detection and redaction, grounding checks (hallucination detection against a source document), and word/phrase blocklists. Zero infrastructure — configure policies in the console or SDK and attach to any Claude on Bedrock call. Native to the AWS ecosystem: integrates with Bedrock Agents, IAM, CloudWatch, and Bedrock-hosted Claude. For AWS-native stacks, replaces the need for self-hosted content safety classifiers. Pair with Guardrails AI for custom business logic and schema validation — Bedrock Guardrails handles the managed safety layer, Guardrails AI handles the custom validation layer.
AWS-managedcontent safetyPII redactiongrounding checkszero infrastructure
OPA — Open Policy Agent
CNCF · open source · policy-as-code engine
Auditreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
The cloud-native standard for policy enforcement. Policies are written in Rego — a declarative language — and evaluated against structured input (agent request, user role, resource being accessed). Handles RBAC, ABAC, entitlements, and any other rule-based authorization decision. Already the standard in Kubernetes, API gateways, and microservices — the same engine applied to agent stacks. An agent harness asks OPA "can this role invoke this MCP tool on this resource?" and OPA returns allow/deny with a reason. Decouples policy definition from policy enforcement — policies live in a repo, not in application code.
policy-as-codeRBACABACentitlementsCNCF standardRego
⚠️ Agent-Specific Threats
Prompt Injection Defense
The attack vector specific to agents — malicious content in tool results, retrieved documents, or external data hijacks agent behaviour by embedding instructions the agent follows as if they were from the user or system prompt.

Input sanitisation — strip instruction-like patterns from tool results before the agent sees them
Output validation — Guardrails AI post-tool checks catch hijacked outputs before they propagate
Sandboxed tool execution — limit blast radius via RBAC and OPA (already in this layer)
Principle of least privilege — agents only have access to tools they need for the current task
Bedrock Guardrails — managed content filter catches adversarial instructions in tool results
Agent Audit Trail
Distinct from observability (debugging) — an audit trail is for compliance. Every action an agent takes, who authorised it, what data it accessed, what it changed, and when.

PostgreSQL — immutable append-only audit log table, already in the stack
Hooks (PostToolUse) — every tool call writes an audit event before returning
OPA decision log — every policy decision (allow/deny) is logged with context
Splunk — centralised audit log aggregation for security teams

Connects to: HITL (human approval = audit event) · Entitlements (who authorised what)
How they connect to the existing stack:Hooks — natural injection point for Guardrails AI and Bedrock Guardrails checks (PreToolUse · PostToolUse · Stop)  ·  MCP Tools — OPA gates which MCP servers a given role can call  ·  AgentCore Identity — AWS-managed authentication compatible with Cognito, Okta, Azure Entra, Auth0  ·  AgentCore Policy — natural language → Cedar policies intercepting every tool call; AWS-native alternative to OPA for Bedrock stacks  ·  OpenSearch / DuckDB — Filtering applied at query time · row-level security · field redaction  ·  Langfuse / Splunk — every policy violation should be traced · denied actions are observable events
📡

5.6.14 Generative UI & UI Protocols

4 protocols/specs · agent↔UI communication
Generative UI is the umbrella layer for agent-created interface surfaces. Where MCP connects agents to tools (vertical) and A2A connects agents to agents (horizontal), Generative UI & UI Protocols connect agents to user interfaces — the last mile of the agent communication stack. AG-UI streams what the agent is doing, A2UI describes what to render, MCP-UI carries UI through MCP contexts, and Open-JSON-UI gives teams a portable JSON contract for generated interfaces.
AG-UI Protocol
Anthropic / community · emerging open standard · agent event streaming
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
WatchMarket-map
Agent-User Interaction Protocol — streams intermediate steps, tool call events, state updates, and final outputs to the frontend in a structured way. The agent event pipeline — what the agent is doing, as it's doing it. Every tool call, reasoning step, and state change can be surfaced in real time to any frontend that speaks the protocol. The MCP equivalent for agent↔UI communication. Complements A2UI: AG-UI handles the event stream, A2UI handles the rendered output.
event streamingagent↔UI protocolopen standardAnthropic / community
A2UI Protocol
Google · Apache 2.0 · v0.9.1 current spec · CopilotKit design partner
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
WatchMarket-map
Agent-to-UI protocol — agents generate declarative component descriptions that clients render natively across web, mobile, and desktop, without executing arbitrary code. Where AG-UI streams what the agent is doing, A2UI describes what to render. Agents send a flat streaming JSON structure describing UI surfaces, components, and data bindings; the client renders using its own native widgets (Angular, Flutter, React, Lit). Secure by design — agents can only use pre-approved components from a catalog, no UI injection attacks. Native MCP integration (A2UI over MCP, MCP Apps in A2UI surfaces) and uses A2A as a transport. Has an explicit integration guide for use alongside AG-UI. Spec moved from v0.8 (now legacy) to v0.9.1 as catalog negotiation and other breaking changes landed — pin a version explicitly rather than assuming compatibility across releases.
UI generationdeclarative componentsGoogle · Apache 2.0v0.9.1 currentMCP + A2A native
MCP-UI
MCP ecosystem · UI resources over MCP
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
DefaultMarket-map
Pattern for delivering interactive UI surfaces through MCP tool/resource contexts. Use it when the agent needs a trusted tool to return more than text or JSON — for example forms, previews, charts, file pickers, or embedded task panes. Belongs under Generative UI because it is about rendering agent/tool output, and cross-links to MCP because the transport and discovery model come from the MCP layer.
MCP-native UItool-returned surfacesinteractive resourcestrusted rendering
Open-JSON-UI
Open declarative UI pattern · JSON interface schema
Watchreviewed 2026-05-24
WatchMarket-map
Portable JSON contract for describing UI components, layouts, data bindings, and actions without letting the model emit arbitrary executable code. Use it as the interchange format behind generated forms, dashboards, inspection panes, and workflow UIs. Fits next to A2UI: A2UI defines an agent-to-UI protocol pattern; Open-JSON-UI is the schema-style representation teams can validate, diff, test, and render across clients.
JSON schemadeclarative UIportable renderersafe components
Generative UI map — complementary, not competing
AG-UI — the event stream
Streams what the agent is doing to the frontend
Analogy: HTTP — the transport layer
Output: Event stream (tool_call · state_delta · message)
Renderer: Any frontend that handles events
Use for: Live tool call display · intermediate reasoning · streaming progress
A2UI — the rendered output
Describes what the agent wants to render
Analogy: HTML — the content layer
Output: Declarative component tree (surfaces · components · bindings)
Renderer: Angular · Flutter · React · Lit · native mobile
Use for: Rich interactive UIs · charts · forms · maps · secure by design
Protocol map:   MCP — agent ↔ tool (vertical)  ·  A2A — agent ↔ agent (horizontal)  ·  AG-UI / A2UI / MCP-UI / Open-JSON-UI — agent ↔ UI (last mile)  ·  A2UI uses A2A as a transport, MCP-UI uses MCP contexts, and Open-JSON-UI gives renderers a validated schema — all compose
🔀

5.6.15 Protocols & Standards

2 protocols + MCP Apps
Cross-cutting standards — not a single layer, but the communication interfaces between layers. Together they form the full agent communication stack: MCP handles vertical agent↔tool communication, A2A handles horizontal agent↔agent communication, and MCP Apps package MCP-connected tools/resources into usable application surfaces.
↕️ MCP — Model Context Protocol  ·  agent ↔ tool  ·  vertical  ·  Anthropic · open standard
MCP — Model Context Protocol
Anthropic · open standard · production-ready
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
DefaultMarket-map
The standardised way an agent calls external tools — databases, APIs, browsers, file systems. Before MCP, every tool needed a bespoke integration for every agent. MCP makes tool connections plug-and-play: each tool exposes a typed interface, each agent speaks the same protocol. Already implicit throughout this stack — DuckDB MCP, Power BI MCP, GitHub MCP, OpenSearch MCP are all MCP servers. The USB-C of agent tooling. Vertical: agent calls down to tool, result returns up.
agent ↔ toolverticalAnthropic-ledopen standardproduction-readyUSB-C analogy
MCP Apps
MCP ecosystem · app surfaces over tool/resource servers
Stablereviewed 2026-05-24
DefaultMarket-map
Application pattern on top of MCP: instead of exposing only callable tools, an MCP server can support richer workflows, resources, and app-like surfaces that the host can present to users. Best treated as an MCP-native app/runtime surface, not as a peer of A2UI. It bridges the MCP Protocol layer and the Generative UI layer when tool outputs need interactive rendering.
MCP-native appstool/resource surfacesagent-hosted workflowsbridges to UI
Sits between: Harness layer → Tools  ·  In primitives: MCP Tools is the harness-level primitive that calls MCP servers
🔌 APIs & CLIs → MCP  ·  API / CLI vs MCP — not three things in competition, two categories with a clear boundary
The correct framing: API / CLI vs MCP
APIs and CLIs are the same category — raw interfaces. A CLI is an API you call via stdin/stdout. A REST API is a CLI you call via HTTP. Both require the caller to know exactly what to call and how. Neither has a schema an agent can introspect at runtime.

MCP is a different category — a typed, discoverable protocol layer built on top of APIs and CLIs. MCP wraps them; it doesn't replace them. The underlying service still has its API or CLI. MCP is the agent-facing interface on top.
REST / GraphQL API
HTTP interface to a service
Caller: any HTTP client · humans · code
Output: JSON / XML / HTML
Schema: OpenAPI (optional)
Discoverable: via OpenAPI docs
Agent-ready: partially — needs wrapper
→ wrapped by MCP
CLI Tool
Shell interface to a service
Caller: humans in terminal · scripts
Output: human-readable text
Schema: man pages (informal)
Discoverable: no
Agent-ready: no — needs wrapper
→ wrapped by MCP
MCP Server ★
Typed agent interface wrapping either
Caller: agents via MCP protocol
Output: structured typed responses
Schema: formal typed schema (required)
Discoverable: yes — agent lists tools
Agent-ready: natively
→ wraps API or CLI underneath
Agent
  ↓  MCP Protocol
MCP Server(typed · discoverable · observable)
  ↓  calls internally
REST API / GraphQL / gRPC / CLI
  ↓  talks to
The actual service (OpenSearch · DuckDB · Power BI · GitHub · Docker)
Common wrappers — APIs & CLIs being served via MCP
CLI → MCP:
git — repo ops · commits · diffs
docker — build · run · logs
kubectl — K8s cluster control
aws — AWS CLI for agents
gh — GitHub · PRs · issues
terraform — infra provisioning
API → MCP:
OpenSearch REST API → OpenSearch MCP
GitHub REST API → GitHub MCP
Power BI API → Power BI MCP
DuckDB API → DuckDB MCP
Slack API → Slack MCP
Notion API → Notion MCP
The trend: every major API is getting an MCP wrapper — the same way every major service got a REST API in the 2010s. Some services now ship their own native MCP server so you never touch their raw API as an agent. Others still require you to either call the API directly via a tool function (raw · fragile) or write a thin MCP wrapper (recommended).
↔️ A2A — Agent-to-Agent Protocol  ·  agent ↔ agent  ·  horizontal  ·  Linux Foundation · open standard
A2A — Agent-to-Agent Protocol
Originated by Google · Linux Foundation–governed since June 2025 · production-ready, 150+ supporting orgs
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
DefaultMarket-map
Open protocol for cross-ecosystem agent interoperability, originated by Google and donated to the Linux Foundation in June 2025, with AWS, Cisco, Microsoft, Salesforce, SAP, and ServiceNow collaborating on its governance. Lets agents from different platforms — Anthropic, LangChain, Google, third-party — delegate to each other without bespoke integrations. Right now your stack is single-ecosystem (Anthropic/LangGraph). A2A becomes relevant when you want to delegate to a specialised Google Vertex AI agent, accept handoffs from third-party financial data agents, or operate in an enterprise where different teams have built agents on different platforms. Horizontal: agent delegates across to another agent, regardless of ecosystem. The TCP/IP of agent communication. Adoption moved quickly: by its one-year mark the project reported 150+ supporting organizations and active production deployments across multiple industries.
agent ↔ agenthorizontalLinux Foundationopen standardproduction-readyTCP/IP analogy
Sits between: Orchestrator layer → Harness layer  ·  Relevant when: cross-vendor agent delegation · enterprise multi-team agent interop · Vertex AI ↔ Claude handoffs  ·  Complements MCP: MCP is vertical (agent→tool) · A2A is horizontal (agent→agent)

DimensionMem0AgentCore Memory
TypeEmbeddable memory middlewareManaged memory service
InfrastructureSelf-host or managed APIZero — fully managed AWS
OverheadLow — drops inZero — API calls only
Short-term memoryVia context managementNative — in-session continuity
Long-term memoryNative — cross-session KVNative — auto-extracts preferences + facts
Episodic memoryNoNative — reflection agent learns from past episodes
Model-agnosticYesBedrock / AWS only
Self-hostableYesNo — AWS-managed only
AWS-nativeNoYes — IAM · CloudWatch · AgentCore
Best forAny stack · lightweight cross-session memoryAWS-native stacks · zero infra · three memory tiers out of the box
Choose whenNot on AWS · model-agnostic · want to self-hostAlready AWS-native · want managed memory without building it
// Claude Code's relationship to every other project
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
ProjectRelationship to Claude Code
StreamlitUI wrapper that surfaces Claude Code agent outputs as an interactive dashboard
CursorIDE harness — same category as Claude Code but GUI-first and multi-model rather than CLI and Claude-only
GitHub CopilotIDE harness — enterprise default, deeper GitHub integration, less autonomous than Claude Code or Cursor
CodexCloud coding agent — OpenAI's equivalent of Claude Managed Agents · async · parallel tasks · no local interface
FlaskAPI gateway — exposes Claude Code / Agent SDK pipelines as HTTP endpoints consumed by any UI or system
FastAPIModern async API gateway — same role as Flask but native async fits LangGraph + Agent SDK natively
Next.js + ReactProduction UI layer streaming Claude Code / Agent SDK responses to end users
CopilotKitReact-first app-layer framework for embedding copilots and agent workflows in product UIs
AG-UI ProtocolThe standard for how Claude Code agent events stream to any frontend
A2UI ProtocolAgents generate declarative UI components the frontend renders — complements AG-UI · uses A2A as transport · MCP-native
MCP-UIInteractive surfaces returned through MCP tool/resource contexts
Open-JSON-UIValidated JSON representation for portable generated interfaces
AirflowOuter DAG orchestrator — schedules and triggers Claude Code / LangGraph pipeline runs on a cron or event
Ralph LoopA pattern for running Claude Code autonomously — the bash substrate all loop patterns run on
ReActThe inner loop Claude Code runs natively — Reason about state → Act via tool → Observe result → repeat
Plan–Execute–ReflectThe outer loop pattern for your report pipeline — Plan queries → Execute → Reflect on output quality → Revise
HITLLangGraph interrupt() pauses a Claude Code subagent run for human review before continuing
Test-Driven LoopClaude Code writes code → GitHub Actions runs tests → failures feed back to Claude Code → iterate until pass
Claude Agent SDKThe raw SDK layer Claude Code is built on top of
LangGraphOrchestration runtime Claude Code can run inside as a headless subagent
Deep AgentsParallel harness at the same level — LangChain's open-source equivalent of Claude Code, both built on their respective runtimes
Mem0Memory middleware dropped into Claude Code
LangfuseObserves Claude Code's traces and tool calls
LangSmithNative LangGraph observability — deepest graph tracing if running LangGraph
SplunkOps observability — ingests Claude Code system logs, pipeline errors, security events
GrafanaMetrics dashboard — latency, throughput, cost-per-run charts for Claude Code pipelines
MLflowExperiment tracking — logs prompt experiments and manages fine-tuned models used by Claude Code
Amazon S3 VectorsVector storage — lowest-cost AWS option for embedding indexes queried by Claude Code subagents
OpenWebUISelf-hostable Chat UI — gives teams a polished interface to Claude Code / Agent SDK without building one
OpenTerminalTerminal UI — developer-facing CLI interface for interacting with Claude Code agent pipelines
RedisShared working state across Claude Code subagents during fan-out
PostgreSQLDurable checkpoint store for LangGraph runs that invoke Claude Code
Amazon RedshiftAnalytical warehouse source queried by Claude Code agents for governed enterprise metrics and historical datasets
LangGraph CheckpointerSnapshots graph state so Claude Code subagent runs can be resumed
MongoDBStores variable-schema agent run artifacts — generated reports, tool call results, agent memory documents
Amazon DocumentDBAWS-managed MongoDB-compatible store — same role as MongoDB for AWS-native Claude Agent SDK pipelines
Amazon DynamoDBServerless KV for agent session state between runs · agent config · rate limiting — zero infrastructure overhead
OpenSearchKnowledge & retrieval layer — RAG context fed into Claude Code's context window
AWS TextractIngestion layer — extracts structured text from raw docs before indexing into OpenSearch
Bedrock Data AutomationManaged ingestion pipeline — processes multimodal docs into S3 before embedding
pgvectorLightweight vector store alternative — SQL + embeddings fed into agent context
Amazon NeptuneGraph traversal layer — structured knowledge fed into agent context via GraphRAG
Claude Agent TeamsAnthropic's native multi-agent coordination — supervisor routes tasks between specialist Claude agents built on the Agent SDK
LangGraph Multi-Agent SupervisorLangGraph equivalent of Claude Agent Teams — LLM supervisor node routes to worker subgraphs via StateGraph
Claude Subagent SpawningParent agent spawns N child Claude agents at runtime via Task() — concurrent children, results synthesised by parent
LangGraph Send() APILangGraph equivalent of Claude subagent spawning — dispatches N parallel subgraph instances at runtime, all write to shared TypedDict state
Guardrails AIInput/output content safety wrapper around Claude Code / Agent SDK — validators fire at hook points
Bedrock GuardrailsAWS-managed content safety wrapper — topic denial, PII redaction, grounding checks, content filters at I/O boundary
OPAPolicy-as-code engine gating which MCP tools and skills a given role can invoke via Claude Code
MCP ProtocolThe vertical protocol Claude Code uses to call DuckDB · Power BI · OpenSearch · GitHub tools
A2A ProtocolThe horizontal protocol that would let Claude Code agents delegate to non-Anthropic agents cross-ecosystem
GitHub ActionsCI/CD feedback loop — agent opens PR · Actions runs tests · results feed back to agent; or Claude Code runs headless inside Actions as a step
Git WorktreesHarness-level parallelism primitive — N Claude Code instances each in their own worktree, isolated filesystem, shared .git history
DockerContainerises Claude Code headless runs and subagent execution environments
KubernetesOrchestrates the container fleet running Claude Code workers at production scale
EKSAWS-managed K8s — the production runtime for AWS-native Claude Code / Agent SDK pipelines
AgentCore MemoryAWS-managed three-tier memory (short-term · long-term · episodic) — replaces Mem0 for AWS-native Claude Agent SDK pipelines
AgentCore (full platform)AWS-managed alternative to wiring LangGraph + Redis + Langfuse + Postgres together — runtime + memory + gateway + policy + evals
// Three-Tier Promotion Path — Claude Code → DeepAgents → LangGraph
Each tier is the same graph expressed in a progressively more production-hardened runtime. Promotion is additive, not a rewrite.
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
Claude Code Primitive→ DeepAgents equivalent→ LangGraph equivalentMigration notes
Subagent (Task())Deep Agents subagent@node + StateGraphPure (state) → state functions; no direct subagent-to-subagent calls
Skill (.claude/skills/)Deep Agents skill@tool / ToolNodeDesign skills as pure functions from day one; wraps cleanly
Hook (PreToolUse/Post)Middleware pipelineconditional_edgeHighest-risk migration; hooks are imperative, edges are declarative
Slash CommandDeep Agents entry pointgraph.invoke()Straightforward — both are named invocation entry points
CLAUDE.mdAGENT.md / configper-node SystemMessageHierarchical CLAUDE.md maps to per-node system prompts in LangGraph
Shared filesystem / JSONFilesystemMiddlewareTypedDict state schemaHighest-risk gap: freeform file I/O → explicit typed state; design PipelineState first
MCP Tools (.claude/mcp.json)CompositeBackend toolsToolNode / @toolMCP servers remain unchanged; wrapper changes per tier
Key principle: Design PipelineState as TypedDict before writing any agents · Each agent reads/writes only its own named keys · No subagent-to-subagent direct calls — communicate only through shared state · This makes every tier transition additive, not a rewrite.

// GraphRAG vs RAG — when to use which
TL;DR
  • Start with standard RAG. Add a graph only when the question depends on connections, not just similar text.
  • GraphRAG costs significantly more at index-build time. Budget for LLM extraction calls before committing.
  • The two approaches are complementary: a production GraphRAG stack still uses vector search alongside the graph.
DimensionStandard RAGGraphRAG
Retrieval unitText chunk (passage, paragraph, sentence window)Entity, relationship, path, neighborhood, community summary
Best question type"What does the policy say about X?" — similarity-based lookup"How is A connected to B?" / "What depends on X?" / "Which obligations apply to this account?"
Corpus typeAny documents — PDFs, tickets, web pages, manualsDocuments where entity relationships matter: compliance, org data, financial networks, dependency maps
Index-build costLow — embed and index chunksHigh — LLM calls for entity extraction + relation extraction + entity disambiguation
Query-time costLow — ANN search + optional rerankVaries — traversal approach: low; community summarization approach: pre-computed, low at query time
Retrieval latencyLow (vector ANN, ms range)Traversal: fast; community summary lookup: fast; deep multi-hop: can be slow without query planning
ExplainabilityCitation of source chunksFull path evidence: node → relationship → node → answer with provenance at each hop
Multi-hop reasoningWeak — chunk retrieval rarely spans relationship chainsStrong — graph traversal follows typed relationship edges across entities
Index maintenanceIncremental upsert by document IDComplex — entity merge on new docs, relationship deduplication, disambiguation across updates
Schema required?No — chunks are untypedYes — node labels, relationship types, and property contracts must be designed before ingestion
When NOT to useWhen answers require multi-hop entity traversal or relationship-based reasoningSimple document Q&A, pure key-value lookup, normal relational reporting, or when the workload is entirely similarity-based
// Graph construction pipeline — from documents to knowledge graph
Building a knowledge graph from documents is a separate pipeline from standard RAG ingestion. Each step has distinct failure modes. The most common place teams get stuck with GraphRAG is not the graph database — it is this pipeline.
Six-stage construction pipeline
1 · Extract entities
Run NER over chunked text. Extract people, orgs, products, concepts, events, systems, risks. Use a fine-tuned NER model or LLM-based extraction with a typed schema.
Failure: hallucinated or over-extracted entities
2 · Extract relations
Identify typed relationships between entities: WORKS_AT, DEPENDS_ON, REPORTS_TO, OWNED_BY, CITED_IN. Each relation needs a source, target, type, and provenance span.
Failure: wrong direction, missing type, noise relations
3 · Resolve identities
Merge "MSFT," "Microsoft Corp," and "Microsoft" into one canonical node. Coreference resolution across documents. This step determines graph quality — poor resolution fragments retrieval.
Failure: duplicate nodes fragment multi-hop paths
4 · Load graph
Batch-upsert nodes and relationships into Amazon Neptune Database. Use idempotent upsert or merge semantics in openCypher, Gremlin, or SPARQL rather than blind inserts to avoid duplicates. Attach provenance: source document, chunk ID, confidence, timestamp.
Failure: duplicate edges, missing provenance
5 · Build vector index
Embed entity descriptions and use Neptune Analytics vector search alongside graph traversal. This enables entity-anchored retrieval: embed the query → find nearest entity nodes → traverse.
Note: same model pin rules as standard RAG
6 · Incremental updates
New documents trigger re-extraction for changed entities only. Merge new relations, re-run disambiguation for affected entity clusters. Community summaries need recompute if community membership changes.
Failure: stale communities, orphaned nodes
Pipeline tooling
Entity + relation extraction
spaCy · GLiNER · LLM-based extraction (Claude, GPT) with typed output schemas · Instructor library for structured LLM output · Microsoft GraphRAG library (open source)
Entity disambiguation
String matching · embedding similarity · knowledge base linking (Wikidata, internal catalogs) · LLM-based coreference resolution · entity linkers (REL, BLINK)
Graph loading + pipelines
AWS SDK for Neptune · Neptune bulk loader · graph-notebook · LangChain LLMGraphTransformer · Airflow or Dagster for orchestration
Pipeline insight · extraction quality determines retrieval qualityA knowledge graph is only as good as its extraction pipeline. The two highest-leverage investments are entity resolution (step 3) and schema design (done before step 1). A graph with fragmented duplicate nodes produces broken multi-hop paths. A graph with an ill-defined schema produces edges that the query planner cannot traverse reliably. Fix both before scaling ingestion volume.
// Graph DB — Amazon Neptune deployment options
TL;DR
  • Use Amazon Neptune Database as the canonical operational graph store for AWS-native knowledge graphs and GraphRAG.
  • Use Neptune Analytics for graph algorithms, exploratory analysis, and vector search within graph traversals.
  • Use Neptune Database when relationships and paths are first-class operational requirements.
OptionBest fitStrengthTradeoff
Amazon Neptune DatabaseOperational knowledge graphs, GraphRAG, entity resolution, dependency paths, fraud/compliance graphs, and AWS-governed workloads.Managed AWS graph database supporting property graphs through Gremlin and openCypher, plus RDF through SPARQL.Requires deliberate graph modeling, query-language selection, and ingestion governance.
Neptune AnalyticsGraph algorithms, investigative analytics, community detection, path analysis, and vector similarity within graph traversals.Memory-optimized analytics engine with graph algorithms, low-latency queries, and vector search.Complements rather than replaces the operational Neptune Database for transactional graph workloads.

What connectsAgent ↔ ToolAgent ↔ Agent
DirectionVertical — agent calls down to toolHorizontal — agent delegates across to agent

In your stackDuckDB · Power BI · OpenSearch · GitHub MCPsNot yet — relevant for cross-vendor handoffs

DimensionAmazon Bedrock GuardrailsGuardrails AI
Made byAWSGuardrails AI Inc.
TypeManaged safety serviceOpen-source validators-as-code framework
InfrastructureZero — fully managedSelf-host or managed Guardrails Hub
Content safety filtersNative — hate, violence, sexual, insultsVia community validators
PII detection + redactionNative — 30+ entity typesVia Presidio validator

Topic denialNative — deny off-topic requestsVia custom validator
Schema / format validationNoNative — core feature
Custom business rulesNo — config only, no codeFull Python code — any logic
Prompt injection defenseNative filterVia validators
Model-agnosticBedrock / Claude onlyAny model
AWS-nativeIAM · CloudWatch · Bedrock AgentsCan run on AWS, not native
CostPay-per-useOpen source — free to self-host
Best forManaged content safety, PII, grounding on AWS-native Claude stacksCustom business logic, schema validation, domain-specific constraints
Replaces each other?No — complementary. Bedrock Guardrails handles the managed AWS safety layer. Guardrails AI handles custom validation logic. Use both: Bedrock Guardrails first, then Guardrails AI.

API gatewayFastAPIExpose pipeline as async HTTP endpoint consumed by Power BI / downstream

Data source ADuckDB (MCP)Structured query subagent — SQL over internal data
Data source BOpenSearch (MCP)Search subagent — hybrid BM25 + vector retrieval

MemoryMem0Cross-run memory — what worked last report cycle, user preferences

DeliveryPower BIDownstream consumer of generated report output

CI / CDGitHub ActionsRun pipeline evals on PR · validate output schema before merge
Market-map path complete. Continue into the managed-platform track when evaluating an AWS-native consolidated implementation; otherwise proceed to Decision Guides.
Managed Platform Implementation

5.7 AWS AgentCore Platform Track

An AWS-native implementation route showing how AgentCore services map onto the vendor-neutral architecture.
TL;DR
  • Choose AgentCore when platform governance and AWS-native identity matter more than portability.
  • AgentCore spans runtime, memory, tool access, identity, observability, and evaluation - not one tool.
  • Document vendor lock-in assumptions and exit criteria before committing to managed runtime or memory.

AgentCore Service Map

Monthly review
☁️Amazon Bedrock AgentCore as a Managed Platform9 services · AWS-native path
Use this as the AWS-native reader path. AgentCore is not one isolated tool; it is a managed platform track that can replace or complement several custom-stack components across runtime, memory, tool access, identity, policy, observability, and evaluation.
AgentCore Runtime
AWS · managed agent runtime
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Managed execution environment for deploying and operating agents without owning the full serving/runtime layer.
runtimemanaged

AWS · managed memory
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
AWS-native memory service for session, user, and long-running agent context when memory should live inside the managed Bedrock stack.
memoryAWS-native

Currentreviewed 2026-05-24
Strong optionMarket-map
Centralizes tool and service access for agents, reducing ad hoc integrations and making access paths easier to govern.
toolsgateway

Identity and access management for agent actions, especially when agents need scoped permissions across AWS and enterprise systems.

Policy controls for governing what agents can do, which tools they can call, and which actions require additional checks.

browserautomation

Watchreviewed 2026-05-24

observabilityCloudWatch

AWS · managed evaluation
Currentreviewed 2026-05-24

evaluationquality gates
How to read this: use the Projects catalog for layer-by-layer alternatives; use this section when you want the consolidated AWS-managed route. AgentCore can replace pieces of a custom LangGraph/LangSmith/PromptFoo-style stack, but it can also coexist with model-agnostic tools where portability or CI-driven testing matters.
Managed-platform path complete. Continue for opinionated build-versus-buy choices, migration paths, and comparison guidance.

5.8 Platform Bill of Materials

A default implementation baseline by layer. Treat this as the starting architecture, then substitute only when requirements justify the added variance.
Models & gateway

Amazon Bedrock-hosted frontier and fast models, routed through LiteLLM or a governed provider gateway.

Preferred default
Agent control

LangGraph for explicit stateful control flows; Claude Code or Codex-class harnesses for engineering workflows.

Preferred default
Durable execution

Temporal for long-running business workflows; Inngest for event-driven application jobs; Airflow or Dagster for data pipelines.

Workload-specific
Knowledge retrieval

OpenSearch for hybrid enterprise search, pgvector for Postgres-native retrieval, and S3 Vectors for large economical vector estates.

Preferred default
Graph intelligence

Amazon Neptune Database for governed operational graphs and Neptune Analytics for algorithms, exploration, and graph-aware vector similarity.

Canonical graph layer
State & memory

PostgreSQL for durable state and audit records, Redis for low-latency working state, and explicit memory services with TTL and provenance.

Preferred default
Tool execution

MCP-compatible tool gateways, scoped credentials, idempotency keys, sandboxed execution, and approval gates for high-risk actions.

Mandatory controls
Evaluation & observability

Langfuse or LangSmith for traces, RAGAS and task-specific graders for retrieval quality, OpenTelemetry for platform telemetry.

Preferred default
Security & policy

Bedrock Guardrails, OPA-style policy enforcement, enterprise identity propagation, tenant-scoped authorization, and immutable audit logs.

Mandatory controls
Infrastructure

AWS-native networking, KMS, Secrets Manager, S3, container or serverless compute, IaC, CI/CD, and environment-separated configuration.

Preferred default
Production takeaway — Use AgentCore as an AWS-native implementation path for the vendor-neutral architecture, while keeping application contracts portable and observable.
Part VI

Production Engineering

Build, evaluate, secure, deploy, operate, and incident-manage production agentic systems.
In this part
  • 6.1Architecture & Delivery
  • 6.2Runtime & Performance
  • 6.3Quality, Safety & Governance
  • 6.4Operations & Reliability
Canonical End-to-End Production Trace
A single request traced through identity, routing, retrieval, Neptune graph expansion, authorized execution, verification, memory, telemetry, and evaluation. Use this as the integration contract connecting the architecture sections that follow.
Control plane
Identity & tenant context
Policy & risk classification
Router & planner
Verifier & stop decision
Owns authorization, budgets, routing, loop control, and release decisions.
Data & knowledge plane
Hybrid vector / lexical retrieval
Permission filtering
Amazon Neptune relationship expansion
Reranking & evidence assembly
Produces an authorized, provenance-rich evidence bundle for reasoning and verification.
Execution & operations plane
Tool gateway & scoped credentials
Sandbox / durable execution
State & governed memory
Trace, cost, lineage & evaluation
Executes side effects safely and records the operational evidence required to run the system.
Feedback loops and non-linear paths
Verification defect→repair or replan within budget→verify again
Weak retrieval→query rewrite or alternate retriever→rerank again
Policy or confidence failure→human review, safe refusal, or reduced-scope response
Tool failure→idempotent retry, compensation, alternate tool, or replan
Memory conflict→reconcile, supersede, quarantine, or decline the write
Evaluation regression→block release, inspect traces, update dataset, and rerun gates
01 · ingress
Identity, tenant, and policy context
Input
Request, user identity, tenant, channel
Output
Authenticated principal and policy context
Timeout
1–2 s
Persist
Request ID, tenant ID, policy snapshot hash
Failure
Deny safely; never continue anonymously
Owner
Identity / platform
02 · control
Risk classification and routing
Input
Normalized request and policy context
Output
Risk tier, route, budgets, required approvals
Timeout
<1 s
Persist
Classification and route decision
Failure
Conservative route or human review
Owner
Agent platform / risk
03 · planning
Plan and execution contract
Input
Goal, route, tool catalog, budgets
Output
Typed steps, stop conditions, evidence needs
Timeout
2–5 s
Persist
Plan version and loop budget
Failure
Fall back to direct answer or escalate
Owner
Agent application
04 · retrieval
Hybrid retrieval and permission filtering
Input
Query, entities, tenant ACLs
Output
Ranked chunks and candidate entities
Timeout
1–4 s
Persist
Query, filters, result IDs, scores
Failure
Retry degraded retrieval or abstain
Owner
Knowledge platform
05 · graph
Neptune relationship expansion
Input
Resolved entities and graph constraints
Output
Allowed paths, neighborhoods, evidence chains
Timeout
1–3 s
Persist
Query template, path IDs, provenance
Failure
Return non-graph baseline; flag reduced confidence
Owner
Knowledge graph team
06 · action
Tool authorization and sandbox execution
Input
Typed tool request and scoped credentials
Output
Result, side-effect receipt, execution logs
Timeout
Tool-specific hard limit
Persist
Arguments hash, authorization, receipt
Failure
Idempotent retry, compensate, or escalate
Owner
Tool owner / platform
07 · verify
Evidence and outcome verification
Input
Draft answer, tool results, evidence pack
Output
Verified answer, confidence, defects
Timeout
2–6 s
Persist
