Verifier score and failed checks
Failure
Repair loop within budget or abstain
Owner
Evaluation / application
08 · memory
Memory-write decision
Input
Outcome, user scope, retention policy
Output
Write, summarize, expire, or reject
Timeout
Async preferred
Persist
Consent, source, TTL, confidence
Failure
Answer succeeds without memory write
Owner
Memory / governance
09 · response
Response and user-visible provenance
Input
Verified answer and authorized citations
Output
Answer, evidence, limitations, next action
Timeout
Within end-to-end SLO
Persist
Response hash and citation set
Failure
Partial result with explicit status
Owner
Product application
10 · operations
Trace, cost, lineage, and evaluation records
Input
All stage events and model/tool usage
Output
Trace, cost, quality, SLO, lineage records
Timeout
Non-blocking export
Persist
OpenTelemetry-compatible spans and eval joins
Failure
Buffer locally; alert on telemetry loss
Owner
Observability / FinOps / ML platform
TL;DR
  • Ship observability, rollback, and eval coverage before going live - not after.
  • The launch readiness gates are a hard go/no-go: every red item blocks launch.
  • Most production failures originate in context and prompt engineering, not the model.
  • Cost: embedding pipeline and inference dominate spend - optimize those before infrastructure.
  • Version prompts, models, tools, schemas, evals, and policies together as a single release artifact.
Production readiness checklist
  • Define the user journey, risk tier, owner, rollback path, and success metric before adding autonomy.
  • Pin prompt, model, tool, retrieval, and policy versions for every release.
  • Require traces, eval capture, cost telemetry, and escalation paths before launch.
🚀Production Deployment Playbookarchitecture · operating model · safety · CI/CD · SLOs · rollout · rollback
Purpose: This playbook turns the agent stack into an operational deployment model. Use it to move from prototype to production with explicit architecture, release gates, monitoring, safety controls, rollout discipline, and rollback paths.

Core principle: Treat prompts, tools, models, retrieval indexes, policies, and eval datasets as production artifacts. Version them, test them, deploy them gradually, observe them continuously, and keep a known-good rollback path.
Reference architecture: production chatbot / agent
1 · Client
Web, mobile, Slack, Teams, voice, embedded copilot, or API client.
2 · Edge & Auth
CDN, API gateway, SSO, session handling, tenant context, RBAC, rate limits.
3 · App API
Request validation, conversation routing, feature flags, prompt/model version selection.
4 · Agent Runtime
Planner, orchestrator, tool router, memory, checkpointer, step limits, budget limits.
5 · Policy Gate
Guardrails, OPA policies, tool permissions, input/output filtering, approval rules.
6 · Knowledge Layer
Connectors, document ACLs, vector/hybrid search, reranking, citations, index versions.
7 · Tools & Actions
Internal APIs, SaaS tools, databases, workflow engines, dry-run mode, human approval.
8 · Ops Plane
Tracing, logs, metrics, evals, audit trail, alerts, cost dashboards, incident runbooks.
Flow: User → UI/API → Auth & tenant context → App API → Agent runtime → Policy gate → Retrieval and/or tools → Model response → Citation/action renderer → Trace, audit, metrics, and feedback capture.
Minimum viable production architecture
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
ComponentRequired?Production responsibility
User interfaceYesChat, copilot, embedded assistant, CLI, or API surface with streaming, retry-safe submission, error states, and feedback capture.
API gatewayYesAuth enforcement, rate limits, request validation, tenant context, abuse controls, and routing to the correct assistant/version.
Conversation serviceYesOwns sessions, message history, metadata, user feedback, transcript retention, and correlation IDs across traces.
Agent runtimeYesExecutes the loop, calls tools, applies step/time/token budgets, checkpoints state, and prevents runaway execution.
Model gatewayYesRoutes by task, model, tenant, cost, latency, and risk tier; handles retries, fallback, caching, budgets, and version pins.
Policy / safety layerYesValidates inputs, outputs, tool calls, data boundaries, approvals, PII controls, and prohibited behaviors.
Retrieval layerIf RAGIngests sources, preserves ACLs, chunks and indexes content, retrieves with metadata, reranks, cites, and handles no-answer cases.
Tool execution layerIf agenticEnforces schemas, permissions, dry-run, approval, idempotency, audit logs, and rollback for external actions.
State and memoryYesStores short-term state, long-term preferences or memory where allowed, retention/deletion rules, and user controls.
Observability and evalsYesCaptures traces, quality signals, safety events, costs, latency, regression tests, golden datasets, and release gates.
Human handoffUsuallyRoutes low-confidence, unsafe, unresolved, or high-value cases to human support with complete context.
Production path: User → frontend → API gateway → auth / tenant context → conversation service → agent runtime → model gateway → policy gate → retrieval / tools → state store → response renderer → traces, feedback, evals, audit, and support handoff.
Product pattern → production risk mapping
PatternMain production riskAdditional controls needed
Basic chatbotWrong answer, bad tone, unsupported claim, unsafe response.Prompt evals, moderation, feedback capture, transcript review, escalation and no-answer behavior.
RAG assistantBad retrieval, stale source, missing permission filter, incorrect citation.Document ACLs, ingestion monitoring, hybrid search, reranking, citation validation, retrieval evals, source freshness checks.
Tool-using copilotInvalid, unauthorized, duplicate, or harmful action.Tool schemas, policy gate, dry-run, approval workflow, idempotency keys, transaction logs, reversible actions.
Autonomous agentRunaway loop, compounding error, excessive cost, unsafe multi-step execution.Autonomy tiers, step budgets, kill switch, sandboxing, bounded objectives, human checkpoints, incident rollback.
Rule of thumb: chatbots mostly need response-quality controls; RAG systems need data and retrieval controls; tool agents need execution controls; autonomous agents need operating limits and explicit rollback paths.
Lifecycle diagram
01 · Scope
Use case, users, success metric, risk tier.
02 · Design
Architecture, data sources, tools, permissions.
03 · Build
Prompts, retrieval, tool schemas, runtime, UI.
04 · Evaluate
Golden sets, red-team tests, regression gates.
05 · Stage
Production-like auth, data, policies, monitoring.
06 · Launch
Canary, feature flags, owner watch, rollback ready.
07 · Observe
Quality, safety, cost, latency, tool success.
08 · Improve
Feedback triage, eval expansion, prompt/model changes.
09 · Govern
Reviews, audits, access recertification, retention.
10 · Respond
Incident detection, mitigation, comms, postmortem.
11 · Roll Back
Revert model, prompt, tool, policy, or index.
12 · Repeat
Ship controlled improvements through the same gates.
Production readiness checklist
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
AreaReady when…Owner
Use caseTarget users, allowed tasks, disallowed tasks, and success metrics are explicit.Product
Auth & tenancyIdentity, tenant context, RBAC, and session lifecycle are enforced on every request.App / Platform
Data accessDocument-level or row-level permissions flow into retrieval and tool calls.Data / Security
PromptsSystem prompts, developer prompts, and templates are versioned, reviewed, and regression-tested.AI Engineering
RetrievalChunking, metadata, reranking, freshness, citations, and no-answer behavior are validated.Data / AI Engineering
ToolsTool schemas, input validation, idempotency, dry-run mode, and approval policies exist.Backend / Security
Memory & stateConversation state, long-term memory, retention, deletion, and user controls are defined.Platform / Legal
EvalsGolden datasets, adversarial cases, regression thresholds, and release gates are in CI/CD.AI Engineering / QA
ObservabilityTraces, logs, model/tool calls, token use, feedback, and audit events are captured.SRE / Platform
SafetyPrompt-injection, jailbreak, PII leakage, harmful output, and unauthorized action tests pass.Security / Trust
Cost controlsBudgets, rate limits, model routing, caching, context limits, and alerting are configured.Platform / Finance
RollbackKnown-good prompt, model, tool, policy, and index versions can be restored quickly.SRE / AI Engineering
Model gateway and routing layer
Routing
Choose model by task type, risk tier, context length, tenant policy, latency target, and cost budget.
Reliability
Retries, provider fallback, timeout policy, degraded mode, circuit breakers, and cached safe responses.
Cost controls
Token budgets, context compression, batch jobs, caching, low-cost classifiers, and per-tenant quotas.
Version binding
Pin prompt, model, tool, policy, embedding, retriever, and reranker versions for every release.
Safety hooks
Input classification, output checks, sensitive-data filtering, refusal routing, and human escalation triggers.
Auditability
Record model name, version, prompt hash, context hash, token usage, latency, and downstream tool calls.
AI artifact CI/CD and release gates
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
ArtifactVersioned?Tests before releaseRollback method
Prompt / system instructionsYesGolden-set accuracy, refusal behavior, jailbreak tests, tone and formatting checks.Revert prompt version or route traffic to previous assistant config.
ModelYesTask benchmarks, latency, cost, safety, regression, and provider-fallback tests.Switch model route to prior version or alternate provider.
Tool schemaYesContract tests, validation failures, auth checks, idempotency, dry-run and approval tests.Disable tool version, revert schema, or force read-only mode.
Retrieval indexYesRecall@k, citation correctness, permission filtering, freshness, deleted-document behavior.Repoint to previous index snapshot or disable affected corpus.
Policies / guardrailsYesAllowed/blocked cases, PII redaction, tool permissions, tenant rules, false-positive checks.Revert policy bundle and refresh cache.
Eval datasetsYesCoverage review, labeling quality, adversarial cases, severity weighting, drift checks.Restore previous eval suite while preserving new failure cases for review.
Recommended release flow: code review → artifact version pin → unit tests → golden evals → safety evals → latency/cost benchmark → staging replay → canary → live monitoring → promote or rollback.
Tool execution safety
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
ControlWhat to implementWhy it matters
Tool allowlistOnly expose approved tools; never let the model invent endpoints or arbitrary SQL/API calls.Reduces blast radius and blocks unauthorized capability expansion.
Per-role permissionsCheck user, tenant, group, data scope, and action type before every tool call.Prevents cross-tenant and privilege-escalation failures.
Read vs write splitSeparate search/read tools from create/update/delete/payment/email tools.Lets teams launch safely in read-only or draft-only mode.
Dry runShow the planned action and expected diff before execution.Supports human review and catches model misunderstandings.
Approval gatesRequire human approval for high-risk, irreversible, financial, legal, or external-facing actions.Keeps autonomy proportional to risk.
Input/output validationValidate schema, types, ranges, object IDs, ownership, and response shape.Prevents malformed requests and tool-result poisoning.
IdempotencyAttach idempotency keys and detect duplicate retries.Stops duplicate tickets, payments, emails, writes, or workflow runs.
Audit logRecord who asked, what the model planned, what tool ran, payload, result, and approval state.Enables investigation, compliance, and rollback.
Data governance and privacy lifecycle
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
QuestionProduction answer required
What data is sent to the model?Classify prompts, retrieved snippets, files, tool results, memory, and metadata by sensitivity level.
Where is data stored?Define storage for transcripts, traces, embeddings, feedback, memory, and tool logs.
How long is it retained?Set retention, deletion, export, legal hold, and tenant-specific policy rules.
How are permissions enforced?Propagate source ACLs into retrieval, tool calls, conversation views, admin dashboards, and traces.
How is sensitive data handled?Redact or tokenize PII/secrets in logs and traces; restrict who can inspect raw prompts and tool payloads.
Can users control memory?Expose memory on/off, view, edit, delete, and enterprise admin policy controls.
What happens when source data changes?Revoke deleted documents, refresh indexes, remove stale embeddings, and recalculate permissions.
RAG production operations
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
AreaProduction requirementFailure signal
IngestionConnector health, schedule, retries, parsing errors, OCR failures, source metadata, and index version.Missing or stale documents, high parse failure rate.
ChunkingDocument-type-specific chunking, section metadata, hierarchy preservation, deduplication.Answers cite irrelevant fragments or lose context.
SearchHybrid retrieval, filters, reranking, query rewriting, and fallback search strategy.Low recall@k, high empty retrieval, repeated user re-asks.
PermissionsDocument, row, object, and tenant ACLs enforced before context is sent to the model.Unauthorized source appears in answer or trace.
CitationsEvery grounded claim links to source chunk, document version, and retrieval timestamp.Citation mismatch, source not found, stale-source complaints.
No-answer behaviorAssistant says when evidence is missing and offers next safe step.Confident unsupported answers.
Drift monitoringTrack source freshness, content churn, retriever quality, and eval failure trends.Quality drops after index, corpus, or model change.
Human handoff and support operations
Escalation triggers
Low confidence, policy uncertainty, unresolved user intent, repeated failure, high-value action, legal/compliance topic, or user requests a human.
Context package
User intent, conversation summary, source citations, retrieved docs, tool plan, attempted actions, confidence, and trace link.
Support queue
Route by tenant, product area, risk level, SLA, and required permission to inspect sensitive traces.
Learning loop
Convert bad answers, support corrections, and incident examples into labeled eval cases and regression tests.
Production metrics table
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
Metric categoryTrackWhy it matters
QualityTask success, answer accuracy, groundedness, citation correctness, no-answer accuracy.Confirms the system is useful, not merely responsive.
RetrievalRecall@k, empty retrieval rate, reranker lift, stale-source rate, permission-filter misses.Shows whether the model is seeing the right evidence.
Tool useTool-call success, validation failures, unauthorized attempts, approval rate, idempotency failures.Agents become risky when they take action incorrectly.
SafetyPolicy blocks, prompt-injection detections, PII redactions, unsafe-output flags, escalations.Tracks whether trust controls are working in the wild.
ReliabilityError rate, timeout rate, fallback rate, retry rate, dependency failures.Measures whether the system behaves like production software.
LatencyTime to first token, total response time, retrieval latency, tool latency, queue wait.Separates model slowness from app, search, and tool bottlenecks.
CostCost per conversation, cost per successful task, token usage, embedding cost, tool/API cost.Prevents successful prototypes from becoming uneconomic products.
User experienceThumbs up/down, abandonment, re-ask rate, escalation rate, resolution rate.Captures whether users trust and reuse the assistant.
Example launch SLOs and thresholds
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
MetricExample launch thresholdAction if breached
Critical safety failures0 in staging and canaryBlock launch; add tests and policy fixes.
Tool execution success> 98% for approved toolsDisable failing tool or keep approval-only mode.
Retrieval citation accuracy> 95% on golden setFix retrieval, chunking, reranking, or citation renderer.
P95 total latency< 8s for standard chat; stricter for embedded copilotsOptimize model route, retrieval, streaming, or tool latency.
Hallucination / unsupported claim rateBelow agreed risk threshold by use caseImprove grounding, no-answer behavior, and eval coverage.
Cost per resolved taskBelow product margin / budget targetCompress context, route cheaper models, cache, or narrow scope.
User thumbs-down rateBelow threshold by segment and intentReview transcripts, add evals, adjust prompt/retrieval/tools.
Escalation rateKnown, explainable, and supportableImprove UX or add staffing before scaling traffic.
Failure-mode runbooks
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
Failure modeDetection signalImmediate mitigationPermanent fix
Model provider outageSpike in timeout/fallback/error rate.Route to fallback model or degraded read-only mode.Add provider circuit breaker and synthetic health checks.
Latency spikeP95/P99 latency breach or queue growth.Reduce context, disable slow tools, switch model route.Profile retrieval/tool/model latency and add budgets.
Cost runawayToken, tool, or per-tenant spend exceeds budget.Apply quota, cheaper model, context cap, or feature flag off.Add cost tests, alerts, and budget-aware routing.
Prompt injectionPolicy detections, suspicious retrieved content, unexpected tool plan.Block tool execution, strip malicious context, require approval.Harden retrieval context, add adversarial evals and policy rules.
Retrieval index staleFreshness metric breach or user reports missing docs.Warn user, disable affected corpus, or force live search.Fix connector, reindex, and add ingestion monitoring.
Unauthorized data exposureSource appears for wrong user/tenant or audit alert fires.Disable corpus, rotate credentials, notify incident owner.Patch ACL propagation and add regression tests.
Tool action errorTool validation, duplicate action, or wrong-object write.Stop writes, force dry-run/approval, revert affected action.Improve schema, ownership checks, idempotency, and test coverage.
Agent loop runawayStep, token, or time budget exceeded.Kill run, summarize partial state, ask human for direction.Add bounded goals, loop guards, and state-machine constraints.
Agent autonomy ladder
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
LevelCapabilityControls required before promotion
0Answer only; no retrieval or tools.Prompt tests, safety filters, transcript logging, feedback.
1Retrieve and summarize approved knowledge.ACL-aware retrieval, citations, no-answer behavior, retrieval evals.
2Draft recommended action or response.Tool dry-run, source evidence, user confirmation, audit trail.
3Execute human-approved action.Approval workflow, idempotency, validation, rollback procedure.
4Execute bounded low-risk action autonomously.Allowlisted tools, budgets, monitoring, incident trigger, kill switch.
5Run bounded multi-step workflow.State machine, checkpointing, dependency health, escalation points.
6Autonomous operation in controlled domain.Formal risk review, mature evals, support staffing, audit, rollback, and executive/system owner approval.
Promotion rule: do not increase autonomy because the demo works; increase autonomy only when live metrics, evals, incident history, and rollback confidence support it.
Rollout plan
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
PhaseScopeExit criteria
PrototypeDeveloper-only, mocked or sandbox tools, synthetic and non-sensitive data.Architecture proves feasible and basic evals pass.
Internal alphaSmall trusted group, read-only access, visible feedback controls, tight monitoring.Critical failures are understood and high-severity safety issues are fixed.
Private betaLimited teams or tenants, production auth, scoped corpora, draft-only or approval-gated actions.Quality, latency, cost, and escalation metrics meet launch thresholds.
CanarySmall percentage of eligible traffic behind feature flags and version pins.No regression in safety, quality, reliability, cost, or support load.
General availabilityBroader rollout with published support path, incident runbook, and operating dashboard.Steady-state operation with weekly review and continuous eval expansion.
Autonomy expansionMove from read-only → draft → human-approved actions → limited autonomous actions.Only expand after tool success, policy compliance, and rollback confidence are proven.
Incident response and rollback plan
Incident triggers
Unsafe output, data leakage, unauthorized tool action, retrieval permission failure, cost spike, latency breach, outage, high hallucination rate, or repeated user escalations.
Immediate containment
Disable feature flag, switch to read-only mode, block risky tools, lower autonomy tier, route to fallback model, or pause affected tenant/corpus.
Rollback targets
Prompt version, model version, tool schema, guardrail policy, retrieval index, reranker, memory setting, feature flag, API integration, or UI release.
Post-incident loop
Preserve traces, identify root cause, add regression evals, update policies, communicate impact, document decisions, and require passing gates before re-enabling.
Minimum rollback rule: every production release must record the exact prompt, model, policy, tool, and retrieval-index versions used. If a change cannot be rolled back independently, it is not production-ready.
Agent Control Plane & Execution Plane
Separate what defines and governs an agent from what runs each task.
TL;DR
  • The control plane owns definitions, policies, versions, budgets, rollout rules, and registries.
  • The execution plane owns live runs, models, tools, retrieval, state, sandboxes, and human approvals.
  • Telemetry flows back into the control plane as evaluation, incident, cost, and outcome feedback.
Control plane — decide what may run
  • Agent, prompt, policy, and workflow versions
  • Model and tool registries
  • Identity, scopes, quotas, and budgets
  • Evaluation gates, canaries, rollout, and rollback
  • Environment and tenant configuration
⇄
Execution plane — perform bounded work
  • Agent sessions, plans, and loop steps
  • Inference, retrieval, and tool invocation
  • Sandboxes, checkpoints, queues, and timers
  • Approvals, cancellations, and compensation
  • Runtime traces, events, and artifacts
Design rule: production configuration should be versioned and deployed from the control plane; live runs should never silently rewrite their own governing policy.

6.1Architecture & Delivery

Start with the canonical architecture, select the right agent pattern, follow a build order, define schemas and environments, and establish deployment gates.

Durable Agent Runtime Engineering
The distributed-systems substrate that lets long-running loops survive failure.
Delivery and idempotencyAssume at-least-once delivery. Use idempotency keys, effect journals, deduplication windows, and replay-safe tool contracts.
Leases and livenessUse heartbeats, leases, ownership transfer, stuck-run detection, and bounded work stealing.
Retries and backpressureDefine retry ownership, exponential backoff, concurrency limits, circuit breakers, and dead-letter queues.
Cancellation and compensationPropagate cancellation through subagents and tools; use sagas or explicit compensation for irreversible partial effects.
Checkpoint and replayPersist workflow history, model/tool inputs, state transitions, and artifact references so a run can resume or be reconstructed.
Human wait statesDurably suspend for approvals without occupying a worker; enforce expiry, revalidation, and revocation before resuming.
ConcernLoop engineeringDurable runtime engineering
Primary questionWhat should the agent do next?How does the work survive failure?
StateReasoning, progress, and confidenceDurable history, checkpoints, and effect records
RetryRe-attempt, critique, or replanRedeliver safely without duplicating side effects
Failure handlingRecover, replan, or escalateReplay, compensate, quarantine, or dead-letter
Agent Reliability Engineering
Operate agentic systems with explicit service levels, error budgets, and runbooks.
Outcome reliabilityTask success, factual support, business acceptance, and human correction rate.
Loop reliabilityUnbounded-loop rate, mean steps, replans, stuck runs, and cancellation effectiveness.
Tool reliabilityInvocation success, permission denials, partial effects, timeout rate, and recovery success.
Retrieval reliabilityMiss rate, context precision/recall, stale-evidence rate, and citation coverage.
Economic reliabilityCost per successful task, retry waste, fan-out amplification, and human-review cost.
Operational reliabilityAvailability, queue delay, time to useful result, incident frequency, and mean time to recovery.
Task success SLO≥ 95% on defined production slices
Unsupported claims≤ 1% for high-trust workflows
Unbounded loops0; every loop has a hard budget
P95 first useful resultSet by product experience, not model latency alone
Cost per successTrack successful outcomes, not average calls
Error-budget rule: when correctness, security, or runaway-loop budgets are exhausted, pause feature expansion and prioritize reliability work.
Agent Handoff Contracts & Artifact Lifecycle
Make multi-agent work inspectable, transferable, reviewable, and reproducible.
Task contractObjective, inputs, assumptions, allowed tools, risk level, completion criteria, budget, and deadline.
Handoff envelopeStatus, outputs, evidence, confidence, unresolved questions, remaining risks, and recommended next action.
Artifact identityStable ID, type, owner, version, checksum, lineage, source references, and environment.
Review stateDraft → validated → reviewed → approved → published → superseded → archived.
ReproducibilityRecord model, prompt, context assembler, tool versions, retrieved evidence, and run trace.
GovernanceRetention, access, correction, deletion, legal hold, and approval requirements follow the artifact—not only the chat.
{
  "task_id": "research-42",
  "status": "partially_complete",
  "artifacts": ["evidence-pack-42"],
  "confidence": 0.84,
  "unresolved": ["Current certification not verified"],
  "recommended_next_step": "Request a current certificate"
}
Delegation rule: a receiving agent should not have to infer whether work is complete, which evidence supports it, or what authority was used.

6.1.1Canonical Production Architecture

Stable
The canonical request path for a production chatbot or agent. Every component is independently observable, versioned, permissioned, and rollbackable.
Reference request flow
1. User / Channel
Web, mobile, Slack, Teams, voice, support desk.
2. API Gateway
Rate limits, auth, tenant routing, request IDs.
3. Conversation Service
Session, history, message state, retention policy.
4. Agent Runtime
Plan, route, call tools, retrieve, respond.
5. Response Renderer
Streaming, citations, cards, actions, handoff.
Policy Gate
PII, prompt-injection, content, approval policy.
Retrieval Layer
ACL-aware search, rerank, citations, freshness.
Model Gateway
Routing, fallback, budgets, model pins, logging.
Tool Execution
Allowlist, schema validation, sandbox, approvals.
State Stores
Postgres, Redis, vector DB, audit log, warehouse.
Cross-cutting control plane: tracing, eval capture, feedback, metrics, cost accounting, security audit, feature flags, release versions, incident alerts, and rollback controls wrap every request path.
PatternRequired additionsProduction rule
Basic chatbotAuth, session store, prompt versions, model gateway, feedback, observability.No production launch without traceable prompt/model versions and human escalation.
RAG assistantIngestion, ACL-aware retrieval, source citations, no-answer behavior, freshness checks.No chunk reaches the prompt until tenant and document permissions are verified.
Tool-using agentTool registry, schemas, permission checks, dry run, approval flow, idempotency keys, audit logs.No write/action tool executes without explicit risk tier, owner, rollback plan, and audit record.

6.1.2Agent Architecture Patterns

Stable
How agent systems are organized before any specific loop executes. Patterns describe the system shape; loops describe repeated execution inside that shape.
Architecture layer · v3.26
Pattern first, loop second
Use this section to choose the shape of the agent system before choosing ReAct, Plan–Execute–Reflect, verifier loops, search loops, or consensus loops. A good production system usually combines a deterministic workflow shell, a small number of agentic nodes, explicit risk gates, and measurable verifier checkpoints.
TL;DR Start with a Single-Agent Tool Loop or Workflow / DAG. Add a Router when requests split into clear domains. Add Planner–Executor when tasks require decomposition. Add Evaluator–Optimizer and Critic / Verifier when quality matters. Add Orchestrator–Worker, Supervisor, Swarm, or Debate only when evals prove the extra coordination improves outcomes enough to justify cost, latency, and operational complexity.
Pattern Taxonomy
Execution patternsShape the main work path.
  • Single-Agent Tool Loop
  • Workflow / DAG
  • Planner–Executor
Routing patternsChoose the right path, skill, model, workflow, or agent.
  • Router
  • Model Router
  • Tool Router
  • Skill Router
Delegation patternsSplit work across specialized agents or workers.
  • Orchestrator–Worker
  • Supervisor
  • Swarm / Peer Agents
Quality patternsImprove or check the output before it reaches users.
  • Evaluator–Optimizer
  • Critic / Verifier
  • Debate / Adversarial Review
Knowledge patternsGround the agent in external knowledge and durable context.
  • Retrieval-Grounded Agent
  • Memory-Augmented Agent
  • Learn–Distill–Persist
Pattern specialization · GraphRAGGraphRAG is Retrieval-Grounded Agent + Knowledge Graph. Standard RAG retrieves chunks. GraphRAG retrieves entities, relationships, paths, neighborhoods, communities, permissions, and provenance. Use Amazon Neptune Database for governed graph storage and traversal, and Neptune Analytics when graph algorithms or vector search over graph entities should be part of the context pack.
Safety patternsControl autonomy, cost, permissions, and failure handling.
  • Human-in-the-Loop Approval
  • Risk-Gated Action
  • Budget-Gated Autonomy
  • Fallback / Graceful Degradation
Pattern Composition Diagram
Use this as the compact mental model for choosing an agent architecture pattern before choosing the inner execution loop.
User Request
   ↓
Router / Risk Classifier
   ↓
Pattern Selection
   ├─ Simple task → Single-Agent Tool Loop
   ├─ Predictable process → Workflow / DAG
   ├─ Complex task → Planner–Executor
   ├─ Multi-domain task → Orchestrator–Worker
   ├─ High-quality output → Evaluator–Optimizer
   ├─ Knowledge-heavy task → Retrieval-Grounded Agent
   └─ Risky action → HITL / Risk-Gated Action
   ↓
Verifier / Evaluator
   ↓
Memory / Trace / Audit Log
   ↓
Final Answer or Action
1 · Request intakeUser request enters through an app, chat surface, API, CLI, or automated job. Attach tenant, auth, trace, and policy context immediately.
2 · Router / risk classifierClassify intent, domain, data sensitivity, action risk, cost budget, and whether the request needs retrieval, tools, approval, or deterministic workflow.
3 · Pattern selectionChoose Single-Agent, Workflow / DAG, Planner–Executor, Orchestrator–Worker, Evaluator–Optimizer, Retrieval-Grounded, HITL, or Risk-Gated path.
4 · Verify / persist / respondRun evaluator or verifier, persist traces and memory updates, disclose limitations when needed, and return final answer or approved action.
Core Pattern Cards
Single-Agent Tool Loop
default · lowest orchestration overhead
Use when the task is narrow and tools are clear. One agent reasons, calls tools, observes results, and stops. This is the baseline before adding routers, supervisors, or multi-agent coordination.
Workflow / DAG
production default · most auditable
Use when the process has predictable stages such as ingest → classify → retrieve → draft → verify → approve → publish. LLMs appear inside bounded nodes, not as the whole control plane.
Router
domain split · skill selection
Use when requests naturally divide into coding, research, SQL, document editing, retrieval, customer support, or data-analysis paths. Avoid when categories are vague or overlapping.
Planner–Executor
decomposition · task graph
Use when the system must separate strategic planning from action execution. Pair with a verifier and a replan loop when observations invalidate the plan.
Orchestrator–Worker
parallel subtasks · specialist workers
Use when subtasks are separable and outputs can be merged. Do not use it to compensate for vague requirements or weak acceptance criteria.
Supervisor
long-running coordination
Use when multiple agents need monitoring, redirection, policy enforcement, and stop decisions over time. A supervisor manages progress; an orchestrator mainly decomposes and delegates.
Evaluator–Optimizer
quality loop · rubric driven
Use when quality matters more than latency. Generate, evaluate against a rubric, critique, revise, and accept only after threshold checks pass.
Critic / Verifier
grounding · policy · tests
Use when outputs can be checked: SQL safety, citation grounding, code tests, schema validity, tool arguments, security policy, or business rules.
Retrieval-Grounded Agent
private/fresh/cited knowledge
Use when the agent must plan queries, retrieve evidence, read sources, reason over context, cite claims, and verify grounding before answering.
Memory-Augmented Agent
continuity · personalization
Use when the agent needs working memory, episodic traces, semantic memory, user preferences, durable state, or learned project context across sessions.
Human-in-the-Loop Approval
irreversible or external action
Use when actions affect money, infrastructure, users, permissions, production systems, sensitive data, or external communications. Draft first; approve before execute.
Risk-Gated Action
autonomy control
Use when agents have write tools. Low-risk actions may execute; medium-risk actions require dry run or sandbox; high-risk actions require approval; prohibited actions are blocked.
Swarm / Peer Agents
exploration · expensive
Use when independent exploration is valuable, such as multiple research agents or competing implementation proposals. Gate with evals because cost and latency rise quickly.
Debate / Adversarial Review
assumption challenge
Use when subtle failure modes, tradeoffs, or high-stakes assumptions need explicit challenge. Prefer targeted red-team review over open-ended debate.
Fallback / Graceful Degradation
resilience · user-facing systems
Use when tools fail, retrieval is incomplete, models time out, or confidence is low. Retry safely, route to fallback, return partial answer, disclose limitation, and log the failure path.
Pattern Selection Matrix
PatternUse whenAvoid whenProduction default?
Single-Agent Tool LoopTask is narrow and tools are clear.Task needs parallel specialists or strict workflow control.Yes
Workflow / DAGSteps are predictable, auditable, and repeatable.Task needs open-ended exploration.Yes
RouterRequests fall into clear domains, skills, tools, or model tiers.Categories are vague or constantly changing.Yes
Planner–ExecutorTask requires decomposition and plan tracking.Direct execution is simpler and cheaper.Yes
Evaluator–OptimizerQuality matters more than latency and a rubric exists.No acceptance criteria or measurable quality signal exists.Yes
Critic / VerifierOutputs can be checked against tests, schema, policy, citations, or business rules.There is nothing objective to verify.Yes
Retrieval-Grounded AgentNeed private, fresh, cited, or domain-specific knowledge.Answer can be produced safely from existing context.Yes
HITL ApprovalActions are irreversible, external-facing, expensive, or sensitive.Read-only low-risk tasks.Yes
Risk-Gated ActionAgent has write tools or privileged tool access.Agent is read-only.Yes
Orchestrator–WorkerSubtasks are separable and mergeable.Requirements are vague or evaluation is weak.Sometimes
SupervisorLong-running multi-agent work needs monitoring and redirects.One-shot task.Sometimes
Memory-Augmented AgentNeed continuity, personalization, or learned project context.No persistence policy or memory deletion path exists.Sometimes
Swarm / Peer AgentsIndependent exploration materially improves outcomes.Latency, cost, or determinism matters more.Rare
Debate / Adversarial ReviewAssumptions require explicit challenge.Simple factual or transactional task.Rare
Fallback / Graceful DegradationUser-facing systems need resilience under model, tool, or retrieval failure.Internal prototype only.Yes
Pattern anti-patterns: do not add a supervisor because the prompt is unclear; do not add swarms before evals prove benefit; do not use debate when a deterministic verifier would work; do not give a router overlapping categories; do not let memory persist without retention, deletion, and audit rules; do not let agents take write actions without risk classification, scoped credentials, and traceable approval.

6.1.3Build-Order Roadmap

A production agent should be assembled in layers. Do not add autonomy until the previous layer is observable, evaluated, and reversible.
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
PhaseBuildExit criteriaDo not add yet
0 · PrototypeSingle model, static prompt, local UI, manual test questions.Can explain target users, top tasks, success/failure examples.Memory, write tools, autonomy.
1 · Internal chatbotAuth, conversation service, prompt versioning, feedback, tracing, support handoff.Internal users can complete common tasks; traces capture every run.External data actions.
2 · RAG assistantIngestion pipeline, embeddings, reranking, ACL filtering, citations, no-answer policy.Retrieval evals pass; citations are accurate; restricted docs never leak.Autonomous actions.
3 · Tool-using agentTool registry, schemas, dry-run execution, approval workflow, audit log.Tool selection and argument evals pass; every action is reversible or compensatable.Unapproved write actions.
4 · Production hardeningCanary releases, SLO dashboards, kill switches, cost budgets, runbooks, on-call.P95 latency, quality, safety, cost, and escalation metrics meet launch gates.Broad autonomy.
5 · Bounded autonomyLow-risk autonomous actions, policy engine, idempotency, queue execution, monitoring.Incident-free soak period; approvals only relaxed for low-risk, high-confidence actions.High-impact financial, legal, security, or customer-visible actions.
// SWE insight · chaos engineering for agent systemsAgent systems have more failure modes than traditional APIs — which makes chaos engineering more important, not less. Failure modes unique to agents: tool call timeout mid-run (does the agent retry, abort, or loop infinitely?), retrieval returns empty results (does the agent hallucinate or acknowledge uncertainty?), model returns malformed structured output (does parsing fail gracefully?), budget exhausted mid-task (partial result or clean abort?). Test by: injecting tool timeouts, returning empty retrieval results for a % of queries, injecting 429s from the model API, exhausting token budget mid-plan. Measure: graceful degradation, coherent partial results, alerting, state integrity. Agent systems that have never been chaos-tested will fail unpredictably in production.

6.1.4Async Job Queue Architecture

Current
Any agent run longer than ~30 seconds needs a job queue under it. Blocking HTTP connections for long-running agents is the most common production architecture mistake for agentic systems.
Why synchronous HTTP fails for agents

A synchronous agent endpoint — POST /run → wait → response — works fine for runs under ~10 seconds. Beyond that: load balancers time out, mobile clients disconnect, server restarts lose in-flight runs, and a single stuck tool call blocks the entire response. The production pattern is always async: accept the job, return a job ID immediately, run the agent in a worker, poll or push the result.

1. API Gateway
POST /run → validates, enqueues, returns job_id immediately (202 Accepted)
→
2. Job Queue
SQS / BullMQ / Redis Streams / Temporal. Durable. At-least-once delivery. Dead-letter queue for failures.
→
3. Worker Pool
Agent runtime. Pulls jobs, runs the agent loop, writes state to checkpoint store. Scales by queue depth.
→
4. Result Store
Redis / DynamoDB / Postgres. Stores job status (pending → running → done/failed), result, trace_id.
Queue optionBest forDurabilityDurable execution?
AWS SQS + Lambda/ECSAWS-native, serverless workersManaged, at-least-onceNo — need external state store
BullMQ (Redis)Node.js stacks, priorities, low latencyRedis AOF persistenceNo — need checkpoint pattern
Celery (Redis/RabbitMQ)Python stacks, ML workloadsBroker-dependentNo — need checkpoint pattern
TemporalLong-running, multi-step, HITL pause/resumeFull event sourcingYes — built-in durable execution
LangGraph + checkpointerLangGraph agents needing pause/resumePostgres / Redis backendYes — graph state persisted per step
Result delivery patterns: (1) Polling — client polls GET /run/{job_id}/status until done; simple but chatty. (2) Webhook — server POSTs result to a client URL on completion; good for server-to-server. (3) SSE stream — client opens GET /run/{job_id}/stream and receives token-by-token events; best UX for chat agents. Use (3) for human-facing agents, (2) for API integrations, (1) for simple internal tooling.
Dead-letter queue is not optional. Every job queue needs a DLQ. Failed agent runs must land somewhere observable — not silently disappear. Configure: max receive count (retries before DLQ), DLQ depth alarm (alert when DLQ > 0), and a replay mechanism (re-enqueue DLQ jobs after fixing root cause). A DLQ is your agent system’s catch-all error handler and audit trail for failures.
Agent state checkpointing & resume

Checkpointing persists agent state after every step so a crashed or paused agent can resume from the last checkpoint rather than restarting from scratch. Essential for HITL approval gates (pause mid-run, wait for human, resume), cost control (restart only failed steps), and long multi-step runs (survive worker restarts).

1
Define checkpoint schema
Minimum fields: run_id, step_index, graph_state (serialized), tool_results_so_far, status (running / awaiting_human / done / failed), created_at. Store in Postgres or Redis with TTL.
2
Write after every tool call
Before executing the next step, persist current state. If the worker crashes between steps, the next worker picks up from the last persisted checkpoint — the write-ahead log pattern from databases applied to agent execution.
3
HITL pause/resume via checkpoint status
When the agent reaches an approval gate: write checkpoint with status: awaiting_human, surface the proposed action to the reviewer, block until status changes to approved or rejected. Worker resumes by loading checkpoint and continuing from step_index + 1.
4
LangGraph checkpointer or Temporal
LangGraph’s built-in checkpointer (Postgres or Redis backend) handles steps 1–3 automatically. Temporal’s workflow engine handles durable execution at the infrastructure level — crashes are transparent to application code. Use Temporal for multi-agent coordination across services; LangGraph checkpointer for single-agent pause/resume.
langgraph checkpointer · temporal workflows · postgres · redis
// SWE insight · structured logging & trace correlationEvery log line from an agent run should carry trace_id, run_id, step_index, tenant_id, and model_version as structured JSON fields — not embedded in a message string. Propagate trace_id from the API gateway through every service, tool call, and retrieval query in W3C TraceContext format. Index logs in a system that supports field-based queries (CloudWatch Logs Insights, Datadog, OpenSearch). The operational question “what did this specific agent run do and why did it fail?” should be answerable from logs within 60 seconds. Without structured logging, agent debugging requires correlating traces, logs, and tool call records by hand — an expensive, error-prone process at 3am during an incident.

6.1.5Real Config Examples

Production behavior should be configured, reviewed, and released like code. These examples show the minimum shape of the control files.
config/models.yaml
default_model: claude-sonnet-4-20260501
fallback_model: gpt-4.1
budgets:
 max_input_tokens: 32000
 max_cost_usd_per_run: 0.10
routes:
 classify: claude-haiku
 rag_answer: claude-sonnet
 complex_plan: claude-opus
fallbacks:
 on_timeout: use_fallback_model
 on_rate_limit: queue_or_degrade
config/tools.yaml
tools:
 create_refund:
  owner: support_ops
  risk_tier: high
  requires_approval: true
  idempotency_key: required
  allowed_roles: [support_agent]
  audit: true
 search_docs:
  owner: knowledge_platform
  risk_tier: low
  requires_approval: false
  acl_filter: required
config/retrieval.yaml
indexes:
 help_center:
  embedding_model: text-embedding-3-large
  chunk_size: 900
  overlap: 120
  reranker: bge-reranker-large
  top_k: 12
  citation_required: true
  acl_field: allowed_principals
  freshness_days: 90
evals/release.yaml
required_suites:
 - grounded_answer_quality
 - citation_accuracy
 - prompt_injection
 - tool_selection
 - permission_boundary
thresholds:
 grounded_quality: ">= 0.88"
 citation_accuracy: ">= 0.95"
 unsafe_action_rate: "== 0"
 p95_cost_usd: "<= 0.08"

6.1.6Production Data Schemas

The minimum data contracts required for debugging, auditability, evals, rollback, and typed validation. Implement these contracts as versioned JSON Schema contracts wherever services touch user input, tool calls, retrieval metadata, structured model output, or persisted state.
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
ObjectRequired fieldsWhy it matters
conversationconversation_id, tenant_id, user_id, channel, created_at, retention_policy, statusSession continuity, deletion, support lookup.
messagemessage_id, conversation_id, role, content_hash, redacted_content, created_atTranscript reconstruction without leaking raw sensitive data.
agent_runrun_id, trace_id, prompt_version, model_version, route, latency_ms, cost_usd, outcomeObservability, eval replay, incident forensics.
retrieved_chunkchunk_id, source_id, doc_version, score, acl_decision, citation_url, freshness_atGrounding, citation accuracy, permission proof.
tool_calltool_call_id, tool_name, schema_version, args_hash, risk_tier, approval_id, idempotency_key, result_statusAction audit, replay prevention, rollback.
structured_output_schemaschema_name, schema_version, json_schema_hash, owner, backwards_compatiblePrevents brittle JSON parsing, catches schema drift, and makes LLM outputs testable.
safety_eventevent_id, policy_name, severity, blocked, detector_version, evidence_hashGovernance, red-team tracking, compliance.
feedback_eventfeedback_id, user_id, run_id, rating, reason_code, free_text_redactedContinuous improvement and eval case mining.
release_artifactrelease_id, git_sha, prompt_version, model_pin, retrieval_config, eval_report_urlReproducibility and rollback.

6.1.7Environment Strategy

Production AI behavior depends on prompts, models, tools, retrieval indexes, policies, and data. Environments must isolate all of them.
EnvironmentPurposeAllowed data/toolsPromotion requirement
Local devFast iteration, unit tests, prompt experiments.Synthetic data, mocked tools, local fixtures.Lint + unit tests before PR.
Shared devIntegration testing across services.Seed data, sandbox model keys, mock write tools.Integration tests and schema validation.
StagingProduction-like behavior without real customer impact.Scrubbed data, staging indexes, sandbox external systems.Full eval run, load test, rollback drill.
Canary prodLimited real traffic exposure.Real data with full policies and audit logging.Live gates pass for quality, latency, safety, cost.
Full prodGeneral availability.Real data, approved tools, pinned release artifacts.Ongoing monitoring, incident response, periodic eval refresh.
Environment rule: never use production secrets, raw PII, or customer write tools in local/dev. Staging must be close enough to production to catch behavioral failures, but isolated enough that accidental actions cannot affect customers.

6.1.8Deployment Pipeline (CI/CD for AI Systems)

AI systems need a CI/CD pipeline that handles three things traditional pipelines do not: prompt changes, model version changes, and behavioral regressions that cannot be detected by unit tests alone.
What goes in version control
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
ArtifactStorageReview requirement
System promptsGit (as .md or .txt files in prompts/ directory)PR review + eval run must pass before merge. Treat as code.
Model version pinsGit (in config/models.yaml or equivalent)PR review. Change triggers automatic eval run in CI.
Tool schemasGit (JSON Schema models)PR review. Schema changes are breaking changes — version them.
Guardrail rulesGit (policy files in policies/)PR review. Safety rule changes require safety eval run to pass.
Golden eval datasetGit LFS or artifact store (e.g. DVC, LangSmith datasets)Modifying expected outputs requires justification. Adding cases is always safe.
Retrieval index configGit (chunking strategy, embedding model, HNSW params)Index config changes trigger reindex pipeline in staging before prod promotion.
CI/CD pipeline stages for AI systems
STAGE 1
Lint & validate
Prompt schema validation, JSON Schema lint, policy file syntax, model pin format check.
STAGE 2
Unit tests
Tool schema validation, output format parsing, guardrail logic, state machine tests. No LLM calls.
STAGE 3
Safety evals
Red-team prompts, injection probes, content policy. Zero tolerance — any failure blocks merge.
STAGE 4
Quality evals
Golden dataset run. Quality, retrieval, tool-use metrics vs. baseline. Regression threshold blocks merge.
STAGE 5
Cost check
Cost-per-task vs. budget. Token usage vs. baseline. Flag if P95 cost exceeds threshold.
STAGE 6
Deploy & canary
Staging deploy → 48h soak → canary (5%) → gate on live metrics → full rollout.
Feature flags for AI features
Every significant AI capability should be behind a feature flag. This enables: targeted rollout to specific user segments or tenants, instant kill switch without a code deploy, A/B testing of different prompts or model versions, gradual traffic migration, and per-tenant capability control. The flag state should be configurable without a code change. Changes to flag state in production should be logged with author and timestamp. The kill switch is the most important flag — verify it works before every launch.
Prompt change management
Treat prompt changes as releases
A prompt change is a behavior change. It goes through the same PR, review, and eval gate process as a code change. "Quick prompt fix" is a deployment risk, not a shortcut. Every prompt change must have a before/after eval comparison.
Keep a prompt changelog
Document what changed, why, what behavior it was intended to fix, and what evals it passed. When a production incident traces back to a prompt change, this changelog is the forensic record that distinguishes intentional change from accidental regression.
The non-negotiable pipeline rule: no change to system prompt, model version, guardrail policy, retrieval index config, or tool schema reaches production without a passing eval run. The eval run is the single source of truth — not developer confidence, not a demo, not a staging spot-check.

6.2Runtime & Performance

Engineer latency, throughput, asynchronous execution, embedding operations, streaming UX, multi-tenancy, model versions, and cost.

TL;DR
  • Every production loop needs an iteration budget, a stop condition, and a checkpoint cadence - decided before launch, not discovered after an incident.
  • Track iteration count, per-iteration cost, and diff-size-per-iteration in the same trace used for evals and cost telemetry.
  • No-progress detection (repeated tool calls, unchanged plans) catches thrashing that a simple iteration cap will not.

6.2.1Loop Engineering (Production)

Stable
Loop engineering checklist
  • Define max iterations, max tokens, max wall-clock time, and max tool calls per loop before launch.
  • Decide and implement the behavior at each limit: hard stop, checkpoint-and-summarize, or escalate to HITL.
  • Instrument per-iteration cost and diff size; alert on no-progress patterns, not just on budget breach.
A control loop pattern (ReAct, Plan–Execute–Reflect, Ralph Loop) describes the reasoning strategy. Loop engineering is the production harness around it — the part that keeps an autonomous loop from becoming an incident.
Loop budget configuration
Budget dimensionTypical defaultWhat breaching it should do
Max iterations5–15 for tool-use agents; 1–3 for chat turns.Checkpoint current state, summarize partial progress, return to caller or escalate.
Max tokens (cumulative)Set per task tier, not globally — a research loop and a classification loop differ by orders of magnitude.Hard stop; never silently truncate context mid-reasoning.
Max wall-clock timeAligned to the UX latency budget — seconds for chat, minutes for background agents.Abort and surface a partial-result or timeout state to the user or caller.
Max tool calls per iteration1–3 for most agents; higher only for explicitly parallel fan-out steps.Block additional calls in that iteration; force a reflect/replan step.
Max identical retries1–2 retries of the same tool with the same arguments.Force a replan with a different approach, or escalate — never retry unchanged indefinitely.
Detecting thrashing vs. converging
Converging — healthy signs
Diff size between iterations shrinks. Tool calls target new information rather than repeating prior calls. Self-assessed confidence trends upward. Plan structure stabilizes rather than being rewritten each pass.
Thrashing — warning signs
Same tool called with the same or near-identical arguments across iterations. Plan oscillates between two or more approaches. Diff size does not shrink, or grows. Error messages repeat without a changed response.
Loop telemetry to capture per run
Log iteration count, per-iteration token cost, per-iteration diff size, tool call repetition rate, and final stop reason (task complete, budget exhausted, no progress, confidence threshold, escalated) to the same trace store used for evals and cost architecture. Treat the distribution of stop reasons across production traffic as a health metric: a sudden rise in "budget exhausted" or "no progress" stops signals a regression upstream — a tool schema change, a retrieval quality drop, or a model version update — well before it shows up as a quality or cost complaint.

6.2.2Inference Cache Operations

KV caching and prefix caching affect serving cost and latency but operate below application memory. Hosted providers expose only selected controls; self-hosted inference requires direct capacity, eviction, and batching decisions.

ConcernOperational meaningPrimary metricDesign implication
PrefillProcessing the input prompt before generation beginsTime to first tokenLong prompts and cache misses dominate startup latency
DecodeGenerating tokens while reusing attention KV stateInter-token latencyModel size, batching, and KV memory pressure dominate
Prefix reuseSharing stable system, tool, policy, or document prefixesPrefix-cache hit rateKeep reusable prefixes stable and place volatile content later
KV-memory pressureGPU or accelerator memory consumed by active sequencesBytes per active token and eviction rateLong contexts reduce concurrency even when compute is available
Continuous batchingScheduling requests with different sequence lengths togetherThroughput and tail latencyOptimize for workload mix, not only single-request speed
EvictionRemoving inactive or low-priority cached sequencesRecompute rateUse tenant, priority, and recency-aware policies
Quantized KV cacheLower-precision cache representationMemory reduction versus quality impactValidate task quality before adopting for high-assurance workloads
Continue toMemory Evaluation Matrix →
Production takeaway — Cache only stable, reusable work, and include model, prompt, policy, retrieval, and tenant versions in cache identity and invalidation rules.
Continue to Latency & Throughput →

6.2.3Latency & Throughput Engineering

⚡Agents fail in production when latency, rate limits, and cost compoundperformance
Set a timeout budgetAllocate budget across routing, retrieval, reranking, model calls, tool calls, verification, and streaming response.
Parallelize safe workRun independent retrieval, metadata lookup, and read-only tool calls in parallel; serialize only stateful or risky writes.
Design graceful degradationFallback from deep research to quick answer, large model to small model, rerank to keyword/vector, or tool action to draft mode.
Control queues and backpressureUse async queues, rate-limiters, dead-letter queues, warm pools, and tenant-level quotas for bursty workloads.

6.2.4Computer-Use Agents and GUI Automation

🖥️Browser and desktop agents are not normal API toolsgui agents
TopicGuidanceRisk
Prefer APIs firstUse API/MCP tools when available; use GUI automation when no stable programmatic interface exists.GUIs are brittle and hard to audit.
Visual groundingTrack screenshot, DOM node, target element, confidence, and before/after state.Misclicks, hidden modals, stale page state.
Action approvalRequire confirmation before purchases, sends, deletes, form submissions, or irreversible changes.Side effects from ambiguous UI state.
SandboxingRun browser/desktop agents in isolated profiles with constrained credentials and network access.Credential leakage and cross-site exfiltration.

6.2.5Embedding Pipeline Operations

The path from raw document to queryable vector is where most RAG failures originate. Treat the embedding pipeline as production infrastructure with SLA discipline equal to the model endpoint.
Chunking strategy decision guide
StrategyHow it worksBest forWeakness
Fixed-size (token window)Split every N tokens with M token overlap.Uniform documents (regulations, manuals). Fast and predictable.Splits mid-sentence; semantic coherence not guaranteed.
Sentence / paragraphSplit at sentence boundaries, optionally grouped into paragraphs.Prose documents (articles, reports). Preserves sentence-level meaning.Very short chunks lose context; very long paragraphs create noisy embeddings.
Semantic chunkingEmbed consecutive sentences; split where cosine similarity drops below threshold.Documents with heterogeneous sections. Better topic coherence per chunk.Slower and more expensive at ingestion. Threshold tuning required.
Hierarchical (parent-child)Small child chunks for retrieval; large parent chunks sent to model after child matches.Long documents where retrieval precision and answer context must both be high.Storage overhead; more complex ingestion pipeline.
Document-level (no chunking)Embed entire document as one vector.Short documents (FAQs, product descriptions). Simplest pipeline.Fails for long documents; single vector loses intra-document granularity.
// ML/DS insight · embedding architectureWhy query and index embeddings must come from the same model: Embedding models are trained to map text into a specific learned vector space — a bi-encoder architecture where each piece of text is embedded independently. Similarity search only works if query and document vectors live in the same space, trained on the same objective. Switching embedding models without reindexing is the same mistake as fitting a StandardScaler on training data and applying the saved scaler parameters to data with a different feature distribution — the coordinate systems no longer align, and cosine similarity between them is meaningless. Always track the embedding model version alongside the index version, and treat any model change as a mandatory full reindex.
Embedding model selection and version management
Pin the embedding model version
The embedding model used to index must be the same used to query. Changing the embedding model requires a full reindex. Track the embedding model version alongside the index version in metadata.
Embedding drift
When you upgrade the embedding model, the existing index is stale — old vectors and new query vectors are in different semantic spaces. Build a parallel reindexing pipeline before cutover, not a blocking migration.
Dimensions and normalization
Different embedding models output different vector dimensions. Normalize vectors before indexing when using cosine similarity. Verify normalization is consistent between index and query paths — a mismatch silently degrades recall.
Cost tracking
Embedding API calls accumulate fast at ingestion. Track tokens embedded per run, cost per corpus, and incremental vs. full reindex cost. Batch embedding calls — do not embed one chunk at a time.
Index freshness and reindexing operations
ScenarioStrategyMonitoring signal
Incremental update (new / updated docs)Upsert by document ID. Re-chunk and re-embed only changed documents. Maintain a change log from your source system.Ingestion lag: time between source change and index availability.
Deleted documentsSoft-delete with tombstone flag. Filter tombstoned chunks from results. Hard-delete on schedule.Stale citation rate: responses citing documents that no longer exist.
Full reindex (model upgrade)Build new index in parallel. Run A/B eval on golden retrieval set. Cut over only after new index passes eval gate.Recall@k on golden set before and after cutover.
Connector failureDetect via ingestion lag alert. Fall back to last known good index. Surface "results may be outdated" warning to user.Ingestion error rate, last successful sync timestamp.
// ML/DS insight · reranker architectureRerankers are cross-encoders — a different architecture from the bi-encoders used for retrieval. A bi-encoder embeds query and document independently, enabling fast approximate nearest-neighbor search across your entire corpus. A cross-encoder attends jointly to both the query and document in a single forward pass — far more accurate, but computationally expensive and not scalable to full-corpus search. The standard pipeline (and the same pattern used in neural NLI and sentence-pair classification) is: bi-encoder for approximate top-k retrieval → cross-encoder to rerank only those top-k candidates. If your reranker lift (the metric below) is near zero, the first-stage retrieval quality is the bottleneck, not the reranker.
Retrieval health metrics to track in production
Recall@k
% of golden queries where the correct document appears in top-k. Target above 0.85 for production RAG.
Empty retrieval rate
% of queries returning zero results. Often signals index staleness or query preprocessing failure.
Stale-source rate
% of responses citing sources older than your freshness SLA. Alert when it exceeds 5%.
Retrieval latency P95
End-to-end time from query to top-k results. Separate from model latency — helps isolate bottlenecks.
Chunk token distribution
Distribution of chunk sizes across the index. Very short or very long chunks signal chunking misconfiguration.
Reranker lift
% improvement in Recall@k after reranking vs. before. If lift is near zero, the reranker is not adding value.
TL;DR
  • Tenant isolation must be designed in from the start - retrofitting is the most painful refactor in AI production.
  • Apply ACLs at retrieval time (pre-filter), not post-filter - post-filter can still leak metadata.
  • Namespace all state keys as tenant:{id}:session:{id} and enforce at the access layer.
  • Track cost per tenant_id - runaway agents and quota abuse surface here first.

6.2.6Multi-Tenancy Patterns

Isolation is not a feature you add at GA — it is a property you design in from the start. Retrofitting tenant isolation is the most painful refactor in AI system production history.
Isolation models
ModelWhat is sharedIsolation guaranteeCostWhen to use
Shared everythingIndex, state store, compute, system prompt.Logical only (ACL filtering). Risk of leakage if filtering fails.Lowest.Internal tools, low-sensitivity data, same trust domain.
Shared compute, separate indicesCompute and harness. Separate vector index per tenant.Strong retrieval isolation. State and compute still shared.Medium.Most SaaS products. Good balance of isolation and cost.
Separate stack per tenantNothing — fully isolated deployment per tenant.Physical isolation. No cross-tenant risk at any layer.Highest.Regulated industries, high-value enterprise contracts, data sovereignty requirements.
Scroll horizontally to compare →
Isolation implementation checklist per layer
System prompt
Inject tenant-specific instructions from a configuration store, not hardcoded. Never allow tenant A's system prompt override to affect tenant B's session. Namespace tenant config by tenant ID.
Retrieval / vector store
Pre-filter all queries by tenant_id before ANN search — never post-filter (post-filter can leak metadata). Separate indices per tenant if isolation risk is high. Test cross-tenant queries in every release.
Conversation state
Namespace all state store keys as tenant:{id}:session:{id}. Never reuse session IDs across tenants. Enforce namespace at the state access layer, not just the application layer.
Tool permissions
Each tenant's agent has its own permission scope. A tool that writes to a database should be scoped to that tenant's schema or namespace. Verify tool scope on every call, not just at setup.
Observability
Tag every trace with tenant_id. Ensure trace data is not accessible cross-tenant in your observability platform. Restrict who in your org can query traces for a given tenant.
Cost accounting
Track token usage, embedding cost, and tool cost per tenant_id. Alert when a tenant's cost exceeds their quota or expected range. Catches abuse and runaway agents early.
Tenant-specific customization safely
Use a structured overlay model: a base system prompt that all tenants share (containing safety rules and core behavior), plus a tenant-specific overlay injected in a labeled section. Never allow tenant-supplied content to override safety rules. Validate the overlay against a schema before injection. Review high-risk tenant customizations manually before enabling in production.
// SWE insight · backpressure & circuit breakersWhat happens when the model API returns 429, the vector store is saturated, or a tool call hangs? Without explicit backpressure design, the failure mode is a cascade: retries amplify load, in-flight requests pile up, memory exhausts, the process dies. Apply circuit breaker pattern at every external call: (1) count failures in a rolling window; (2) trip the breaker after threshold — fail fast, no calls (open state); (3) probe with one test call after a cooldown (half-open); (4) reset on success. Libraries: tenacity (Python), opossum (Node), or AWS SDK retry config. Token bucket rate limiting at the gateway prevents a runaway agent from exhausting your model API quota for all tenants.

6.2.7Streaming & UX Latency Patterns

Latency is not just a performance metric — it is a UX design dimension. The gap between 2s and 8s feels very different depending on whether the user sees anything happening.
Streaming transport options
TransportHow it worksBest forNotes
Server-Sent Events (SSE)Server pushes a stream of events over a single HTTP connection. Client receives tokens as generated.Simple chat interfaces. Wide browser support. Works with HTTP/2.Unidirectional. Use for most chatbot / agent UIs.
WebSocketFull-duplex bidirectional channel. Both client and server can send at any time.Interactive agents where user can interrupt, clarify, or cancel mid-stream.More complex to implement and scale. Requires sticky sessions or pub/sub relay.
HTTP chunked transferSingle HTTP response with Transfer-Encoding: chunked. Response body arrives in pieces.Fallback for environments where SSE is not supported.Older browsers may buffer chunks before rendering. Test carefully.
Perceived latency strategies
Stream immediately
Start sending tokens as soon as the first token arrives. Do not buffer the full response. Even 1-2 tokens visible immediately reduces perceived wait time dramatically.
Show agentic progress
For multi-step agents, stream intermediate events: "Searching knowledge base...", "Found 3 relevant sources...", "Drafting response...". Users tolerate longer waits when they can see progress.
Optimistic UI
Show the user's message in the thread immediately before the API call returns. Do not wait for round-trip confirmation before rendering. Undo on failure.
Skeleton loading
When tool or retrieval results must complete before streaming begins, show a skeleton placeholder. The visual activity reduces abandonment significantly.
Cancellation
Allow users to stop generation mid-stream. Close the SSE connection and abort the upstream model call. Partial responses should be clearly marked as incomplete.
Latency budgeting
Allocate time budget per stage: retrieval ≤1s, first token ≤2s, total ≤8s for standard chat. Monitor each stage separately to isolate where slowdowns occur.
Tool call rendering during streaming
When an agent invokes a tool mid-generation, the stream pauses while the tool executes. Render a tool-call card in the message thread: show the tool name, parameters (redact sensitive values), and a spinner. When the result returns, collapse the card to a summary and continue the stream. For long-running tools, show elapsed time to manage expectations.

6.2.8Model Version Management

Model updates are the most common source of silent production regressions. The model changed; your prompts, evals, and expectations did not.
// ML/DS insight · model version = distribution shiftA new model version is a new data-generating process with a different output distribution — and your existing golden dataset is a sample from the old process. This is the same problem as train/test distribution mismatch: your test set may not cover the new model's specific failure modes, especially edge cases that the old model handled correctly but the new model handles differently. Before promoting a new version, audit whether your golden dataset still has good coverage of the new model's behavioral space. New failure modes often cluster in areas that weren't failure modes before — corners of the input distribution the old model happened to handle well. If your eval suite passes but production quality drops after a model upgrade, distribution shift in the golden set is the first hypothesis to investigate.
Version pinning strategy
EnvironmentPinning ruleWhen to update
ProductionAlways pin to a specific dated model version string (e.g. claude-sonnet-4-20250514). Never use an alias like claude-sonnet-latest in production.After: (1) new version available, (2) eval suite run and passing, (3) canary deployed and stable. Update is a production release, not a config change.
StagingPin to same version as production, or the candidate version being evaluated.When testing a model version upgrade. Must pass evals before production promotion.
DevelopmentMay use latest alias for experimentation. Must pin before any PR that will reach staging.Whenever exploring new model capabilities. Always resolves to a specific version before PR merge.
Model version upgrade process
1
Identify new version. Read provider changelog for behavior changes and deprecations.
2
Run full eval suite against new version. Compare quality, safety, latency, and cost vs current.
3
Fix prompt regressions. New model versions sometimes require prompt adjustments for equivalent behavior.
4
Deploy to staging. Run 48-hour soak test with real traffic shape. Monitor all production metrics.
5
Canary to production (5-10%). Gate full rollout on no metric regression over 24 hours.
Provider deprecation handling
Subscribe to deprecation announcements for every model version you use in production. Maintain a version inventory: model version string, environments using it, deprecation date, planned migration date. Start the upgrade process at least 30 days before deprecation. Never be in a position where a provider forces a model update without your eval suite having run first. Treat provider-forced upgrades as a production incident risk and treat A/B testing across model versions as a first-class operational capability.
// MLOps insight · shadow mode for agent upgradesShadow mode is the safest way to validate a new agent version before any user-facing cutover. Route live traffic to both current and candidate agent versions simultaneously. Serve only the current version's response to users; collect both responses for offline evaluation. Compare: answer quality (LLM-as-judge), tool call patterns, latency, cost, error rate. Promote only when shadow eval passes your quality threshold — zero users were exposed to the experiment. This is identical to shadow mode in ML model serving: a traffic splitter at the API gateway, a parallel execution path, and a comparison eval pipeline. Unlike A/B testing, shadow mode has zero user risk. Essential before any model version upgrade or major prompt change in a production system.
TL;DR
  • Most spend is in input tokens and embedding pipeline - optimize those before infrastructure.
  • Semantic cache threshold is a precision-recall tradeoff, not a free win - tune on a held-out query set.
  • Route by complexity: simple to cheapest model; standard RAG to mid-tier; reasoning to frontier.
  • Add cost-per-task assertions to your eval suite - cost regression is a regression even if quality passes.

6.2.9Cost Architecture

Canonical treatment: owns unit economics, model-routing budgets, caching economics, cost telemetry, and FinOps release gates.
Cost checklist
  • Set token, tool-call, retrieval, and model-routing budgets before scale testing.
  • Measure cache hit rate, fallback rate, retry amplification, and expensive-model escalation.
  • Prefer cheaper models only after evals prove task success and safety remain stable.
The prototype works. The unit economics do not. Cost architecture is the discipline of making production AI systems financially sustainable at scale.
Cost anatomy per request
TL;DR
  • Use this table to compare tradeoffs quickly; start with the default or mature option before adopting specialized alternatives.
  • Check the freshness badge or review date for market/vendor claims before making a production decision.
  • For high-risk systems, require observability, access control, evaluation coverage, and rollback before launch.
Cost componentDriverReduction lever
Input tokensSystem prompt + context + conversation history + retrieved docs + user message.Prompt compression, history summarization, tighter retrieval, context budgeting.
Output tokensResponse length. Verbose models or unbounded output windows drive this up fast.Explicit length constraints in prompt. Max output token cap per request tier.
Embedding callsEmbedding every query and every ingested document chunk.Cache query embeddings for semantically similar queries. Batch ingestion calls.
